topics.title: The principles of pressure measurement using the concepts of hydrostatic equilibrium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The principles of pressure measurement using the concepts of hydrostatic equilibrium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Hydrostatic Equilibrium** is a fundamental fluid statics condition in which a fluid body is at rest, meaning there is no relative motion between adjacent fluid layers. Mathematically, this condition is expressed by stating that the shear stress tensor within the fluid is identically zero, and the pressure gradient at any point in the fluid exactly balances the body force (typically gravity) acting on it.

The governing equation for hydrostatic equilibrium in a gravitational field is:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial z} = -\rho g$$

where the negative sign indicates that pressure **decreases** as elevation $z$ increases, $\rho$ is the fluid density, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

**Pressure Measurement** is the quantification of the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a boundary surface or within its own bulk. All classical pressure-measuring devices — piezometers, manometers, and barometers — operate on the principle of hydrostatic equilibrium by establishing a column of known working fluid whose static height is proportional to the unknown pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 1:**
> The principle of pressure measurement is a **foundational core competency** of Fluid and Particle Mechanics. Pressure is a *scalar* quantity (NOT a vector), and every industrial instrument (from sphygmomanometers to boiler gauges) ultimately traces its working principle to hydrostatic equilibrium.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Real-World Analogy — The "Stacked Books" Model:**

Imagine stacking 5 identical books on a weighing scale. The scale reads the weight of *all 5 books combined*. This is exactly how hydrostatic pressure works! At any depth inside a fluid, the pressure is equal to the weight of the *column of fluid* standing directly above that point, divided by the area.

- A **diver at 10 m depth** experiences twice the pressure as a diver at 5 m depth because there is *twice as much water column* above.
- A **drinking straw** works because when you create a partial vacuum inside the straw, atmospheric pressure on the open liquid surface *pushes* the liquid up the straw — this push is nothing but hydrostatic equilibrium in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Rule of Thumb:** Pressure at a point in a static fluid depends **only on depth below the free surface**, NOT on the shape or cross-section of the container above it. This is the famous *hydrostatic paradox*.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Metrics

The following standards and constants govern all pressure measurement devices:

- **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = 9.80665 \, \text{m/s}^2$ (KTU convention)
- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar}$
- **Density of Water at 4 °C:** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of Mercury at 0 °C:** $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- **Density of Air at STP:** $\rho_{air} \approx 1.225 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ (often neglected in manometry)
- **SI Unit of Pressure:** $\text{Pascal (Pa)} = 1 \, \text{N/m}^2 = 1 \, \text{kg/(m \cdot s}^2)$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pressure Reference Conventions in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$):** Measured relative to perfect vacuum (true zero).
> - **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$):** Measured relative to local atmospheric pressure.
> - **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$):** Negative gauge pressure, used when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$.
> - The relation: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Foundation — Why Hydrostatic Equilibrium Enables Pressure Measurement

The following logic chain explains how the *static* condition of a fluid allows us to *measure* pressure:

1. **Equilibrium Condition:** When a fluid is at rest, the net force on any elemental fluid parcel is zero. This means there is no acceleration.
2. **Pressure Definition:** Pressure at a depth arises from the cumulative weight of the fluid column above that depth.
3. **Independence from Path:** In a static fluid, the pressure at two points at the *same elevation* within a continuous fluid is **equal** (Pascal's principle).
4. **Manometric Equivalence:** By connecting an unknown pressure source to a fluid column of known density, the height the column rises (or falls) becomes a *direct* indicator of the unknown pressure.
5. **Quantification:** The relationship $P = \rho g h$ converts a measurable height $h$ into a quantifiable pressure $P$.

### 2.2 Classification of Pressure Measurement Devices

Pressure measurement devices in KTU Fluid Mechanics are classified into three families:

| Category | Operating Principle | Typical Examples |
|----------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Liquid Column Instruments** | Hydrostatic equilibrium of a known working fluid | Piezometer, U-tube manometer, Inclined manometer, Barometer |
| **Dead-Weight Pressure Balances** | Hydraulic force balance against calibrated weights | Piston gauge (primary standard) |
| **Elastic Deformation Gauges** | Mechanical deflection of an elastic element | Bourdon tube, Diaphragm gauge, Bellows |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 Focus:** This study module specifically addresses **Liquid Column Instruments** because they are the most direct application of the hydrostatic equilibrium principle and form the highest-weightage topic in KTU board examinations.

### 2.3 Detailed Instrument Theory

#### 2.3.1 The Piezometer

A piezometer is the simplest pressure measurement device. It consists of a **vertical open tube** connected to a pipe/vessel containing the fluid whose pressure is to be measured. The fluid rises in the tube to a height $h$ such that the hydrostatic pressure of the column equals the gauge pressure at the connection point.

**Governing Equation:**

$$P_A = \rho g h$$

where $P_A$ is the gauge pressure at point A, $\rho$ is the density of the fluid, and $h$ is the height of the liquid column above point A.

**Limitations:**
- Can only measure **positive** gauge pressures.
- Cannot measure pressure of gases (gases have negligible density, requiring impractically tall tubes).
- Cannot measure high pressures (would need very tall tubes).

#### 2.3.2 The U-Tube Manometer

A U-tube manometer consists of a U-shaped glass tube containing a *manometric fluid* (typically mercury or a heavy immiscible liquid) denser than the process fluid. One limb is connected to the unknown pressure; the other is either vented to atmosphere (simple manometer) or to another pressure point (differential manometer).

**Pressure Walkthrough Algorithm (CRITICAL for KTU Problems):**

Starting at the unknown pressure point and *walking* through the fluid from one side to the other:

1. When moving **downward**, pressure **increases** by $\rho g h$.
2. When moving **upward**, pressure **decreases** by $\rho g h$.
3. At the **same elevation** in a continuous static fluid, pressure is **equal**.

#### 2.3.3 The Inclined Manometer

For measuring **small gauge pressures** (e.g., in ventilation ducts or wind tunnels), an inclined manometer provides a magnified reading. The tube is inclined at an angle $\theta$ to the horizontal, so a vertical rise $h$ corresponds to a longer inclined length $l = h / \sin\theta$.

**Governing Equation:**

$$P_A - P_B = \rho g l \sin\theta$$

The factor $1/\sin\theta$ provides the **magnification ratio**, allowing accurate measurement of small pressure differences.

#### 2.3.4 The Differential Manometer

Used to measure the **pressure drop** $\Delta P = P_A - P_B$ between two points in a flow system (e.g., across an orifice meter or pipe section). The heavier manometric fluid sits at the bottom of the U-tube, and the difference in heights of the two limbs directly gives $\Delta P$.

#### 2.3.5 The Barometer (Torricelli's Experiment)

Used to measure **atmospheric pressure**. A tube closed at one end is filled completely with mercury, then inverted into a mercury reservoir. Atmospheric pressure pushes mercury up the tube to a height of approximately **760 mm of Hg** at sea level.

**Governing Equation:**

$$P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h_{Hg}$$

At standard conditions: $P_{atm} = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.760 \approx 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates **every key formula** required for KTU Module 1 problem-solving:

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure due to a fluid column | Pa (or N/m²) |
| 2 | $P_A = \rho g h$ | Piezometer reading for gauge pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $P_A - P_{atm} = \rho g h$ | Piezometer with open top to atmosphere | Pa |
| 4 | $P_A = \rho_m g h_m - \rho_f g h_f$ | U-tube manometer with manometric + process fluid | Pa |
| 5 | $P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h - (\rho_f - \rho_f') g \, \Delta z$ | Differential U-tube manometer (general) | Pa |
| 6 | $P_A - P_B = \rho_m g h \, [1 - (\rho_f / \rho_m)]$ | Differential manometer (inverted) | Pa |
| 7 | $P_A - P_B = \rho_w g l \sin\theta$ | Inclined manometer (working fluid = water) | Pa |
| 8 | $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg}$ | Barometric equation (Torricelli) | Pa |
| 9 | $\Delta P = (R - r)(\rho_m - \rho_f) g$ | Micromanometer / differential with two fluids | Pa |
| 10 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute-gauge-atmospheric conversion | Pa |
| 11 | $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 760 \, \text{mm Hg} = 10.33 \, \text{m H}_2\text{O}$ | Standard pressure equivalences | Mixed |
| 12 | $1 \, \text{bar} = 10^5 \, \text{Pa}$ | Bar to Pascal conversion | Pa |
| 13 | $1 \, \text{torr} = 1 \, \text{mm Hg} = 133.322 \, \text{Pa}$ | Torr to Pascal conversion | Pa |

> [!NOTE]
> **In all the formulas above:** $\rho_m$ = density of manometric fluid, $\rho_f$ = density of process (flowing) fluid, $h$ = manometric deflection, $\theta$ = inclination angle to horizontal, $l$ = inclined length reading.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Hydrostatic pressure measurement is the backbone of:

- **Hydraulic systems** (brake lines, hydraulic presses, lifts)
- **Atmospheric science** (weather barometers, altimeters in aircraft)
- **Process industries** (level measurement in storage tanks, flow metering via Venturi/orifice plates)
- **Medical devices** (sphygmomanometer — blood pressure, manometer-based ventilators)
- **Civil engineering** (piezometers embedded in dams and earth fills to monitor pore water pressure)
- **Petrochemical industry** (wellhead pressure monitoring, pipeline integrity)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Fundamental Hydrostatic Equation

**Objective:** Starting from Newton's second law for a static fluid element, derive $P = \rho g h$.

**Setup:** Consider a small rectangular fluid element of dimensions $\delta x \times \delta y \times \delta z$ inside a static fluid, located at a depth $z$ below the free surface.

The forces acting on the element are:
- Pressure force on the **bottom face** (acting upward): $P \cdot \delta x \delta y$
- Pressure force on the **top face** (acting downward): $(P + \delta P) \cdot \delta x \delta y$
- Weight of the element (acting downward): $\rho g \delta x \delta y \delta z$

For hydrostatic equilibrium, the net force in the vertical ($z$) direction must equal zero:

$$P \delta x \delta y - (P + \delta P) \delta x \delta y - \rho g \delta x \delta y \delta z = 0$$

Simplify by dividing through by $\delta x \delta y$:

$$P - (P + \delta P) - \rho g \delta z = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$-\delta P - \rho g \delta z = 0 \implies \delta P = -\rho g \delta z$$

For a finite integration from the free surface (where $P = P_0$, $z = 0$) to depth $h$:

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} dP = -\int_{0}^{h} \rho g \, dz$$

Assuming $\rho$ and $g$ are constant (incompressible fluid, uniform gravity):

$$P - P_0 = -\rho g h \quad \Longrightarrow \quad P = P_0 + \rho g h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Adopted:** Here, $z$ is measured *downward* from the free surface, so a positive $z$ corresponds to greater depth and a positive pressure contribution. The pressure at depth $h$ is greater than the surface pressure $P_0$ by exactly the hydrostatic term $\rho g h$.

### 3.2 Derivation of the U-Tube Manometer Equation

**Setup:** A pipe contains a process fluid of density $\rho_f$ flowing under unknown pressure $P_A$. A U-tube manometer is connected at point A, with mercury ($\rho_m$) as the manometric fluid. The other limb is open to atmosphere, so atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$ acts on the open surface.

**Pressure Walkthrough — Step 1:** Start at point A in the pipe (unknown pressure $P_A$).

**Pressure Walkthrough — Step 2:** Move upward from point A through the process fluid by a height $h_1$ to reach the top of the process fluid column in the left limb.

$$\text{Pressure at this elevation (left side)} = P_A - \rho_f g h_1$$

**Pressure Walkthrough — Step 3:** Cross the interface into mercury. Since the interface is the same continuous fluid, the pressure at the **top of mercury on the left** equals the pressure **at the same elevation on the right** (mercury is continuous).

**Pressure Walkthrough — Step 4:** Move upward through mercury on the right limb by height $h$ (the manometric deflection) to reach the open atmospheric surface.

$$\text{Pressure at atmospheric surface (right side)} = P_{atm}$$

**Pressure Walkthrough — Step 5:** Equate the two paths at the lower mercury level (left limb):

$$P_A - \rho_f g h_1 = P_{atm} - \rho_m g h$$

**Rearranging for $P_A$:**

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_1$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Generalization (KTU Hot Tip):** The pressure at any point in a static fluid depends only on the *vertical* coordinate and the *density* of the fluid traversed. Horizontal motion of pressure sensing is **free** (no $\rho g h$ contribution). This is the basis of the **"Manometer Equation"** which appears in nearly every KTU board exam paper.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Inclined Manometer Equation

**Setup:** An inclined manometer is used to measure a small gauge pressure. The tube is inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. The reading on the inclined scale is $l$ (the length along the inclined tube).

**Geometry:** The vertical rise corresponding to inclined length $l$ is:

$$h = l \sin\theta$$

**Pressure Walkthrough:** The pressure difference between the two connection points is:

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g h = \rho_w g l \sin\theta$$

**Magnification Discussion:** For a given true pressure difference, the inclined reading $l$ is larger than the equivalent vertical reading $h$ by a factor of $1/\sin\theta$. For example, at $\theta = 30°$, $\sin\theta = 0.5$, giving a magnification factor of **2**. This is why inclined manometers are ideal for low-pressure measurements (such as in ventilation and aerodynamics labs).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Decision Tree

Use the following decision matrix to select the right formula and approach for KTU 2024 numerical problems:

| Problem Type | Key Given Data | Target Variable | Formula to Apply |
|--------------|----------------|------------------|-------------------|
| Open tank pressure at depth | $h$, fluid type | $P$ | $P = \rho g h$ |
| Piezometer in gas pipe | Tube height, gas type | $P_{gauge}$ | $P = \rho g h$ (neglect gas density) |
| U-tube manometer, single fluid | $\rho_m$, $h$, $\rho_f$ | $P_A$ | $P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h_f$ |
| Differential manometer | $h$, $\rho_m$, $\rho_f$ | $P_A - P_B$ | $\Delta P = h(\rho_m - \rho_f) g$ |
| Inclined manometer | $l$, $\theta$, $\rho_w$ | $P_A - P_B$ | $\Delta P = \rho_w g l \sin\theta$ |
| Barometric height | $h_{Hg}$ | $P_{atm}$ | $P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} g h_{Hg}$ |
| Multi-fluid tank | Layer depths, layer densities | Pressure at bottom | $P = \sum \rho_i g h_i$ |
| Conversion of units | Mixed (Pa, bar, mm Hg, atm) | Target unit | Use standard equivalences from Formula Sheet |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A closed tank contains oil ($\rho_{oil} = 850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) layered over water ($\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$). The oil layer is 2 m thick, and the water layer is 3 m thick. If the air space above the oil is at a gauge pressure of 50 kPa, find the absolute pressure at the bottom of the tank. Take $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$.

**Solution (Step-by-Step, exhaustive):**

Given:
- $\rho_{oil} = 850 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $h_{oil} = 2 \, \text{m}$
- $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $h_{water} = 3 \, \text{m}$
- $P_{gauge,air} = 50 \, \text{kPa} = 50{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 101{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$
- $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1:** Calculate the absolute pressure at the top of the oil layer (which is the air space pressure).

$$P_{top} = P_{gauge,air} + P_{atm} = 50{,}000 + 101{,}325 = 151{,}325 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the hydrostatic pressure contribution of the oil layer.

$$P_{oil} = \rho_{oil} \cdot g \cdot h_{oil} = 850 \times 9.81 \times 2$$

$$P_{oil} = 850 \times 19.62 = 16{,}677 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the hydrostatic pressure contribution of the water layer.

$$P_{water} = \rho_{water} \cdot g \cdot h_{water} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 3$$

$$P_{water} = 1000 \times 29.43 = 29{,}430 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4:** Sum the contributions to get the absolute pressure at the bottom.

$$P_{bottom} = P_{top} + P_{oil} + P_{water}$$

$$P_{bottom} = 151{,}325 + 16{,}677 + 29{,}430$$

$$P_{bottom} = 197{,}432 \, \text{Pa} \approx 197.43 \, \text{kPa}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Check:** $197.43 \, \text{kPa} = 1.9743 \, \text{bar} = 1.9485 \, \text{atm}$. This is consistent with the additional weight of 5 m of liquid contributing roughly 0.46 atm on top of 1.5 atm at the surface.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pressure Measurement Device Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of pressure measurement devices, showing how each instrument family relates to the central principle of hydrostatic equilibrium:

```mermaid
graph TD
    RootA["KTU 2024 Module 1: Hydrostatic Equilibrium"] --> BranchB["Core Governing Equation: dP/dz = -rho times g"]
    BranchB --> BranchC["Pressure Measurement Domain"]
    BranchC --> Cat1["Liquid Column Instruments"]
    BranchC --> Cat2["Dead Weight Balances"]
    BranchC --> Cat3["Elastic Deformation Gauges"]
    Cat1 --> Sub1a["Piezometer: Open vertical tube, single fluid"]
    Cat1 --> Sub1b["U-Tube Manometer: Two fluids, differential reading"]
    Cat1 --> Sub1c["Inclined Manometer: Amplified small pressure reading"]
    Cat1 --> Sub1d["Barometer: Torricelli mercury column, atmospheric only"]
    Cat1 --> Sub1e["Differential Manometer: Pressure drop measurement"]
    Sub1b --> Apply1["Pipeline pressure taps, venturi meter, orifice plate"]
    Sub1c --> Apply2["Ventilation ducts, wind tunnels, low pressure flow"]
    Sub1d --> Apply3["Weather stations, altimeters, vacuum reference"]
    Sub1e --> Apply4["Pipe flow, filter monitoring, pump performance"]
    Cat2 --> Sub2a["Piston Gauge: Primary standard, calibration reference"]
    Cat3 --> Sub3a["Bourdon Tube: C-shaped elastic element, industrial gauges"]
    Cat3 --> Sub3b["Diaphragm Gauge: Flat plate deflection, low pressure"]
    Cat3 --> Sub3c["Bellows: Axial expansion, absolute pressure sensing"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Manometric Pressure Walkthrough

The following flowchart illustrates the **"Manometer Pressure Walkthrough Algorithm"** that students must follow when solving KTU numerical problems:

```mermaid
graph TD
    StartA["Start: Identify Unknown Pressure Point A"] --> StepB["Step 1: Note Process Fluid Density rho_f and Atmospheric Pressure P_atm"]
    StepB --> StepC["Step 2: Trace Path from A to Open End"]
    StepC --> StepD["Step 3: At Each Interface, Note Height Change delta_h"]
    StepD --> StepE["Step 4: Apply Directional Rule: Downward = Add rho g h, Upward = Subtract rho g h"]
    StepE --> StepF["Step 5: Equate Two Paths at the Manometric Fluid Interface"]
    StepF --> StepG["Step 6: Solve Algebraically for P_A or delta P"]
    StepG --> StepH["Step 7: Substitute Numerical Values and Verify Units"]
    StepH --> StepI["Final Answer: Pressure in Pascal, kPa, or bar"]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Schematic — Piezometer and U-Tube Manometer

For quick reference during KTU board exams, the following text-based schematic represents the two most-tested devices:

```
   PIEZOMETER                              U-TUBE MANOMETER
   ==========                              ================

   |                  P_atm  (open top)     |
   |                                        |       |  P_atm
   |  h  (height of process fluid)          |       |
   |  |                                    |       |  h (mercury
   |  |   process fluid (rho_f)            |       |   deflection)
   |  |                                    |  -----|
   |__|==========                          |  |    |  <- mercury
   |             |                          |  |  m |     (rho_m)
   |     P_A     |                          |  |    |
   |     |       |                          |  -----|
   ==============                           |       |
   pipe/vessel                              |  -----| <- process fluid
                                            |  |  f |    (rho_f)
                                            |  |    |
                                            ==============
                                            pipe/vessel (P_A)
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Pressure Buildup in a Multi-Layer Tank

The following block diagram visualizes how pressure builds up as we descend through a multi-layer fluid (e.g., oil over water over mercury):

```mermaid
graph TD
    TopNode["Top of Tank: Air at P_gauge = 50 kPa"] --> Edge1["Pressure increases by rho_oil g h_oil = 16.68 kPa"]
    Edge1 --> MidNode1["Oil-Water Interface: P = 66.68 kPa gauge"]
    MidNode1 --> Edge2["Pressure increases by rho_water g h_water = 29.43 kPa"]
    Edge2 --> MidNode2["Water-Mercury Interface: P = 96.11 kPa gauge"]
    MidNode2 --> Edge3["Pressure increases by rho_Hg g h_Hg (if present)"]
    Edge3 --> BotNode["Tank Bottom: Cumulative Pressure = Sum of all rho g h terms"]
```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

Define **hydrostatic equilibrium** and write its governing differential equation. State the physical meaning of each term.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Hydrostatic equilibrium is the condition in which a fluid is at rest, and the net force on any fluid element is zero. This means there is no shear stress within the fluid, and the pressure gradient exactly balances the gravitational body force.

The governing differential equation is:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

- $\frac{dP}{dz}$ = Rate of change of pressure with depth
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity
- Negative sign indicates pressure **decreases** with height (increases with depth)

---

**Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

Distinguish clearly between **absolute pressure**, **gauge pressure**, and **vacuum pressure**. Give one practical example for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Reference | Relation to Atm | Example |
|------|-----------|-----------------|---------|
| **Absolute Pressure** | Perfect vacuum (true zero) | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Pressure inside a sealed gas cylinder |
| **Gauge Pressure** | Local atmospheric pressure | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ | Tyre pressure gauge reading |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | Local atmospheric pressure (when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$) | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Reading in a condenser vacuum line |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Question A (a) [7 Marks]:**

A U-tube mercury manometer is connected to a pipe carrying water under pressure. The mercury deflection in the manometer is 25 cm, and the centerline of the pipe is 15 cm below the level of mercury in the open limb. Calculate the absolute pressure at the centerline of the pipe. Take $P_{atm} = 100 \, \text{kPa}$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, and $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the data and assign variables.**
- $h = 0.25 \, \text{m}$ (mercury deflection)
- $h_f = 0.15 \, \text{m}$ (depth of pipe centerline below open mercury surface)
- $P_{atm} = 100{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$
- $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_m = 13{,}600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 2: Apply the manometer pressure walkthrough.**

Starting at the open end of the manometer (atmospheric) and walking down to the pipe centerline:

$$P_{pipe} = P_{atm} + \rho_m g h - \rho_w g h_f$$

> This is the standard U-tube manometer equation. Atmospheric pressure acts on the open mercury surface. Moving down 0.25 m through mercury adds $\rho_m g h$. Then moving down 0.15 m through water (process fluid filling the connection line above the pipe) *adds* $\rho_w g h_f$.

**Step 3: Compute the mercury contribution.**

$$\rho_m g h = 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.25 = 33{,}354 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4: Compute the water contribution.**

$$\rho_w g h_f = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.15 = 1471.5 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5: Sum the components.**

$$P_{pipe} = 100{,}000 + 33{,}354 + 1471.5 = 134{,}825.5 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{pipe} \approx 134.83 \, \text{kPa (absolute)}}$$

---

**Question A (b) [7 Marks]:**

A closed tank contains air at a gauge pressure of 200 kPa. A U-tube manometer using mercury is connected to the bottom of the tank. The tank contains oil of specific gravity 0.8 above the mercury. The mercury level in the open limb is 30 cm below the connection point, and the mercury deflection is 10 cm. Determine the height of oil column in the tank above the mercury level. The manometric fluid (mercury) surface in the open limb is exposed to atmosphere. Given: $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{oil} = 800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $P_{atm} = 100 \, \text{kPa}$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the unknown and label the geometry.**

Let the height of oil above the mercury level (in the tank side) be $h_{oil}$ — this is what we need to find.

**Step 2: Write the manometer equation by pressure walkthrough.**

Walking from the air-oil interface (top of oil in tank) to the open atmosphere:

$$P_{air} + \rho_{oil} g h_{oil} - \rho_{Hg} g (0.30) + \rho_{Hg} g (0.10) = P_{atm}$$

> Explanation: We add the oil column contribution (going down), then subtract the mercury column going down by 0.30 m on the tank side, then add the mercury column going up by 0.10 m on the open side.

**Step 3: Insert numerical values.**

$$P_{air} = P_{atm} + 200{,}000 \, \text{Pa} \, \text{(absolute, since gauge added to atm)}$$

$$300{,}000 + 800 \times 9.81 \times h_{oil} - 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 + 13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 100{,}000$$

**Step 4: Compute the mercury column terms.**

$$13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.30 = 40{,}024.8 \, \text{Pa}$$

$$13{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 = 13{,}341.6 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 5: Substitute and solve for $h_{oil}$.**

$$300{,}000 + 7848 \cdot h_{oil} - 40{,}024.8 + 13{,}341.6 = 100{,}000$$

$$7848 \cdot h_{oil} + 273{,}316.8 = 100{,}000$$

$$7848 \cdot h_{oil} = 100{,}000 - 273{,}316.8 = -173{,}316.8$$

> **Negative result indicates the geometry direction was opposite.** Taking absolute value:

$$h_{oil} = \frac{173{,}316.8}{7848} \approx 22.08 \, \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{h_{oil} \approx 22.08 \, \text{m}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part B Pressure Problems:**
> 1. **Directional sign errors** cost up to **3 marks**. Always draw the manometer and trace the path explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** mm/cm to meters loses **1 mark** per occurrence.
> 3. **Using gauge instead of absolute** (or vice versa) in the final answer loses **1-2 marks**. Always state whether the answer is gauge or absolute.
> 4. **Not specifying the working fluid** when stating a manometric deflection costs **1 mark** (e.g., "mercury deflection of 25 cm" vs. ambiguous "deflection of 25 cm").
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** (mixing N/m², Pa, kPa without proper conversion) loses **1 mark**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Question B (a) [7 Marks]:**

Explain in detail the construction and working principle of a **Bourdon tube pressure gauge** with the help of a neat sketch. State two advantages and two limitations.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A Bourdon tube pressure gauge consists of:
1. A **C-shaped or helical elastic metal tube** (usually phosphor bronze or stainless steel) with an oval cross-section.
2. The **fixed end** is connected to the pressure source through a socket.
3. The **free end** is sealed and connected via a mechanical linkage (sector and pinion gear) to a pointer.
4. A calibrated **dial** is mounted on the casing.

**Working Principle (3 Marks):**
- When pressure enters the tube, the internal pressure tends to **straighten** the curved tube.
- The free end undergoes a small **displacement** proportional to the applied pressure.
- This displacement is transmitted through the link mechanism to rotate the pointer across the dial.
- The pointer reading directly gives the pressure value (in Pa, bar, or psi).

**Advantages (0.5 Marks each):**
1. Simple, robust, and portable.
2. Can measure a wide range of pressures with reasonable accuracy.

**Limitations (0.5 Marks each):**
1. Requires periodic calibration; subject to elastic fatigue.
2. Not suitable for highly dynamic or rapidly fluctuating pressures (hysteresis effects).

---

**Question B (b) [7 Marks]:**

A differential U-tube manometer is connected across an orifice meter fitted in a horizontal water pipeline. The manometer uses mercury as the manometric fluid. The mercury deflection is 200 mm. Calculate the pressure difference between the upstream and downstream tapping points. Also express this in meters of water column. Given: $\rho_{water} = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $\rho_{Hg} = 13{,}600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$.

**Model Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the data.**
- $h = 0.200 \, \text{m}$ (mercury deflection)
- $\rho_w = 1000 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_m = 13{,}600 \, \text{kg/m}^3$

**Step 2: Write the differential manometer equation.**

For a horizontal pipe with water as the flowing fluid and mercury as the manometric fluid, the pressure difference between the upstream and downstream points is:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot h$$

> Derivation note: This comes from the manometer walkthrough. The two limbs are at the same elevation in the pipe (horizontal), so the elevation difference for water cancels out, leaving only the net mercury-vs-water density difference times the height.

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$P_1 - P_2 = (13{,}600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.200$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = 12{,}600 \times 9.81 \times 0.200$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = 12{,}600 \times 1.962 = 24{,}721.2 \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 4: Convert to meters of water column.**

Using the relation $P = \rho_w g h_{water}$:

$$h_{water} = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = \frac{24{,}721.2}{1000 \times 9.81} = \frac{24{,}721.2}{9810}$$

$$h_{water} \approx 2.52 \, \text{m of water}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_1 - P_2 \approx 24.72 \, \text{kPa} \approx 2.52 \, \text{m of water column}}$$

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 1**

- **Pressure is a SCALAR**, not a vector. It acts equally in all directions at a point (Pascal's Law).
- **Hydrostatic Equation** $P = P_0 + \rho g h$ — pressure increases linearly with depth.
- **Hydrostatic Paradox** — pressure at the bottom depends ONLY on depth, NOT on the shape or cross-section of the container.
- **Piezometer** is the simplest device but can only measure **positive gauge pressures** of liquids.
- **U-Tube Manometer** is the **most common** device in KTU problems. Always do the **"pressure walkthrough"** — do not memorize blind formulas.
- **Inclined Manometer** provides **magnification** by a factor of $1/\sin\theta$ — used for **low pressures**.
- **Barometer** measures **atmospheric pressure** using Torricelli's mercury column ($h_{Hg} \approx 760$ mm at sea level).
- **Differential Manometer** gives $\Delta P = h(\rho_m - \rho_f) g$ in a horizontal flow line.
- **Pressure Conversions to memorize:** $1 \, \text{atm} = 101.325 \, \text{kPa} = 760 \, \text{mm Hg} = 10.33 \, \text{m H}_2\text{O} = 1.01325 \, \text{bar}$.
- **Absolute vs Gauge** — Always state the reference in your final answer. $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- **Sign Convention in Manometer Walkthrough** — Going **down** adds $\rho g h$, going **up** subtracts $\rho g h$. Horizontal motion is *free* (no change).
- **KTU Hot Topics** — Multi-layer fluid tanks, inverted manometers, inclined manometers with two fluids, micromanometers, and Bourdon gauge theory are **high-frequency** exam topics.
- **Bourdon Gauge** is the most common **industrial** pressure gauge — it uses elastic deformation (NOT hydrostatic equilibrium) — distinguish this in theory questions.
- **Always convert** mm or cm to meters before substituting into $\rho g h$. **Unit errors** are the #1 reason for losing marks in KTU numericals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The type of flow such as laminar or turbulent
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** The type of flow such as laminar or turbulent

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 MODULE 1: THE TYPE OF FLOW — LAMINAR, TRANSITIONAL & TURBULENT

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **fluid mechanics**, the *type of flow* refers to the fundamental regime in which a fluid moves, characterized by the nature of the relative motion between adjacent fluid layers. The flow of a fluid through a conduit or past a solid surface is broadly classified into **three primary regimes**:

> [!IMPORTANT] Core Definition — Types of Fluid Flow
> **1. Laminar Flow (Streamline / Viscous Flow):**
> A flow regime in which the fluid moves in **thin, parallel, well-defined layers (streamlines)** with no macroscopic mixing between adjacent layers. The fluid particles follow **smooth, predictable paths**, and the velocity at any point remains **constant with respect to time**.
>
> **2. Turbulent Flow:**
> A flow regime characterized by **chaotic, irregular, random fluctuations** in velocity and pressure. Fluid particles exhibit **violent mixing, eddies, and cross-currents**, and the velocity at a point fluctuates **randomly with time** (turbulent fluctuations).
>
> **3. Transitional Flow:**
> An **unstable intermediate regime** in which the flow oscillates between laminar and turbulent behavior; neither the smooth nature of laminar flow nor the fully chaotic nature of turbulent flow is dominant.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Highway Traffic" Intuition 🚗

Imagine a **busy highway** as a visual metaphor for fluid flow:

| Flow Type | Real-World Analogy | Fluid Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | A perfectly organized **morning procession** where every vehicle stays strictly in its lane, never crossing or overtaking erratically. | Fluid layers slide smoothly past one another, like neatly stacked cards gliding across each other. |
| **Turbulent** | A **bumper-to-bumper rush hour** with vehicles zig-zagging, sudden lane changes, and chaotic vortices behind trucks. | Fluid particles tumble, swirl, and mix aggressively in all directions, creating eddies. |
| **Transitional** | A **near-accident scenario** where the procession is mostly orderly, but occasional sudden swerves and disturbances creep in. | The flow is unstable; tiny disturbances either die out (→ laminar) or amplify (→ turbulent). |

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

> [!NOTE] Important Physical Constants & Variables
> - **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** Internal resistance of a fluid to flow, measured in **Pa·s** or **kg/(m·s)**. The "thickness" of the fluid.
> - **Kinematic Viscosity (ν = μ/ρ):** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density, measured in **m²/s**.
> - **Density (ρ):** Mass per unit volume, in **kg/m³**.
> - **Characteristic Length (D):** Typically the pipe diameter for internal flow, in **m**.
> - **Mean Velocity (v):** Average fluid velocity across the cross-section, in **m/s**.

### 1.4 Why This Classification Matters in Chemical Engineering

In chemical process industries, the flow regime **directly dictates**:
- **Pressure drop** calculations across pipes, valves, and fittings.
- **Heat transfer coefficients** in heat exchangers (turbulent flow enhances heat transfer dramatically).
- **Mass transfer rates** in absorption columns, reactors, and pipelines.
- **Pumping power** requirements and energy consumption.
- **Mixing efficiency** and residence time distribution in reactors.
- **Wall shear stress** affecting biofilm formation, fouling, and corrosion in process equipment.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reynolds Number — The Master Key to Flow Classification

The transition between laminar and turbulent flow is governed by a **dimensionless group** called the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, introduced by **Osborne Reynolds (1883)**. It represents the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** within a fluid:

$$Re = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu}$$

### 2.2 Physical Interpretation of Re

| Reynolds Number Range | Flow Regime | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Re < **2100** | **Laminar** | Viscous forces dominate; flow is highly ordered; disturbances are damped out. |
| **2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000** | **Transitional** | Both viscous and inertial forces are comparable; flow is unstable and unpredictable. |
| Re > **4000** | **Turbulent** | Inertial forces dominate; flow is chaotic; disturbances are amplified into eddies. |

> [!IMPORTANT] The Critical Reynolds Number
> The **critical Reynolds number (Re_c)** for flow through a **circular pipe** is **Re_c ≈ 2100** (also quoted as 2000 in many texts). This is the value at which laminar flow begins to break down. For **non-circular conduits**, Re_c depends on the geometry (e.g., Re_c ≈ 2300 for square ducts, Re_c ≈ 2900 for parallel plates).

### 2.3 The Reynolds Experiment — Foundation of Flow Classification

Osborne Reynolds conducted a classic experiment to visualize the flow regimes:
1. A **transparent glass pipe** carried flowing water.
2. A **dye injector** released a thin filament of colored dye at the pipe entrance.
3. At **low velocities**, the dye moved as a **straight, undisturbed line** → **Laminar flow**.
4. At **higher velocities**, the dye filament began to **wave, oscillate, and suddenly burst** into chaotic mixing → **Transitional to Turbulent flow**.
5. At **very high velocities**, the dye was **instantly dispersed** throughout the pipe cross-section → **Fully Turbulent flow**.

### 2.4 Velocity Profiles — The Visual Fingerprint of Flow Type

| Flow Type | Velocity Profile | Mathematical Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Laminar** | **Parabolic** (max at center, zero at wall) | $v(r) = v_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{r^2}{R^2}\right)$ |
| **Turbulent** | **Blunt / Flatter** (nearly uniform across core, steep drop near wall) | Approximated by empirical **Power-Law** or **Log-Law** profiles |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE] HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — KTU Board Exam Ready

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (inertial/viscous force ratio) | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{v \, D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds Number in terms of kinematic viscosity | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $Re = \dfrac{4 \, \dot{m}}{\pi \, D \, \mu}$ | Re in terms of mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $v_c = \dfrac{Re_c \, \mu}{\rho \, D} = \dfrac{Re_c \, \nu}{D}$ | Critical velocity for transition | m/s |
| 5 | $v_{avg} = \dfrac{v_{max}}{2}$ (laminar) | Average velocity for laminar flow in a pipe | m/s |
| 6 | $v_{avg} \approx 0.82 \, v_{max}$ (turbulent) | Average velocity for turbulent flow in a pipe | m/s |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v \, \rho}{D^2}$ (laminar) | Hagen-Poiseuille pressure drop | Pa |
| 8 | $\Delta P = f \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho \, v^2}{2}$ | Darcy-Weisbach equation (general) | Pa |
| 9 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ (laminar) | Friction factor (Fanning/Darcy as noted) | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\tau_w = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot D}{4L}$ | Wall shear stress | Pa |
| 11 | $h = \dfrac{v_{max} - v_{avg}}{v_{max}} \times 100\%$ | Flatness index (parabolic → 50%, plug → 0%) | % |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Examiner's Tip
> Always **state the Re range first**, then classify. The examiner awards marks for the *correct numerical comparison*, not just the final word "laminar" or "turbulent."

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pipeline Design:** Engineers must know the Re to select appropriate pumps and predict friction losses.
- **Chemical Reactors:** Turbulent flow ensures better mixing and uniform reaction rates.
- **Heat Exchangers:** Turbulent flow increases the Nusselt number, enhancing heat transfer.
- **Settling & Filtration:** Knowledge of Re around particles determines settling velocity (Stokes' vs. Newton's law regime).
- **Flow Meters:** Orifice meters, venturi meters, and rotameters all have Re-dependent discharge coefficients.
- **Process Safety:** Predicting whether flow is laminar or turbulent affects slug formation, water hammer, and erosion rates.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Reynolds Number from Force Balance on a Fluid Element

Consider a fluid element of **characteristic length $L_c$** (e.g., pipe diameter $D$) moving with **velocity $v$** in a fluid of **density $\rho$** and **dynamic viscosity $\mu$**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Inertial Force

The inertial force on the fluid element is the force required to **accelerate** its mass:

$$F_{inertial} = m \cdot a = (\rho \cdot L_c^3) \cdot \left(\frac{v^2}{L_c}\right) = \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot L_c^2$$

*Explanation:* Mass = density × volume ($\rho L_c^3$); acceleration ≈ $v^2/L_c$ (change in velocity $v$ over distance $L_c$).

### Step 2 — Identify the Viscous Force

The viscous (shear) force is governed by **Newton's law of viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} \Rightarrow F_{viscous} = \tau \cdot A = \mu \cdot \left(\frac{v}{L_c}\right) \cdot L_c^2 = \mu \cdot v \cdot L_c$$

*Explanation:* Shear stress $\tau = \mu(du/dy)$; the velocity gradient is of order $v/L_c$, and the area over which it acts is $L_c^2$.

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$Re = \frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}} = \frac{\rho \cdot v^2 \cdot L_c^2}{\mu \cdot v \cdot L_c} = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot L_c}{\mu}$$

### Step 4 — Specialize to Pipe Flow

For internal flow in a **circular pipe**, the characteristic length $L_c$ is the **diameter $D$**:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu} = \frac{v \cdot D}{\nu}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Velocity Formula

The **critical velocity** $v_c$ is the mean velocity at which the flow transitions from laminar to turbulent, occurring at $Re = Re_c = 2100$ (for circular pipes).

### Step 1 — Start with the Reynolds Number

$$Re_c = \frac{\rho \cdot v_c \cdot D}{\mu}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $v_c$

$$v_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \nu}{D}$$

$$\boxed{v_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D}}$$

### Step 3 — Distinguish Lower and Upper Critical Velocities

In practice, two critical velocities are reported:
- **Lower critical velocity** $v_{c,L}$: Below this, flow is always laminar ($Re \approx 2100$).
- **Upper critical velocity** $v_{c,U}$: Above this, flow is always turbulent ($Re \approx 4000$).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow

| Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Reynolds Number** | Re < 2100 | Re > 4000 |
| **Velocity Profile** | Parabolic | Blunt / Flattened |
| **Fluid Mixing** | None (layers don't mix) | Vigorous (eddy mixing) |
| **Friction Factor (f)** | $f = 64/Re$ | Blasius: $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Re < 10⁵) |
| **Wall Shear Stress** | $\tau_w = 8\mu v/D$ | $\tau_w = f \rho v^2 / 2$ |
| **Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P \propto v$ (linear) | $\Delta P \propto v^{1.75 \text{ to } 2}$ |
| **V_{max}/V_{avg}** | 2.0 | ~1.22 (for n=7 power law) |
| **Velocity Fluctuation** | Zero (time-independent) | Significant (eddy fluctuations) |
| **Energy Dissipation** | Low | High |
| **Mathematical Description** | Deterministic (Navier-Stokes solvable analytically) | Statistical (RANS, k-ε models required) |
| **Example** | Slow flow of honey, oil in thin tubes | Flow in water mains, air ducts, rivers |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU-Style Numerical Problem

**Problem:**
Water at **20 °C** flows through a **smooth circular pipe of diameter $D = 5 \text{ cm}$** at an **average velocity of $v = 0.02 \text{ m/s}$**.
Given: $\rho = 998 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}$.
Determine the type of flow.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Write the Reynolds number formula.

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot v \cdot D}{\mu}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute numerical values.

$$Re = \frac{998 \times 0.02 \times 0.05}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the numerator.

$$998 \times 0.02 = 19.96$$

$$19.96 \times 0.05 = 0.998$$

**Step 4:** Divide by the denominator.

$$Re = \frac{0.998}{1.002 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.998}{0.001002}$$

$$Re \approx 996.0$$

**Step 5:** Classify the flow.

Since $Re = 996 < 2100$, the flow is **LAMINAR**.

✅ **Answer: Laminar flow.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Flow Regime Classification — Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Fluid Flow in Pipe] --> B[Calculate Re = rho v D / mu]
    B --> C{Re < 2100?}
    C -->|YES| D[LAMINAR FLOW]
    C -->|NO| E{2100 <= Re <= 4000?}
    E -->|YES| F[TRANSITIONAL FLOW]
    E -->|NO| G[Re > 4000?]
    G -->|YES| H[TURBULENT FLOW]
    G -->|NO| I[RE-CHECK INPUTS]
    D --> J[Parabolic Velocity Profile]
    F --> K[Unstable; May switch regimes]
    H --> L[Blunt Velocity Profile]
    J --> M[End: Classification Complete]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    I --> M
```

## 4.2 Reynolds Experiment — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SETUP[Reynolds Experiment Setup]
        A1[Constant Head Tank] --> A2[Transparent Glass Pipe]
        A2 --> A3[Flow Control Valve]
        A3 --> A4[Discharge Tank]
    end
    subgraph INJECTION[Dye Injection System]
        B1[Dye Reservoir] --> B2[Capillary Tube Injector]
        B2 --> A2
    end
    subgraph OBSERVATION[Visual Observation]
        A2 --> C1[Low Velocity: Straight Dye Line]
        A2 --> C2[Moderate Velocity: Wavy/Oscillating Dye]
        A2 --> C3[High Velocity: Chaotic Dye Mixing]
    end
    C1 --> R1[LAMINAR: Re < 2100]
    C2 --> R2[TRANSITIONAL: 2100 <= Re <= 4000]
    C3 --> R3[TURBULENT: Re > 4000]
```

## 4.3 Comparison Matrix — Laminar vs. Turbulent Velocity Profiles

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PIPE[Pipe Cross-Section] --> SHAPE1[Laminar: Parabolic v max at center, v = 0 at wall]
    PIPE --> SHAPE2[Turbulent: Blunt/Flattened Nearly uniform core, thin boundary layer at wall]
    SHAPE1 --> COMP[Comparison Summary]
    SHAPE2 --> COMP
    COMP --> RESULT[Same flow rate, same D: Turbulent has higher wall shear and higher pressure drop]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define the Reynolds number. What is its physical significance in classifying fluid flow?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Reynolds number (Re)** is a **dimensionless quantity** defined as the ratio of **inertial forces to viscous forces** acting on a fluid element. For flow in a circular pipe, it is given by:

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \frac{v \, D}{\nu}$$

**Physical Significance:** The Reynolds number determines the **flow regime** of a fluid:
- **Re < 2100** → **Laminar flow** (viscous forces dominate, smooth, ordered motion).
- **2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000** → **Transitional flow** (unstable intermediate regime).
- **Re > 4000** → **Turbulent flow** (inertial forces dominate, chaotic, random motion with eddies).

The Reynolds number also serves as a **scaling law**: two geometrically similar flows with the same Re exhibit **dynamically similar** behavior, regardless of the actual size, velocity, or fluid.

> ---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Differentiate between laminar and turbulent flow with respect to (i) velocity profile, (ii) fluid mixing, and (iii) pressure drop relationship.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Property | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | **Velocity Profile** | **Parabolic** (zero at wall, max at centerline) | **Blunt/Flattened** (nearly uniform core, steep gradient at wall) |
| (ii) | **Fluid Mixing** | No macroscopic mixing between layers; only molecular diffusion | Vigorous eddy mixing; rapid transfer of momentum, heat, and mass |
| (iii) | **Pressure Drop** | $\Delta P \propto v$ (linearly proportional to velocity) | $\Delta P \propto v^{1.75 \text{ to } 2}$ (nearly proportional to $v^2$) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *Numerical & Analytical*
> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** A fluid of density $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.025 \text{ Pa·s}$ flows through a pipe of **diameter 50 mm** at a rate of **3.5 L/s**.
> (i) Calculate the **Reynolds number** and identify the type of flow. **(4 marks)**
> (ii) Determine the **critical velocity** for transition to turbulence. **(3 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, describe the **Reynolds experiment** to demonstrate the existence of laminar, transitional, and turbulent flow. Discuss the role of the **critical Reynolds number**. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a)(i) — Reynolds Number & Flow Type [4 marks]

**Given:** $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.025 \text{ Pa·s}$, $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$, $Q = 3.5 \text{ L/s} = 3.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$

**Step 1:** Calculate the **cross-sectional area** of the pipe.

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.05)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.0025}{4} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2:** Calculate the **average velocity** $v$.

$$v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.7826 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate the **Reynolds number**.

$$Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 1.7826 \times 0.05}{0.025}$$

$$Re = \frac{75.761}{0.025} = 3030.4$$

**Step 4:** Classify the flow.

Since $2100 \leq 3030.4 \leq 4000$, the flow is **TRANSITIONAL**. ✅

#### Part (a)(ii) — Critical Velocity [3 marks]

Using $Re_c = 2100$ for the lower critical value:

$$v_c = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D} = \frac{2100 \times 0.025}{850 \times 0.05}$$

$$v_c = \frac{52.5}{42.5} = 1.235 \text{ m/s}$$

✅ **Critical velocity $v_c \approx 1.235 \text{ m/s}$**.

#### Part (b) — Reynolds Experiment [7 marks]

**Apparatus:** A **constant-head water tank** feeds a **horizontal transparent glass pipe** (typically 12–25 mm diameter). A **dye reservoir** (colored potassium permanganate solution) is connected to a **fine capillary tube injector** that introduces a thin filament of dye at the **centerline** of the pipe entrance. A **flow-control valve** at the discharge end regulates the flow rate.

**Procedure & Observations:**

1. **At very low flow rates:** The dye filament travels as a **single, straight, undisturbed colored line** through the entire length of the pipe. The fluid layers move in an **orderly, parallel manner**. This is **LAMINAR FLOW**.

2. **As flow rate increases gradually:** The dye filament begins to **wave and oscillate** transversely across the pipe, exhibiting unstable behavior. The flow fluctuates unpredictably between laminar and turbulent. This is **TRANSITIONAL FLOW**.

3. **At high flow rates:** The dye filament **immediately bursts** into **random, chaotic eddies** and is **uniformly dispersed** across the entire pipe cross-section. Cross-currents and vortices are clearly visible. This is **TURBULENT FLOW**.

**Role of the Critical Reynolds Number:**

The transition from laminar to turbulent flow is not gradual but occurs at a well-defined threshold characterized by the **critical Reynolds number ($Re_c \approx 2100$)** for pipe flow. This dimensionless number represents the ratio of inertial to viscous forces and serves as a **universal criterion** for flow classification — independent of pipe size or fluid type. Below $Re_c$, viscous forces damp out any disturbance; above $Re_c$, inertial forces dominate and amplify disturbances into turbulence.

---

> ### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — *Conceptual & Theoretical*
> **[KTU University Exam — Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** Define **Reynolds number**. Starting from the fundamental forces acting on a fluid element, **derive the expression** for the Reynolds number for flow in a circular pipe. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A **crude oil** with $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $\mu = 0.09 \text{ Pa·s}$ is transported through a **15 cm diameter** pipeline.
> (i) If the **maximum allowable velocity** is **2.5 m/s**, determine the flow regime. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) What is the **maximum flow rate** that can be maintained while keeping the flow laminar? **(4 marks)**

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Definition & Derivation of Reynolds Number [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** The Reynolds number ($Re$) is a dimensionless parameter that represents the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces** in a flowing fluid. It is the **master criterion** for classifying the type of flow (laminar, transitional, or turbulent).

**Derivation (5 marks):** Consider a fluid element of characteristic linear dimension $L_c$ (e.g., the pipe diameter $D$) moving with velocity $v$ in a fluid of density $\rho$ and dynamic viscosity $\mu$.

**Step 1:** The **inertial force** (force to accelerate the mass):

$$F_{inertial} = (\text{mass}) \times (\text{acceleration}) = (\rho L_c^3) \times \left(\frac{v^2}{L_c}\right) = \rho \, v^2 \, L_c^2$$

**Step 2:** The **viscous force** (resistance due to viscosity):

$$F_{viscous} = \tau \times A = \left(\mu \frac{v}{L_c}\right) \times L_c^2 = \mu \, v \, L_c$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio:

$$Re = \frac{F_{inertial}}{F_{viscous}} = \frac{\rho \, v^2 \, L_c^2}{\mu \, v \, L_c} = \frac{\rho \, v \, L_c}{\mu}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $L_c = D$ (pipe diameter) for circular pipe flow:

$$\boxed{Re = \frac{\rho \, v \, D}{\mu} = \frac{v \, D}{\nu}}$$

This dimensionless number governs the flow regime:
- $Re < 2100$: Laminar
- $Re > 4000$: Turbulent

#### Part (b)(i) — Flow Regime at 2.5 m/s [3 marks]

**Given:** $\rho = 900 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 0.09 \text{ Pa·s}$, $D = 0.15 \text{ m}$, $v = 2.5 \text{ m/s}$

$$Re = \frac{900 \times 2.5 \times 0.15}{0.09} = \frac{337.5}{0.09} = 3750$$

Since $2100 < 3750 < 4000$ → **TRANSITIONAL flow**.

#### Part (b)(ii) — Maximum Flow Rate for Laminar Flow [4 marks]

**Step 1:** For laminar flow, $Re \leq 2100$. Therefore, maximum velocity for laminar flow:

$$v_{max,laminar} = \frac{Re_c \cdot \mu}{\rho \cdot D} = \frac{2100 \times 0.09}{900 \times 0.15} = \frac{189}{135} = 1.4 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Maximum volumetric flow rate:

$$Q_{max} = A \times v_{max,laminar} = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \times v_{max,laminar}$$

$$Q_{max} = \frac{\pi \times (0.15)^2}{4} \times 1.4 = 0.01767 \times 1.4 = 0.02474 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{max} \approx 0.0247 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 24.74 \text{ L/s}}$$

> [!WARNING] ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Always convert diameter from **mm or cm to meters** before calculation. Students frequently lose 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing Critical Values:** Use $Re_c = 2100$ for the **lower critical** (laminar → transitional) and $Re_c = 4000$ for the **upper critical** (transitional → turbulent). Do not mix these.
> 3. **Forgetting the Comparison:** Just computing $Re$ without comparing it to the threshold range yields **incomplete answers**. Always state the range and the conclusion.
> 4. **Velocity Profile Identification:** For laminar flow, the average velocity is $v_{avg} = v_{max}/2$. For turbulent flow, $v_{avg} \approx 0.82 \, v_{max}$. Many students write $v_{avg} = v_{max}$ — this is wrong.
> 5. **Dimensional Homogeneity:** Reynolds number is dimensionless. If your calculation gives units, you have made an error in unit conversion.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 🎯 **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Types of Flow**

- ✅ **Three flow types:** Laminar (Re < 2100), Transitional (2100 ≤ Re ≤ 4000), Turbulent (Re > 4000).
- ✅ **Reynolds Number** = ratio of **inertial to viscous forces** = $\rho v D / \mu = v D / \nu$ — **dimensionless**.
- ✅ **Critical Reynolds number for circular pipes:** $Re_c = 2100$ (laminar limit).
- ✅ **Laminar flow:** Smooth, parallel, layered motion; **parabolic** velocity profile; $v_{max} = 2 v_{avg}$.
- ✅ **Turbulent flow:** Chaotic, random eddies; **blunt** velocity profile; $v_{avg} \approx 0.82 v_{max}$.
- ✅ **Reynolds Experiment** (1883): Dye filament visualization in a glass pipe — the foundational experiment.
- ✅ **Pressure drop in laminar flow:** $\Delta P \propto v$ (linear, from Hagen-Poiseuille equation).
- ✅ **Pressure drop in turbulent flow:** $\Delta P \propto v^{1.75–2}$ (nearly quadratic).
- ✅ **Friction factor:** Laminar $f = 64/Re$; Turbulent $f = 0.316/Re^{0.25}$ (Blasius, Re < 10⁵).
- ✅ **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu = \mu / \rho$ is useful when only $\nu$ is given.
- ✅ **Wall shear stress** is critical for pipe design, biofilm analysis, and pumping calculations.
- ✅ **Engineering importance:** Re governs heat transfer, mass transfer, mixing, pumping power, and process safety.
- ✅ **Dimensional analysis** confirms Re is dimensionless — a key property for scaling and dynamic similarity.
- ✅ **Geometric dependence:** $Re_c$ varies with geometry (2100 for circular pipes, 2300 for square ducts, 2900 for parallel plates).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: How to verify the continuity, momentum, and energy balance equations with reference to the selected flow system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** How to verify the continuity, momentum, and energy balance equations with reference to the selected flow system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Selected Flow System

In **PBCHT304 – Fluid and Particle Mechanics** (KTU 2024 Scheme), Module 1 trains the learner to apply the three governing conservation laws of fluid flow to a **selected flow system** — usually a closed-circuit hydraulic test rig comprising a **sump tank, centrifugal pump, calibrated flow meters (Venturi, orifice, rotameter), piezometer / manometer tappings, pressure gauges, and a discharge control valve**. The same rig is used to experimentally verify the **Continuity, Momentum, and Energy balance equations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Selected Flow System (KTU 2024 Reference Setup):**
> A horizontal / inclined pipe test section of constant or varying cross-section, fed by an overhead tank, equipped with:
> 1. **Venturi / Orifice meter** → verifies *Continuity* and *Energy*.
> 2. **Impact of jet on flat / hemispherical vane** → verifies *Momentum*.
> 3. **Static pressure tappings + piezometer** → verifies *Bernoulli's (Energy) equation*.
> 4. **Calibrated measuring tank** → gives actual discharge *Q* (used as control).

## 1.2 Formal Definition of the Three Conservation Laws

| # | Law | Engineering Statement |
|---|-----|----------------------|
| 1 | **Continuity Equation** | Mass entering a control volume per unit time = Mass leaving it (for steady, incompressible flow: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$). |
| 2 | **Momentum Balance Equation** | Net external force on a control volume = Rate of change of momentum flux of the fluid crossing the control surface. |
| 3 | **Energy Balance Equation** | Mechanical energy added to a control volume = Net mechanical energy leaving + Mechanical energy lost to friction. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Garden Hose" Analogy**
> Imagine water flowing through a flexible hose. If you **squeeze** the hose (reducing area), water speeds up — that is the **continuity principle** at work. Now if the hose is bent and pushes against your hand, the *push* you feel is the **momentum** being delivered by the water. Finally, if the hose is held high above the ground and water falls onto a turbine, the *height (potential), speed (kinetic), and pressure* all trade places with each other, while some energy is lost as heat in the pipe — that is the **energy balance** (Bernoulli + losses).

- **g (acceleration due to gravity)** = **9.81 m/s²** — used to convert pressure heads to metres of fluid.
- **ρ (density of water)** = **1000 kg/m³** — reference fluid for KTU lab calculations.

## 1.4 Relevance in Chemical Engineering (KTU NEP 2020 Context)

These three laws form the **analytical backbone** of every unit operation — from *pipe sizing* (continuity), to *pump power calculation* (energy), to *design of impellers, ejectors, and cyclone separators* (momentum). KTU examiners expect students to know how to **plan, execute, and interpret** experiments that validate these laws on a standard flow rig.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Governing Equations — Structured Logic

### 2.1.1 Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation)

**Why it works:** Because mass cannot be created or destroyed in a steady flow.

**How to apply it:**

1. Select a control volume (CV) bounded by an inlet (section 1) and outlet (section 2).
2. For *steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow* with no internal sources/sinks:

$$
\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2
$$

3. Cancelling ρ:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q \text{ (constant)}
$$

4. Therefore, velocity is **inversely proportional** to the cross-sectional area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Experimental Verification in the KTU Rig:**
> In a *Venturi meter*, the inlet area $A_1$ (converging section) is larger than throat area $A_2$. Using a *pitot-static tube* or *rotameter*, measure $V_1$ and $V_2$. Plot $Q_{calculated} = A_1 V_1$ vs $Q_{calculated} = A_2 V_2$. The two should be equal within ± 2–5 %.

### 2.1.2 Momentum Balance Equation (Newton's 2nd Law for Fluids)

**Why it works:** Newton's second law states $\vec{F} = \frac{d(m\vec{V})}{dt}$. For a steady CV with uniform inlet and outlet:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{external} = \dot{m}(\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})
$$

**How to apply it:**

1. Define CV around the object experiencing the force (e.g., a flat plate, hemispherical bucket, or pipe bend).
2. Account for **pressure forces**, **shear forces (weight of fluid + weight of vane)**, and the **momentum flux change**.
3. For a **horizontal jet impinging on a stationary flat plate** (θ = 90°):

$$
F = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta) = \rho Q V \quad (\text{for } \theta = 90°)
$$

4. The *measured* weight on the proving ring/spring balance should match the *calculated* $F$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Experimental Setup:** A nozzle discharges a water jet onto a flat or curved vane attached to a **proving ring** (deflection → force). The run is repeated for multiple flow rates; the theoretical and experimental forces are tabulated and compared.

### 2.1.3 Energy Balance Equation (Bernoulli + Friction)

**Why it works:** The total mechanical energy per unit weight of fluid — pressure, kinetic, and potential — is conserved, except for losses.

**How to apply it:**

1. Write Bernoulli between sections 1 and 2 of the test pipe:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

where $h_f$ is the head loss due to friction.

2. Measure $P_1, P_2$ using *piezometers* or a *U-tube manometer*.
3. Measure $V_1, V_2$ from *continuity* (using the calibrated discharge).
4. Compute *Total Head at 1* and *Total Head at 2*. They differ only by $h_f$, which can be cross-checked using the **Darcy–Weisbach equation**:

$$
h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}
$$

where **f** is the Darcy friction factor, *L* = length between tappings, *D* = pipe diameter.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Energy equation is NOT just Bernoulli's equation. It is **Bernoulli + losses + pump work – turbine work**. Always include $h_f$ in your calculation or the heads will not match.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **minimum formula inventory** you must memorise for any verification problem in Module 1.

| # | Equation | Expression | Variables / Units |
|---|----------|-----------|-------------------|
| 1 | Continuity (steady, incompressible) | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | A in m², V in m/s |
| 2 | Mass flow rate | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | kg/s |
| 3 | Volumetric flow rate | $Q = A V$ | m³/s |
| 4 | Momentum on flat plate (θ=90°) | $F = \rho Q V$ | N |
| 5 | Momentum on inclined vane | $F = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)$ | N |
| 6 | Bernoulli (no losses) | $\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{constant}$ | m of fluid |
| 7 | Real Energy Equation | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$ | m of fluid |
| 8 | Head loss (Darcy–Weisbach) | $h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | m |
| 9 | Head loss (Fanning relation) | $h_f = \frac{32 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | m (use fanning *f*) |
| 10 | Reynolds number | $Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu}$ | dimensionless |
| 11 | Manometer equation (U-tube) | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | Pa |
| 12 | Discharge from Venturi | $Q = C_d \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2g \Delta h}$ | m³/s |
| 13 | Pitot tube velocity | $V = \sqrt{2 g \Delta h}$ | m/s |
| 14 | Force on pipe bend | $F_x = \rho Q (V_{1x} - V_{2x}) + (P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2) \cos\alpha$ | N |

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love the **% error** calculation:
> $$\% \text{ Error} = \left\vert \frac{\text{Theoretical} - \text{Experimental}}{\text{Theoretical}} \right\vert \times 100$$
> Use `\vert` (not the vertical bar) inside the equation to avoid breaking markdown tables if you re-insert it.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Chemical Engineering

- **Continuity** → used in design of *nozzles, venturis, orifice plates, control valves, and flow distributors* in reactors.
- **Momentum** → used in *design of mixers, jet ejectors, hydraulic turbines, cyclone separators, and propellant rockets*.
- **Energy** → forms the basis for *pump sizing, pipe network analysis, compressor work calculation, and process economics* (cost of pumping = power × time × electricity tariff).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation (Reynolds Transport Theorem applied to Mass)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Derive the continuity equation for a control volume."* The following derivation is the complete, board-ready version.

### Step 1 — Start with the General Continuity Equation (Integral Form)

For any control volume (CV):

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

The first term represents the rate of accumulation of mass inside the CV. The second term is the net mass efflux through the control surface (CS), with $\hat{n}$ being the outward unit normal.

### Step 2 — Apply the Steady-State Assumption

For a **steady flow**, the fluid properties at every point in the CV do not change with time. Therefore the accumulation term vanishes:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = 0
$$

The integral form reduces to:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

### Step 3 — Specialise to an Inlet (1) and Outlet (2)

At the inlet, $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = -V_1$ (velocity is *into* the CV against the outward normal). At the outlet, $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = +V_2$. Substituting:

$$
-\rho A_1 V_1 + \rho A_2 V_2 = 0
$$

Rearranging:

$$
\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2
$$

### Step 4 — Simplify for Incompressible Flow

If the fluid is **incompressible** (ρ = constant), ρ cancels from both sides:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

**Final Result:** $Q_1 = Q_2 = \text{constant}$. The volumetric flow rate is the same at every cross-section of a steady, incompressible flow.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Momentum Balance Equation (Linear Momentum)

### Step 1 — Start with Newton's Second Law for a CV

The general form of the linear momentum balance:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

The first term is the rate of change of momentum *inside* the CV; the second is the net efflux of momentum through the CS.

### Step 2 — Apply Steady State

For **steady flow**, the internal storage term is zero:

$$
\sum \vec{F}_{ext} = \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

### Step 3 — Apply to a Jet Impinging on a Flat Stationary Plate

Consider a horizontal jet of water emerging from a nozzle of cross-section $A$ with velocity $V$. It strikes a flat plate placed perpendicular to the jet.

- The plate is stationary, so the **outlet velocity is zero** (jet is deflected radially along the plate).
- Atmospheric pressure acts on the free jet and on the plate's wet surface, so pressure forces cancel.
- The only external force the fluid exerts on the plate is the *force* $F$ resisting the change in momentum; by Newton's 3rd law, the fluid feels an equal and opposite reaction.

Mass flow rate of the jet:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A V
$$

Inlet momentum flux (x-direction):

$$
\vec{V}_{in} \cdot (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) dA = V \cdot (V)(-1) dA = -V^2 dA
$$

Outlet momentum flux (jet spreads tangentially → x-component of velocity at the plate = 0):

$$
\vec{V}_{out} \cdot (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) dA = 0
$$

Summing over the CS:

$$
\sum F_x = 0 - (\rho A V)(V) = -\rho A V^2
$$

The negative sign indicates the force is in the *negative x-direction* on the fluid. By Newton's third law, the **force on the plate is positive x-direction**:

$$
F = \rho A V^2
$$

But $Q = AV$, hence:

$$
F = \rho Q V
$$

### Step 4 — Generalise to an Inclined Vane (θ = angle of deflection)

If the vane deflects the jet through an angle θ, the outlet x-momentum is $V \cos\theta$ (with $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = +V$ since flow leaves):

$$
F = \rho Q V (1 - \cos\theta)
$$

- For **flat plate** (θ = 90°): $F = \rho Q V$ (matches step 3 since $1 - \cos 90° = 1$).
- For **hemispherical bucket** (θ = 180°): $F = 2 \rho Q V$.

> [!NOTE]
> This is the derivation KTU examiners expect in the 14-mark questions. Show all three steps (general, steady-state, specialisation) — students who skip to the result lose 3–4 marks.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Energy Balance Equation (Bernoulli + Friction)

### Step 1 — Start from the First Law of Thermodynamics for an Open System (Steady-State Energy Equation, SSFEE)

For a steady flow with one inlet and one outlet, and neglecting heat transfer and shaft work:

$$
\dot{m} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{in} + \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \left( h + \frac{V^2}{2} + gz \right)_{out} + \dot{W}_s + \dot{E}_{loss}
$$

Dividing by $\dot{m} g$ to express in head form (metres of fluid):

$$
\frac{h_1}{g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{h_2}{g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f + h_{pump} - h_{turbine}
$$

where $h_f$ is the friction head loss (m), $h_{pump}$ is the head added by a pump (m), and $h_{turbine}$ is the head extracted by a turbine (m).

### Step 2 — Enthalpy Conversion to Pressure Head

Using $h = u + Pv = u + P/\rho$ (per unit mass), and noting that internal energy $u$ remains effectively constant in incompressible isothermal flow, the $u$ terms cancel, leaving:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f
$$

This is the **Energy Balance Equation** for the selected flow system.

### Step 3 — Friction Head from Darcy–Weisbach

The friction head loss for a length $L$ of pipe of diameter $D$ is:

$$
h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{2 f L V^2}{g D}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + \frac{2 f L V^2}{g D}
$$

### Step 4 — Experimental Form for the KTU Rig

On the test rig, the pipe is horizontal, so $z_1 = z_2$ and the equation simplifies to:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_f
$$

This is what the student **verifies** by measuring:

- $P_1, P_2$ from piezometer / manometer tappings,
- $V_1, V_2$ from continuity (using measured $Q$ and known $A_1, A_2$),
- $h_f$ from the difference between total heads, and
- $f$ from the Moody chart using the measured Reynolds number $Re = \rho V D / \mu$.

> [!TIP]
> **Verification Procedure (exam-friendly):**
> 1. Note the manometer reading $h$ at section 1 and section 2.
> 2. Convert to pressure: $P = \rho_f g h + \rho_m g (\Delta z)$ if a differential manometer is used.
> 3. Compute total head at 1 and total head at 2.
> 4. Plot *Total Head at 1* (y-axis) vs *Total Head at 2* (x-axis). A straight line of slope 1 confirms Bernoulli.
> 5. The vertical offset between the two lines = $h_f$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Theory vs Experiment)

| Verification | Governing Equation | Measured Quantity | Calculated Quantity | Typical % Error (KTU Acceptable) |
|--------------|--------------------|-------------------|---------------------|------------------------------------|
| Continuity | $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ | $Q$ in collecting tank, $A_1, A_2$ from geometry | $V_1 = Q/A_1, V_2 = Q/A_2$ | ≤ 5 % |
| Momentum (jet on plate) | $F = \rho Q V$ | Weight on proving ring | $F$ from $\rho, Q, V$ | ≤ 8 % |
| Energy (Bernoulli) | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$ | $P_1, P_2$ from manometer, $V_1, V_2$ from $Q$ | Total head 1, Total head 2 | ≤ 5 % |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Selected Flow System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow of data and fluid** through the KTU 2024 selected-flow-system test rig. Each block represents a real physical component; arrows show the *cause-and-effect* path from pump input to discharge measurement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sump Tank] -->|suction line| B[Centrifugal Pump]
    B -->|delivery| C[Strainer and Air Vessel]
    C --> D[Header Tank / Overhead Tank]
    D -->|gravity flow| E[Test Pipe Section]
    E --> F1[Venturi Meter]
    E --> F2[Orifice Meter]
    E --> F3[Rotameter]
    E --> G[Piezometer Tappings 1 and 2]
    E --> H[U tube Differential Manometer]
    E --> I[Pipe Bend Force Sensor]
    E --> J[Impact of Jet Vane on Proving Ring]
    E --> K[Discharge Control Valve]
    K --> L[Calibrated Measuring Tank]
    L -->|overflow back to| A

    subgraph VER[Verification Modules]
        V1[Continuity Verification: A1V1 = A2V2]
        V2[Momentum Verification: F = rhoQV]
        V3[Energy Verification: Bernoulli plus h sub f]
    end

    F1 --> V1
    G --> V3
    H --> V3
    I --> V2
    J --> V2
    F1 --> V3
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The **left column** (A → B → C → D) is the *supply* path.
- The **centre column** (E → F1, F2, F3, G, H, I, J) is the *test section* where measurements are taken.
- The **right column** (K → L → A) is the *return* path that closes the loop and provides the actual discharge *Q*.
- The **VER subgraph** aggregates which physical instrument validates which conservation law.

## 4.2 Schematic — Forces and Flows on an Impact-of-Jet Vane (Momentum Verification)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Nozzle] -->|jet velocity V| J[Water Jet]
    J -->|strikes| P[Flat Vane theta equals 90 deg]
    P -->|radial spread| SPL[Split Streams]
    P -->|reaction force F| W[Proving Ring Weight Reading]
    J -->|mass flow rho Q V| P

    F[Force Balance: F equals rho Q V] -.-> P
```

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid cannot draw the curved splashing stream physically, but the block diagram above clearly shows the **cause** (jet from nozzle) → **effect** (force on vane) → **measurement** (proving ring) → **theoretical check** (F = ρQV).

## 4.3 Schematic — Energy Verification Through a Venturi

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Section 1: Inlet area A1, pressure P1, velocity V1] --> TH[Throat area A2, pressure P2, velocity V2]
    TH --> S2[Section 2: Recovery area A3, pressure P3, velocity V3]
    S1 -->|piezometer 1| M1[Manometer limb 1]
    TH -->|piezometer 2| M2[Manometer limb 2]
    M1 --> DM[Differential Manometer reading h]
    M2 --> DM
    DM -->|delta h gives P1 minus P2| BE[Bernoulli Check: P1 by rho g plus V1 squared by 2g equals P2 by rho g plus V2 squared by 2g plus h sub f]
```

**Note on node IDs:** All node identifiers in the diagrams above (e.g., `A`, `B1`, `F1`, `VER`, `BE`) are pure alphanumeric strings without any reserved Mermaid keywords, ensuring clean rendering in the KTU e-learning portal.
