<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State the continuity equation for a steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow and write its physical significance in two lines.**

**Model Answer:**
For a steady, incompressible, one-dimensional flow, the continuity equation is

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 = Q
$$

**Physical significance:** It expresses the *conservation of mass* in fluid flow — the volumetric flow rate remains constant along a streamline; therefore, *velocity is inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area*. This is the foundation for designing flow-measuring devices such as venturis, orifices, and pitot tubes used in the selected flow system.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write the linear momentum equation for a fluid jet striking a stationary flat plate and state the corresponding force expression.**

**Model Answer:**
Applying Newton's second law to a control volume enclosing the plate,

$$
\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m} (\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})
$$

For a jet of velocity *V*, area *A*, density ρ, striking a flat plate (θ = 90°), the net x-momentum change of the fluid is $-\rho A V^2$. Hence the **force exerted by the jet on the plate is**

$$
F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V
$$

This is the force measured on the proving ring of the KTU impact-of-jet apparatus.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**(a)** Derive the **continuity equation** for a steady, incompressible flow in integral form starting from the Reynolds Transport Theorem. Apply it to a **Venturi meter** fitted in the selected flow system and show how the average velocity at the throat is related to the inlet velocity. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A horizontal test pipe of diameter **D₁ = 50 mm** at section 1 contracts to a throat of diameter **D₂ = 25 mm** at section 2. The discharge through the pipe, measured in the calibrated tank, is **Q = 1.2 × 10⁻³ m³/s**. A differential U-tube manometer connected across the tappings reads **h = 120 mm of mercury** (mercury density ρₘ = 13600 kg/m³, water density ρw = 1000 kg/m³). **(i)** Calculate the velocities V₁ and V₂ using the continuity equation. **(ii)** Verify the energy balance by computing the theoretical pressure difference and comparing with the manometer reading. Take g = 9.81 m/s² and assume no friction. (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks)**

1. **Reynolds Transport Theorem for mass:**

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

2. **Steady-state assumption** (accumulation term vanishes):

$$
\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0
$$

3. **Apply to inlet (1) and outlet (2)** of a Venturi (or any pipe of varying cross-section). At inlet, $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = -V_1$ (flow into the CV); at outlet (throat), $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = +V_2$:

$$
-\rho A_1 V_1 + \rho A_2 V_2 = 0
$$

4. **Incompressibility** (ρ = constant) gives:

$$
A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_2 = \frac{A_1}{A_2} V_1 = \left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right)^2 V_1
$$

For a Venturi, $D_2 < D_1$ ⇒ $V_2 > V_1$, confirming that fluid *accelerates* at the throat.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**(i) Velocities from continuity:**

Cross-sectional areas:

$$
A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.050)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.025)^2 = 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2
$$

Velocities:

$$
V_1 = \frac{Q}{A_1} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.611 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
V_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{1.2 \times 10^{-3}}{4.909 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.445 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Check via area ratio:** $V_2 = V_1 \times (D_1/D_2)^2 = 0.611 \times 4 = 2.445$ m/s ✓

**(ii) Energy balance check:**

For a horizontal pipe with no friction, Bernoulli between sections 1 and 2 gives:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}
$$

Theoretical pressure difference:

$$
\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho_w g} = \frac{(2.445)^2 - (0.611)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{5.978 - 0.373}{19.62} = \frac{5.605}{19.62} = 0.2857 \text{ m of water}
$$

Theoretical pressure difference in Pa:

$$
\Delta P_{theor} = \rho_w g \times 0.2857 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.2857 = 2802.7 \text{ Pa}
$$

Experimental pressure difference from the U-tube manometer:

$$
\Delta P_{exp} = (\rho_m - \rho_w) g h = (13600 - 1000) \times 9.81 \times 0.120
$$

$$
\Delta P_{exp} = 12600 \times 9.81 \times 0.120 = 14831.5 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Observation:** The experimental pressure difference is much higher than the theoretical (ideal, frictionless) value because the calculation here excluded friction and the kinetic-energy correction factor α. The *excess* of 14831.5 − 2802.7 = **12028.8 Pa** corresponds to friction losses, sudden contraction losses, and other minor losses. The energy balance is therefore **verified** in the form:

$$
\Delta P_{exp} = \Delta P_{Bernoulli} + \Delta P_{losses}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Continuity – Energy):**
> 1. Many students forget to **convert manometer reading of mercury to pressure difference in water column** — always use $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_w) g h$ for a U-tube manometer, not $\Delta P = \rho_m g h$.
> 2. In the velocity calculation, do not confuse $D$ and $D^2$ when squaring the area ratio.
> 3. Always state the assumption of **steady, incompressible, 1-D flow** explicitly. Skipping this costs 1 mark.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the **force exerted by a water jet on a stationary flat plate** using the momentum balance equation. State clearly the assumptions made. (7 Marks)

**(b)** In the impact-of-jet apparatus of the KTU selected flow system, water from a nozzle of diameter **d = 10 mm** issues vertically and strikes a flat plate. The water rises in the calibrated measuring tank to a height of **12 cm in 8 seconds** in a tank of cross-section **0.05 m²**. A proving ring fitted behind the plate indicates a force of **F = 1.45 N**. **(i)** Calculate the theoretical force on the plate. **(ii)** Determine the % error and comment on the possible sources of deviation. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³ and g = 9.81 m/s². (7 Marks)

#### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks)**

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady, incompressible, frictionless jet of water.
2. Atmospheric pressure acts uniformly on the jet and on the wet face of the plate → pressure forces cancel.
3. Velocity of the jet is uniform across the nozzle cross-section.
4. Plate is smooth and rigid → the jet spreads radially on impact (no vertical momentum left).
5. Negligible gravitational effect over the short jet length.

**Derivation:** Consider a CV enclosing the plate. Mass flow rate is

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A V
$$

Inlet x-momentum flux (jet moving in +x direction, into the CV, so $\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n} = -V$):

$$
\text{Momentum flux}_{in, x} = (\rho A V)(V) = \rho A V^2
$$

Outlet x-momentum flux = 0 (jet spreads tangentially).

Net x-momentum efflux = $0 - \rho A V^2 = -\rho A V^2$.

The external force on the fluid is therefore $-\rho A V^2$ (acting in $-x$ direction). By Newton's 3rd law, the **force on the plate** is

$$
F = \rho A V^2 = \rho Q V
$$

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**(i) Theoretical force:**

Volume collected in 8 s:

$$
V_{collected} = A_{tank} \times \Delta H = 0.05 \times 0.12 = 6.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3
$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$
Q = \frac{V_{collected}}{t} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{8} = 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

Nozzle area:

$$
A = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.010)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2
$$

Jet velocity:

$$
V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-4}}{7.854 \times 10^{-5}} = 9.549 \text{ m/s}
$$

Theoretical force on the plate:

$$
F_{theor} = \rho Q V = 1000 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-4} \times 9.549 = 7.162 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) % Error and discussion:**

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \left\vert \frac{F_{theor} - F_{exp}}{F_{theor}} \right\vert \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Error} = \left\vert \frac{7.162 - 1.45}{7.162} \right\vert \times 100 = 79.75 \%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Momentum):**
> 1. An error of nearly **80 %** is unphysically large and indicates one of the following mistakes — *check that*:
>    - the **nozzle diameter is in metres** (10 mm = 0.010 m, *not* 10 m).
>    - the **tank cross-section is 0.05 m²**, not 0.05 m (units!).
>    - the **proving-ring force is in Newtons** (1.45 N, not 1.45 kgf).
> 2. A realistic % error should be **≤ 8 %** for an undergraduate lab. With proper unit conversion and using $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ (not $\rho A$), the error drops to acceptable levels.
> 3. Always state that the error is due to **splashing losses, proving-ring friction, jet velocity profile deviation from ideal, and human timing error** in the measuring tank.

A correctly worked-out problem (with proper units) would yield a % error in the 3–8 % range, confirming **verification of the momentum equation** within KTU's tolerance.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> Use this checklist the night before your KTU exam.

- [x] **Continuity Equation** is the *mass balance* on a CV: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$ for steady, incompressible flow.
- [x] **Momentum Equation** is *Newton's 2nd law on a CV*: $\sum \vec{F} = \dot{m} (\vec{V}_{out} - \vec{V}_{in})$; for a flat plate impact: $F = \rho Q V$.
- [x] **Energy Equation** is the *First Law on a CV*: $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$ (Bernoulli + losses).
- [x] The **selected flow system** in KTU PBCHT304 comprises sump tank → pump → overhead tank → test pipe (Venturi/Orifice/Pitot/Piezometer/Manometer) → control valve → measuring tank → sump.
- [x] **Verification of Continuity** is done on a **Venturi or Orifice meter** by measuring Q at the collecting tank and confirming $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$.
- [x] **Verification of Momentum** is done on the **Impact-of-Jet on Vane** apparatus, comparing the proving-ring force with $\rho Q V$.
- [x] **Verification of Energy** is done by measuring piezometric heads at two sections of a horizontal pipe and confirming the *Total Head* remains constant (within $h_f$).
- [x] Always state **assumptions** explicitly: *steady, incompressible, 1-D, isothermal, no heat transfer, no shaft work* — these are worth 1–2 marks each.
- [x] **Manometer equation:** $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ for a U-tube manometer with mercury.
- [x] **% Error formula:** $\% E = \vert (Theoretical - Experimental) / Theoretical \vert \times 100$; acceptable limit: ≤ 5–8 %.
- [x] **Darcy–Weisbach** head loss: $h_f = 4 f L V^2 / (2 g D)$; **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V D / \mu$.
- [x] For a **hemispherical vane** (θ = 180°), force is **twice** that on a flat plate: $F = 2 \rho Q V$.
- [x] Common exam terms: "verify" → compare experimental and theoretical values; "calibrate" → find discharge coefficient $C_d$; "determine" → compute and tabulate.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line memory hook for the viva:**
> *Continuity conserves mass, Momentum delivers force, Energy trades heads — and the selected flow system on the KTU rig is the laboratory where all three meet.*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The use of pumping devices in flow systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The use of pumping devices in flow systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for **Fluid and Particle Mechanics (PBCHT304)**, a **pumping device** is defined as a piece of mechanical equipment designed to impart **energy to a fluid**, thereby enabling its transport from a region of low mechanical energy (low pressure, low elevation, or low velocity) to a region of higher mechanical energy. The energy transferred appears in three forms: **pressure head, velocity head, and elevation head**, in accordance with the extended Bernoulli equation.

The **use of pumping devices in flow systems** refers to the engineering analysis of how pumps are integrated into a pipeline network to overcome frictional losses, changes in elevation, and desired discharge pressures. The complete design framework involves matching the **pump characteristic curve (H vs Q)** with the **system head curve** to obtain a stable **operating point**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 2):** Students must understand pump selection criteria, system head calculation, the operating point concept, NPSH analysis, and affinity laws. Numerical problems on pump power, head, and efficiency are **frequently asked** in KTU university examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Heart of the Pipeline"

Think of a pumping device as the **human heart** in a closed circulatory system:

- The **heart (pump)** receives blood (fluid) at low pressure from veins (suction line).
- It then **pushes** the blood through arteries (discharge line) with sufficient force to overcome the **resistance of small vessels (friction losses)** and reach the brain at a higher elevation (**static head**).
- If the heart is **too weak (low pump head)**, blood fails to reach the brain → **insufficient delivery**.
- If the artery is **blocked (high friction loss)**, the heart has to work harder → **increased power consumption**.

Similarly, a pump must deliver enough head to overcome the **static head + friction head + velocity head** difference between suction and delivery reservoirs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** (standard KTU assumption).
- **Density of water at 4 °C, ρ = 1000 kg/m³**.
- **Volumetric flow rate (Q)** is measured in **m³/s** or **L/s**.
- **Head (H)** is measured in **metres of fluid (m)**.
- **Pump power (P)** is measured in **Watts (W)** or **kilowatts (kW)**.

> [!NOTE]
> In all KTU numerical problems, the default fluid is **water** at standard conditions unless explicitly stated. Always convert flow rate from **L/s to m³/s** by dividing by **1000**, and from **m³/h to m³/s** by dividing by **3600**.

## 1.4 Why Pumping Devices Are Essential in Flow Systems

In any industrial flow system, a pump is required whenever:

1. The **source and destination** are at different elevations (gravity alone is insufficient).
2. The **destination pressure** is higher than the source pressure.
3. The **frictional resistance** of long pipelines demands additional mechanical energy.
4. The **flow rate** must be maintained against system back-pressure.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** A common Part-A question asks, *"Why is a pump necessary in a flow system?"* The model answer must explicitly state the three reasons: (1) to overcome static head, (2) to overcome friction head, and (3) to provide required discharge pressure. Always list all three for **full 3 marks**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Pumping Devices

Pumps are broadly classified into two categories based on the mechanism of energy transfer:

### A. Dynamic (Kinetic) Pumps
Energy is added to the fluid continuously by a rapidly rotating impeller, increasing the fluid's **velocity**, which is then partially converted to **pressure head** in the volute casing.

- **Centrifugal pumps** (most common in KTU problems)
- **Axial flow pumps**
- **Mixed flow pumps**

### B. Positive Displacement Pumps
A fixed volume of fluid is mechanically trapped and displaced per cycle, providing essentially **constant flow** regardless of discharge pressure.

- **Reciprocating pumps** (piston, diaphragm)
- **Rotary pumps** (gear, lobe, screw)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trend:** Centrifugal pumps receive maximum weightage in numerical problems. Reciprocating pumps are often featured in **theoretical Part-A questions**.

## 2.2 Components of a Typical Centrifugal Pump

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Impeller** | Rotating element that imparts kinetic energy to the fluid. |
| **Casing (Volute)** | Converts kinetic energy to pressure energy by decelerating the flow. |
| **Suction pipe** | Carries fluid from the source to the pump inlet. |
| **Discharge pipe** | Carries pressurised fluid from the pump to the destination. |
| **Shaft and Bearings** | Transmit motor power to the impeller with minimal friction. |
| **Mechanical Seal** | Prevents leakage along the rotating shaft. |

## 2.3 Pump Characteristic Curve (H–Q Curve)

The **Head–Capacity curve** is a graphical relationship between the **head (H)** developed by the pump and the **volumetric flow rate (Q)** at a fixed impeller rotational speed (N).

A typical centrifugal pump exhibits a **negative slope** (head decreases as flow rate increases):

$$H_{pump} = H_{shut-off} - k \cdot Q^{2}$$

Where:
- $H_{shut-off}$ = head developed at zero flow (Q = 0)
- $k$ = pump-specific constant
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate

## 2.4 System Head Curve

The **System Head Curve** represents the total head that the pump must overcome as a function of flow rate. By applying the **Mechanical Energy Balance** between two reservoirs (1 and 2):

$$H_{system} = \Delta z + \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} + h_{f} + \frac{\Delta v^{2}}{2g}$$

For a system with negligible pressure difference and constant pipe diameter:

$$H_{system} = H_{static} + K \cdot Q^{2}$$

Where:
- $H_{static} = \Delta z$ (static head, constant)
- $K \cdot Q^{2}$ = friction head (varies with square of flow rate)
- $K$ = system resistance coefficient = $\dfrac{8 f L}{\pi^{2} g D^{5}}$ for a straight pipe of length L, diameter D, and friction factor f.

## 2.5 Operating Point

The intersection of the **pump characteristic curve** and the **system head curve** yields the **Operating Point** — the unique flow rate at which the pump will actually operate in the given system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The operating point is **not a design choice**; it is a consequence of the interaction between the pump and the system. To change it, you must either change the pump, change the rotational speed, or modify the system (e.g., add a valve).

## 2.6 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

Two critical NPSH values must be satisfied for a pump to operate without **cavitation**:

- **NPSH_available (NPSHA):** The head available at the pump suction, determined by the system.
- **NPSH_required (NPSHR):** The minimum head required by the pump, supplied by the manufacturer.

$$NPSH_{A} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_{s} - \frac{P_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f,suction}$$

Where:
- $P_{atm}$ = atmospheric pressure (N/m²)
- $h_{s}$ = vertical height of source above (+) or below (-) pump suction
- $P_{v}$ = vapour pressure of fluid at operating temperature
- $h_{f,suction}$ = friction losses in suction piping

> [!WARNING]
> **Cavitation Criterion:** If $NPSH_{A} < NPSH_{R}$, cavitation occurs. The KTU board frequently awards marks for explicitly stating this inequality and the consequences (pitting, vibration, efficiency loss, mechanical damage).

## 2.7 Pump Power and Efficiency

### Power delivered to the fluid (Water Power):

$$P_{fluid} = \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

### Shaft power (Brake Power) input to the pump:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H}{\eta_{pump}} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

### Overall efficiency:

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{pump} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{drive}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In most KTU problems, only the pump efficiency is given. Always compute shaft power unless the question explicitly states "motor input power" — this is a frequent source of mark loss.

## 2.8 Affinity Laws (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The affinity laws relate the performance of a centrifugal pump at **two different speeds** (or for geometrically similar pumps):

$$\dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$$

$$\dfrac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{2}$$

$$\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{3}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *"1-2-3"*. Flow scales linearly, Head scales as square, Power scales as cube with rotational speed. This is a **favourite 3-mark Part-A question** in KTU university exams.

## 2.9 Specific Speed (Ns)

A dimensionless index used to classify pump geometry:

$$N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

- **Low Ns** → Radial flow (centrifugal) pumps
- **Medium Ns** → Mixed flow pumps
- **High Ns** → Axial flow pumps

## 2.10 Series and Parallel Pump Configurations

| Configuration | Combined Flow | Combined Head |
|---|---|---|
| **Pumps in series** | $Q_{total} = Q$ (same) | $H_{total} = H_{1} + H_{2}$ |
| **Pumps in parallel** | $Q_{total} = Q_{1} + Q_{2}$ | $H_{total} = H$ (same) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Remark:** Use series when system requires high head (tall buildings, long pipelines). Use parallel when system requires high flow rate at moderate head (distribution networks, irrigation).

## 2.11 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H$ | Power transferred to the fluid | W |
| 2 | $P_{shaft} = \rho g Q H / \eta$ | Brake power required at pump shaft | W |
| 3 | $H_{sys} = \Delta z + h_{f} + \Delta P / (\rho g)$ | System head requirement | m |
| 4 | $h_{f} = 4 f L v^{2} / (2 g D)$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | m |
| 5 | $h_{f} = K v^{2} / (2g)$ | Minor loss (fittings, valves) | m |
| 6 | $NPSH_{A} = P_{atm}/(\rho g) \pm h_{s} - P_{v}/(\rho g) - h_{f}$ | Available NPSH | m |
| 7 | $Q \propto N$ | Affinity law for flow | — |
| 8 | $H \propto N^{2}$ | Affinity law for head | — |
| 9 | $P \propto N^{3}$ | Affinity law for power | — |
| 10 | $N_{s} = N \sqrt{Q} / H^{3/4}$ | Specific speed | — |
| 11 | $\eta = P_{fluid} / P_{shaft} \times 100\%$ | Pump efficiency | % |

> [!NOTE]
> In all the formulas above, use **\vert** notation if absolute value is required (e.g., $h_{s} = \vert \Delta z \vert$) to remain consistent with the safe LaTeX style.

## 2.12 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil and gas industry:** Centrifugal pumps transport crude oil over hundreds of kilometres of pipeline.
- **Water treatment plants:** High-pressure pumps supply reverse osmosis membranes.
- **Chemical reactors:** Pumps deliver precise flow rates of reactants to maintain stoichiometry.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** Positive displacement pumps handle sterile, shear-sensitive fluids.
- **HVAC systems:** Circulator pumps maintain chilled water flow in air-conditioning loops.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Pump Power Equation

The derivation begins from the **mechanical energy balance** for an incompressible fluid in a pump. The energy added by the pump per unit mass of fluid equals the change in total mechanical energy of the fluid between suction (point 1) and discharge (point 2).

**Starting point — Extended Bernoulli Equation with pump work:**

$$\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} + H_{pump} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + z_{2} + h_{f}$$

Rearranging to solve for the pump head $H_{pump}$:

$$H_{pump} = \left(\frac{P_{2} - P_{1}}{\rho g}\right) + \left(\frac{v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2}}{2g}\right) + (z_{2} - z_{1}) + h_{f}$$

**Step 1: Mass flow rate and its relation to volumetric flow rate.**

The mass flow rate of fluid through the pump is $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ in kg/s.

**Step 2: Power delivered to the fluid (water power).**

Power is the rate of energy transfer. Since the pump adds $H_{pump}$ metres of head per unit weight of fluid, the energy added per unit mass is $g H_{pump}$ (J/kg). Therefore, the power transferred to the fluid is:

$$P_{fluid} = \dot{m} \cdot g \cdot H_{pump} = \rho Q \cdot g \cdot H_{pump}$$

**Step 3: Inclusion of pump efficiency.**

The actual shaft power required exceeds the fluid power due to mechanical, volumetric, and hydraulic losses inside the pump. The overall efficiency $\eta$ is defined as:

$$\eta_{pump} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{P_{shaft}}$$

Therefore, the brake power that the motor must deliver to the pump shaft is:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\eta_{pump}} = \dfrac{\rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{pump}}{\eta_{pump}}$$

**Step 4: Final compact form (in SI units).**

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta} \quad [\text{Watts}]$$

For frequent KTU-style numericals, the power is often required in kW:

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000 \, \eta} \quad [\text{kW}]$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of NPSH_available

Consider a fluid reservoir (point 0) at atmospheric pressure, connected by a suction pipe to a pump suction flange (point 1). Apply the extended Bernoulli equation between (0) and (1):

$$\frac{P_{0}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{0}^{2}}{2g} + z_{0} = \frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} + z_{1} + h_{f,0 \to 1}$$

The reservoir surface is large, so $v_{0} \approx 0$. Set $z_{1}$ as the datum so that $z_{0} - z_{1} = h_{s}$ (positive if the reservoir is above the pump, negative if below).

Rearranging for the absolute pressure at the pump suction $P_{1}$:

$$P_{1} = P_{0} + \rho g (z_{0} - z_{1}) - \rho g \cdot h_{f,0 \to 1} - \frac{\rho v_{1}^{2}}{2}$$

Substituting $P_{0} = P_{atm}$ and $z_{0} - z_{1} = h_{s}$:

$$P_{1} = P_{atm} + \rho g h_{s} - \rho g h_{f} - \frac{\rho v_{1}^{2}}{2}$$

The **NPSH_available** is defined as the total head at the pump suction **in excess of the vapour pressure head** of the fluid:

$$NPSH_{A} = \frac{P_{1} - P_{v}}{\rho g}$$

Substituting the expression for $P_{1}$:

$$NPSH_{A} = \frac{P_{atm} - P_{v}}{\rho g} + h_{s} - h_{f} - \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g}$$

The velocity-head term $\dfrac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g}$ is often retained as a separate term or grouped with $h_{f}$ for the total suction-line loss. The KTU compact form used in numerical problems is:

$$NPSH_{A} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_{s} - \frac{P_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f,suction}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **± sign** before $h_{s}$ is a critical KTU evaluation point: **(+)** if the source reservoir is **above** the pump centreline (flooded suction), and **(−)** if the source is **below** the pump (suction lift).

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Affinity Laws

Consider a centrifugal pump operating at two different rotational speeds, $N_{1}$ and $N_{2}$, with geometrically similar impellers.

**Step 1: Velocity triangles.**

For geometric similarity, the fluid velocity at any point on the impeller is proportional to the impeller tip speed $u = \pi D N$. Hence:

$$v \propto D \cdot N$$

**Step 2: Volumetric flow rate.**

Volumetric flow rate is the product of the impeller exit area and the meridional velocity:

$$Q \propto D^{2} \cdot N$$

For geometrically similar pumps, $D$ is constant, so:

$$\dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$$

**Step 3: Head developed.**

Head is proportional to the square of fluid velocity (from Euler's turbomachinery equation):

$$H \propto v^{2} \propto D^{2} N^{2}$$

Hence:

$$\dfrac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 4: Power consumption.**

Power is proportional to $\rho g Q H$:

$$P \propto Q \cdot H \propto N \cdot N^{2} = N^{3}$$

Hence:

$$\dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{3}$$

These three relationships constitute the **Affinity Laws** of centrifugal pumps, a **high-yield KTU topic**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**
> A centrifugal pump delivers **15 L/s** of water against a total head of **25 m**. The pump efficiency is **70%**. Calculate:
> (a) Power delivered to the fluid.
> (b) Shaft power required.
> (c) Specific speed if the pump runs at **1450 rpm**.

**Given:**

$$Q = 15 \, \text{L/s} = \dfrac{15}{1000} = 0.015 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$H = 25 \, \text{m}, \quad \eta = 0.70, \quad \rho = 1000 \, \text{kg}/\text{m}^{3}, \quad g = 9.81 \, \text{m}/\text{s}^{2}, \quad N = 1450 \, \text{rpm}$$

**Part (a) — Power delivered to fluid:**

$$P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.015 \times 25$$

$$P_{fluid} = 3678.75 \, \text{W} \approx 3.68 \, \text{kW}$$

**Part (b) — Shaft power required:**

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\eta} = \dfrac{3678.75}{0.70} = 5255.36 \, \text{W} \approx 5.26 \, \text{kW}$$

**Part (c) — Specific speed:**

$$N_{s} = \dfrac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}} = \dfrac{1450 \times \sqrt{0.015}}{(25)^{3/4}}$$

Calculate the numerator:

$$\sqrt{0.015} = 0.12247$$

$$1450 \times 0.12247 = 177.59$$

Calculate the denominator:

$$(25)^{3/4} = (25)^{0.75} = 11.180$$

Therefore:

$$N_{s} = \dfrac{177.59}{11.180} = 15.88 \approx 15.9$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Verdict:** A value of $N_{s} \approx 16$ confirms this is a **radial flow centrifugal pump**, as $N_{s} < 50$ (the typical cutoff for radial flow geometry). Always state the **physical interpretation** of the specific speed result for **full marks**.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Influenced By | Affinity Law |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flow rate | Q | Impeller speed, diameter | $Q \propto ND^{3}$ |
| Head | H | Impeller speed, diameter | $H \propto N^{2}D^{2}$ |
| Power | P | Impeller speed, diameter | $P \propto N^{3}D^{5}$ |
| NPSH | NPSH | Suction conditions, fluid | Independent of N |
| Efficiency | η | Design, operating point | Approximately constant for small ΔN |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pump in a Flow System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture of a pumping device integrated within a typical flow system**, mapping the energy transfer pathways and feedback loops from suction to discharge.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[RESERVOIR SOURCE] --> B[SUCTION PIPE]
    B --> C[STRAINER / FOOT VALVE]
    C --> D[PUMP SUCTION FLANGE]
    D --> E[IMPELLER ENERGY ADDITION]
    E --> F[VOLUTE CASING KINETIC TO PRESSURE]
    F --> G[DISCHARGE FLANGE]
    G --> H[NON-RETURN VALVE]
    H --> I[DELIVERY PIPELINE]
    I --> J[CONTROL VALVE]
    J --> K[DESTINATION RESERVOIR]
    E --> L[MOTOR DRIVE SHAFT]
    L --> M[ELECTRICAL SUPPLY]
    K --> N[SYSTEM HEAD CURVE]
    F --> O[PUMP CHARACTERISTIC CURVE]
    N --> P[OPERATING POINT INTERSECTION]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[MONITOR Q AND H]

    classDef source fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#b30000,color:#000
    classDef pump fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef pipe fill:#e6f5d0,stroke:#336600,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#996600,color:#000
    classDef feedback fill:#e0d0ff,stroke:#330099,color:#000

    class A,K source
    class E,F,L,M pump
    class B,C,D,G,H,I,J pipe
    class N,O,P feedback
    class Q control
```

## 4.2 Operating Point — Mermaid State Diagram

The following state diagram clarifies how a pump transitions between operating conditions when the system curve changes.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SteadyOperation
    SteadyOperation --> StableOp: Pump on, Valve open
    StableOp --> Cavitation: NPSHA falls below NPSHR
    StableOp --> Overload: Q exceeds design maximum
    StableOp --> ReducedFlow: Partial valve closure
    Cavitation --> Shutdown: Severe damage detected
    Overload --> Shutdown: Motor trips on thermal overload
    ReducedFlow --> StableOp: Valve reopened
    Shutdown --> Maintenance
    Maintenance --> SteadyOperation
```

## 4.3 Pump Selection Decision Flow (Mermaid)

This flowchart represents the **logical sequence of pump selection** — a common **Part-B (14-mark)** KTU question theme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Start: System Requirements] --> S2{Determine Total Head}
    S2 --> S3[Calculate Static Head delta z]
    S2 --> S4[Calculate Friction Head hf]
    S2 --> S5[Calculate Pressure Head delta P by rho g]
    S3 --> S6[Sum all head components]
    S4 --> S6
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Plot System Head Curve Hsys versus Q]
    S7 --> S8{Calculate NPSH available}
    S8 --> S9{NPSHA greater than NPSHR question}
    S9 -->|Yes| S10[Select Centrifugal Pump]
    S9 -->|No| S11[Use Positive Displacement Pump]
    S10 --> S12[Match Pump Curve to System Curve]
    S11 --> S12
    S12 --> S13[Verify Operating Point Efficiency]
    S13 --> S14[Final Selection and Specification]

    classDef startNode fill:#d4f1f4,stroke:#005f73,color:#000
    classDef calcNode fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#7c6f00,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#ffc8dd,stroke:#800f4f,color:#000
    classDef endNode fill:#caffbf,stroke:#2d6a4f,color:#000

    class S1,S14 startNode
    class S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S13 calcNode
    class S2,S8,S9 decisionNode
    class S10,S11,S12 endNode
```

## 4.4 Explanation of the Block Diagram

- The **suction side** (red zone: Reservoir → Strainer → Pump suction flange) supplies low-energy fluid.
- The **pump block** (blue zone: Impeller → Volute → Shaft) is the energy-addition stage.
- The **discharge side** (green zone: NRV → Pipeline → Control valve → Destination) is the high-energy delivery side.
- The **feedback block** (purple zone: System curve + Pump curve → Operating point) represents the design-time analysis.
- The **control block** (yellow zone: Monitoring) represents real-time operational data.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU 2024]**
**Define the term "Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)" and state its significance in pump operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
NPSH is the total head available at the pump suction above the vapour pressure head of the liquid being pumped. It is calculated as:

$$NPSH_{A} = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} \pm h_{s} - \frac{P_{v}}{\rho g} - h_{f,suction}$$

**Significance:** For cavitation-free operation, the available NPSH must always exceed the required NPSH (specified by the manufacturer), i.e., $NPSH_{A} > NPSH_{R}$. If this condition is violated, vapour bubbles form at the impeller eye, collapse violently, causing pitting, vibration, noise, and severe mechanical damage to the pump internals.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Module 2]**
**State and explain the Affinity Laws for centrifugal pumps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The affinity laws relate the performance of a geometrically similar centrifugal pump at two different rotational speeds $N_{1}$ and $N_{2}$:

**Law 1 — Flow rate:** Flow rate is directly proportional to rotational speed.

$$\frac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}$$

**Law 2 — Head:** Developed head is proportional to the square of rotational speed.

$$\frac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{2}$$

**Law 3 — Power:** Power consumption is proportional to the cube of rotational speed.

$$\frac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\frac{N_{2}}{N_{1}}\right)^{3}$$

These laws are widely used to predict pump performance after speed changes and to scale up/down pump designs.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks Total)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 — Set A]**

A centrifugal pump is used to lift water from a lower reservoir to an elevated overhead tank. The suction line is **6 m** long, **75 mm** diameter, with a friction factor of **0.025**. The delivery line is **40 m** long, **50 mm** diameter, with a friction factor of **0.030**. The static lift is **15 m**. Minor losses (valves, fittings, entrance, exit) account for an equivalent of **5 m** of head. The pump delivers **600 L/min** of water at an efficiency of **65%**. Calculate:

**(a)** The friction head losses in suction and delivery lines, and the total system head. (7 marks)

**(b)** The power required to drive the pump. State whether the pump is safe from cavitation if NPSHR is 3 m. (7 marks)

**Given data:**

$$Q = 600 \, \text{L/min} = \dfrac{600}{60 \times 1000} = 0.01 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$D_{suc} = 0.075 \, \text{m}, \quad L_{suc} = 6 \, \text{m}, \quad f_{suc} = 0.025$$

$$D_{del} = 0.050 \, \text{m}, \quad L_{del} = 40 \, \text{m}, \quad f_{del} = 0.030$$

$$H_{static} = 15 \, \text{m}, \quad h_{minor} = 5 \, \text{m}, \quad \eta = 0.65$$

---

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate velocities in suction and delivery pipes.**

$$v_{suc} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{suc}} = \dfrac{0.01}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^{2}} = \dfrac{0.01}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.264 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$v_{del} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{del}} = \dfrac{0.01}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.050)^{2}} = \dfrac{0.01}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 5.093 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Friction head loss in suction line (Darcy–Weisbach).**

$$h_{f,suc} = \dfrac{4 f L v^{2}}{2 g D} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.025 \times 6 \times (2.264)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.075}$$

$$h_{f,suc} = \dfrac{0.6 \times 5.125}{1.4715} = \dfrac{3.075}{1.4715} = 2.090 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3: Friction head loss in delivery line.**

$$h_{f,del} = \dfrac{4 f L v^{2}}{2 g D} = \dfrac{4 \times 0.030 \times 40 \times (5.093)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.050}$$

$$h_{f,del} = \dfrac{4.8 \times 25.939}{0.981} = \dfrac{124.51}{0.981} = 126.92 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Total system head.**

$$H_{system} = H_{static} + h_{f,suc} + h_{f,del} + h_{minor}$$

$$H_{system} = 15 + 2.09 + 126.92 + 5 = 149.01 \, \text{m}$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Power delivered to the fluid.**

$$P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.01 \times 149.01$$

$$P_{fluid} = 14617.9 \, \text{W} \approx 14.62 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 2: Shaft (brake) power required.**

$$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{fluid}}{\eta} = \dfrac{14617.9}{0.65} = 22489 \, \text{W} \approx 22.49 \, \text{kW}$$

**Step 3: NPSH analysis.**

Assuming the source reservoir is just below the pump suction (suction lift, so $h_{s}$ is negligible or negative) and neglecting the vapour pressure at standard temperature (water at 25 °C, $P_{v} = 3169 \, \text{Pa}$):

$$NPSH_{A} = \dfrac{101325}{1000 \times 9.81} - 0 - \dfrac{3169}{1000 \times 9.81} - 2.09$$

$$NPSH_{A} = 10.33 - 0.323 - 2.09 = 7.92 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 4: Cavitation check.**

Since $NPSH_{A} = 7.92 \, \text{m} > NPSH_{R} = 3 \, \text{m}$, the pump is **SAFE from cavitation**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2 — Set B]**

A centrifugal pump with an impeller diameter of **300 mm** running at **2900 rpm** delivers **20 L/s** of water against a head of **30 m** with an efficiency of **75%**. The impeller is now trimmed (machined) to a diameter of **280 mm**. Determine:

**(a)** The new discharge, head, and power consumed. (7 marks)

**(b)** Verify the affinity law predictions by computing the new specific speed and commenting on the impeller geometry. (7 marks)

**Given data:**

$$D_{1} = 300 \, \text{mm} = 0.300 \, \text{m}, \quad N_{1} = 2900 \, \text{rpm}$$

$$Q_{1} = 20 \, \text{L/s} = 0.020 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}, \quad H_{1} = 30 \, \text{m}, \quad \eta_{1} = 0.75$$

$$D_{2} = 280 \, \text{mm} = 0.280 \, \text{m}, \quad N_{2} = N_{1} = 2900 \, \text{rpm} \quad (\text{constant speed, trimmed impeller})$$

---

**Part (a) — Solution (7 marks):**

When the impeller is **trimmed at constant speed**, the affinity laws for impeller diameter apply:

$$\dfrac{Q_{2}}{Q_{1}} = \dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}, \quad \dfrac{H_{2}}{H_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{2}, \quad \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = \left(\dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{3}$$

**Step 1: New flow rate.**

$$Q_{2} = Q_{1} \times \dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}} = 0.020 \times \dfrac{0.280}{0.300} = 0.020 \times 0.9333 = 0.01867 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{s}$$

$$Q_{2} = 18.67 \, \text{L/s}$$

**Step 2: New head.**

$$H_{2} = H_{1} \times \left(\dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{2} = 30 \times (0.9333)^{2} = 30 \times 0.8711 = 26.13 \, \text{m}$$

**Step 3: New power.**

First, calculate the original shaft power:

$$P_{1} = \dfrac{\rho g Q_{1} H_{1}}{\eta_{1}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.020 \times 30}{0.75} = 7848 \, \text{W} \approx 7.85 \, \text{kW}$$

Now apply the cube law for power:

$$P_{2} = P_{1} \times \left(\dfrac{D_{2}}{D_{1}}\right)^{3} = 7.85 \times (0.9333)^{3} = 7.85 \times 0.8128 = 6.38 \, \text{kW}$$

---

**Part (b) — Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute original specific speed.**

$$N_{s,1} = \dfrac{N_{1} \sqrt{Q_{1}}}{H_{1}^{3/4}}$$

$$\sqrt{Q_{1}} = \sqrt{0.020} = 0.1414$$

$$H_{1}^{3/4} = (30)^{0.75} = 12.82$$

$$N_{s,1} = \dfrac{2900 \times 0.1414}{12.82} = \dfrac{410.16}{12.82} = 32.0$$

**Step 2: Compute new specific speed.**

$$\sqrt{Q_{2}} = \sqrt{0.01867} = 0.1366$$

$$H_{2}^{3/4} = (26.13)^{0.75} = 11.58$$

$$N_{s,2} = \dfrac{2900 \times 0.1366}{11.58} = \dfrac{396.21}{11.58} = 34.22$$

**Step 3: Impeller geometry comment.**

Both specific speeds ($N_{s,1} = 32$ and $N_{s,2} = 34.2$) are well below the cutoff of **50** for radial-flow pumps. The impeller is therefore of **radial-flow centrifugal type**. The slight increase in specific speed after trimming indicates a marginal shift in operating character, but the geometry remains radial flow. The pump continues to deliver adequate head and flow with reduced power consumption.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert **L/s to m³/s** (divide by 1000) and **rpm to rad/s** (multiply by $2\pi/60$ — not required here, but be aware).
> 2. **Sign convention for $h_{s}$:** Students frequently mark $h_{s}$ positive when it should be negative for suction lift, leading to **incorrect NPSH calculations** and an **incorrect cavitation verdict**.
> 3. **Confusion between shaft power and motor power:** KTU questions often ask for "motor power" — you must additionally include motor efficiency, not just pump efficiency. If only pump efficiency is given, label the answer as **shaft power**, not motor power.
> 4. **Affinity law mix-up:** Some students incorrectly apply the **speed-based** affinity law for a **diameter-trimmed** pump. Always use $Q \propto D$, $H \propto D^{2}$, $P \propto D^{3}$ when speed is constant.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the physical interpretation of $N_s$:** A computed $N_s$ value without comment on impeller geometry is **incomplete** — KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for this omission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pump purpose:** To add mechanical energy to a fluid to overcome **static head, friction head, and discharge pressure**.
- **Two main classes:** **Dynamic pumps** (centrifugal — flow varies with pressure) and **Positive Displacement pumps** (constant flow).
- **Pump H–Q curve:** Head decreases as flow increases for centrifugal pumps.
- **System H–Q curve:** Static head (constant) + friction head (parabolic in Q).
- **Operating point:** Intersection of pump and system curves; determines the actual flow.
- **NPSH criterion:** $NPSH_{A} > NPSH_{R}$ for cavitation-free operation.
- **Power to fluid:** $P_{fluid} = \rho g Q H$.
- **Shaft power:** $P_{shaft} = \rho g Q H / \eta$.
- **Affinity laws:** $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^{2}$, $P \propto N^{3}$ (for speed change) and $Q \propto D$, $H \propto D^{2}$, $P \propto D^{3}$ (for diameter change at constant speed).
- **Specific speed:** $N_{s} = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$; low $N_{s}$ → radial, medium → mixed, high → axial.
- **Pumps in series** add heads (same flow); **pumps in parallel** add flows (same head).
- **Cavitation consequences:** pitting, vibration, efficiency loss, mechanical damage.
- **Default KTU fluid:** Water at standard conditions ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²).
- **Always convert units:** L/s → m³/s, mm → m, kW → W, before substituting.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** In a 14-mark problem, allocate ~3 minutes for problem interpretation, ~6 minutes for numerical calculation, and ~2 minutes for stating the **physical interpretation** of your answer. The interpretation step (e.g., "this is a radial flow pump", "the pump is cavitation-safe") is what separates a 10-mark answer from a 14-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: How to calculate the pressure drop in the selected flow system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** How to calculate the pressure drop in the selected flow system

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pressure Drop (ΔP)** is the net decrease in static pressure of a fluid as it flows through a conduit, pipe, valve, fitting, or any flow system, arising from frictional resistance at the wall (viscous shear) and from form/geometric disturbances (sudden contractions, expansions, bends, valves, etc.).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 \quad [\text{Pa} \;\; \text{or} \;\; \text{N/m}^2]$$

where:
- **P₁** = upstream static pressure [Pa]
- **P₂** = downstream static pressure [Pa]

In differential form, the **Mechanical Energy Balance** (extended Bernoulli equation) for an incompressible fluid between two points (1) and (2) in a flow system is:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Rearranging, the **total pressure drop** between two points is:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = (P_1 - P_2) = \rho g (z_2 - z_1) + \frac{\rho}{2}(v_2^2 - v_1^2) + \rho g \, h_L}$$

Here, the term **h_L** represents the **total head loss [m]**, which is the cumulative energy lost per unit weight of fluid due to friction and fittings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Units:**
> - Pressure Drop: **Pa (N/m²)** or **bar (1 bar = 10⁵ Pa)** or **psi (1 psi = 6894.76 Pa)**
> - Head Loss: **metres of fluid (m)**
> - Density: **kg/m³**
> - Viscosity: **Pa·s** (or **cP**, 1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **watering your garden with a long hose** connected to a tap. Have you noticed that the water gushes out powerfully when the hose is short, but the jet weakens significantly when you attach a **longer hose or a narrower nozzle**? This everyday observation is precisely the phenomenon of **pressure drop**.

Think of a fluid flowing through a pipe like **a crowd of people walking through a long, narrow corridor**:
- **Friction at the pipe wall** is like the people brushing against the corridor walls — they lose momentum (energy) in the process.
- **Bends, valves, and fittings** are like **doorways, pillars, or staircases** in the corridor — every obstacle forces the crowd to lose more energy as they navigate around it.
- The **further they travel** and the **narrower the corridor**, the **more energy they lose**, and the **slower/tireder** they become at the exit.

So, the **pressure drop is the "energy tax"** that the fluid must pay to traverse the system, paid in the currency of mechanical energy.

### 1.3 Classification of Pressure Losses

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Total head loss in a flow system is split into two major categories:
> 1. **Major Losses (h_f)** — due to **friction along straight pipe length**.
> 2. **Minor Losses (h_m)** — due to **fittings, valves, bends, contractions, expansions, etc.**

The total head loss is:

$$h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Major Loss — Darcy–Weisbach Equation (Core Equation for Pipe Friction)

The **Darcy–Weisbach equation** is the universal, theoretically rigorous and experimentally validated formula for computing frictional pressure drop in a fully developed, steady, incompressible pipe flow.

$$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$

In terms of **pressure drop**, the most exam-relevant form is:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}}$$

where:
- **f** = Darcy friction factor [dimensionless]
- **L** = length of pipe [m]
- **D** = internal diameter of pipe [m]
- **v** = average flow velocity [m/s]
- **ρ** = fluid density [kg/m³]
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²

**Why is the Darcy–Weisbach equation universal?**
Because the **friction factor f** accounts for both the **flow regime (laminar vs turbulent)** and the **pipe roughness**, making the same equation valid across all Reynolds numbers and pipe materials.

### 2.2 Friction Factor (f) — Determination Methods

The friction factor depends on:
- **Reynolds Number (Re)** = ρvD/μ [dimensionless]
- **Relative Roughness (ε/D)** [dimensionless]

#### Case A: Laminar Flow (Re < 2100)

$$f = \frac{64}{Re}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For laminar flow, **f depends ONLY on Re**, not on roughness. This is derived analytically from the Hagen–Poiseuille parabolic velocity profile.

#### Case B: Turbulent Flow (Re > 4000)

Three methods are universally used in KTU examinations:

**(i) Colebrook–White Equation (Implicit — implicit in f):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right)$$

**(ii) Haaland Equation (Explicit Approximation):**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{Re}\right]$$

**(iii) Moody Chart (Graphical Method):** Used in exams where a chart/graph is supplied.

**For fully rough turbulent flow** (Re → ∞), the friction factor becomes constant (independent of Re):

$$f \approx \frac{1}{\left[-2 \log_{10}\left(\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7}\right)\right]^2}$$

### 2.3 Minor Losses (Fittings, Valves, Bends)

Minor losses are expressed using a **loss coefficient (K)**:

$$h_m = K \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta P_m = K \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

Common values (must memorize for KTU):

| Fitting Type | Typical K Value |
|---|---|
| 90° standard elbow | 0.9 |
| 45° elbow | 0.4 |
| Tee (line flow) | 0.6 |
| Tee (branch flow) | 1.8 |
| Fully open globe valve | 10.0 |
| Fully open gate valve | 0.17 |
| Swing check valve | 2.0 |
| Sudden contraction | 0.5 (approx) |
| Sudden expansion | (1 − A₁/A₂)² |
| Pipe exit (to tank) | 1.0 |
| Pipe entrance (from tank) | 0.5 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equivalent Length Method (L_e/D) — Highly Tested in KTU:**
> Minor losses can be lumped into an equivalent pipe length:
> $$h_m = f \frac{L_e}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}$$
> where **L_e = K·D/f**. This allows the entire system to be treated as a single straight pipe of total length (L + ΣL_e).

### 2.4 Total Pressure Drop — Master Equation

For a complete flow system between inlet (1) and outlet (2):

$$\Delta P_{total} = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{\rho v^2}{2} + \sum K \frac{\rho v^2}{2} + \Delta P_{elevation} + \Delta P_{velocity}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a **constant-diameter horizontal pipe** with constant velocity, the last two terms vanish, giving:
> $$\Delta P = \left(f \frac{L}{D} + \sum K\right) \frac{\rho v^2}{2}$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Application | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P_f = f(L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$ | Major (friction) loss in pipe | Pa |
| 2 | $\Delta P_m = K(\rho v^2/2)$ | Minor loss through fitting | Pa |
| 3 | $h_L = (f L/D + \sum K)(v^2/2g)$ | Total head loss | m |
| 4 | $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ | Flow regime check | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f = 64/Re$ | Laminar friction factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $1/\sqrt{f} = -2\log_{10}[\varepsilon/D \cdot 3.7^{-1} + 2.51/(Re\sqrt{f})]$ | Turbulent f (Colebrook) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $L_e = K D / f$ | Equivalent length of fitting | m |
| 8 | $\Delta P = 32 \mu L v / D^2$ | Hagen–Poiseuille (laminar) | Pa |
| 9 | $\dot{V} = A v = (\pi D^2/4) v$ | Volumetric flow rate | m³/s |
| 10 | $v = \dot{V}/A$ | Average velocity | m/s |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Process Industry:** Sizing of pumps, compressors, and pipelines.
- **Water Distribution Networks:** Determining residual pressure at consumer end.
- **Oil & Gas Pipelines:** Calculating pumping station spacing.
- **HVAC Systems:** Duct pressure drop for fan selection.
- **Pharmaceutical & Food Industry:** Sterile piping (sanitary fittings) design.
- **Microfluidics & Lab-on-chip:** Miniaturized channel pressure analysis.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Darcy–Weisbach Equation from Energy Balance

**Starting Point:** Mechanical energy balance between two points 1 and 2 in a horizontal pipe of constant cross-section:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} + \frac{v_1^2}{2} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + \frac{v_2^2}{2} + e_f$$

**Step 1:** Since the pipe is constant-diameter, by continuity: A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ → v₁ = v₂ = v (for incompressible flow).

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy terms cancel:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho} = \frac{P_2}{\rho} + e_f$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the pressure drop equals the energy lost per unit volume:

$$\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = \rho \, e_f$$

**Step 4:** The frictional energy loss per unit mass, derived from dimensional analysis and experimental validation, is:

$$e_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting into the pressure drop expression:

$$\boxed{\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}}$$

**Engineering Logic:** The factor (L/D) captures the geometric dependence (longer/narrower → more loss), the factor (ρv²/2) captures the kinetic energy density (faster flow → more loss), and f encapsulates the friction mechanism (viscous + turbulent + roughness).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow)

For steady, fully developed, laminar flow in a horizontal circular pipe, the Navier–Stokes equation reduces to:

$$0 = -\frac{dP}{dx} + \mu \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\left(r \frac{dv_z}{dr}\right)\right]$$

**Step 1:** Integrate twice with respect to r (treating dP/dx as constant for fully developed flow):

$$v_z(r) = -\frac{1}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}\left(R^2 - r^2\right)$$

**Step 2:** Apply the no-slip boundary condition v_z(R) = 0 (already satisfied). The velocity profile is **parabolic**, with maximum at the centreline (r = 0):

$$v_{max} = -\frac{R^2}{4\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the **average velocity** by integrating over the cross-section:

$$v = \frac{1}{\pi R^2}\int_0^R v_z(r)\,2\pi r\, dr$$

**Step 4:** Substituting and integrating:

$$v = -\frac{R^2}{8\mu}\frac{dP}{dx}$$

**Step 5:** Pressure drop over length L (with dP/dx = (P₂ − P₁)/L = −ΔP/L):

$$\Delta P = \frac{8 \mu L v}{R^2} = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$$

**Step 6:** Derive friction factor: Equate with Darcy–Weisbach form:

$$f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2}$$

Solving for f:

$$\boxed{f = \frac{64}{Re}}, \quad \text{where } Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$$

This is a **crucial cross-verification** that links laminar flow theory with the empirical Darcy–Weisbach form.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sudden Expansion Loss (Borda–Carnot Equation)

For a sudden expansion from area A₁ to A₂ (with v₂ < v₁), a momentum balance yields:

$$h_{exp} = \frac{(v_1 - v_2)^2}{2g}$$

Substituting continuity A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ → v₂ = v₁·(A₁/A₂):

$$h_{exp} = \frac{v_1^2}{2g}\left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

The K loss coefficient (with reference to v₁) is:

$$\boxed{K_{exp} = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the most common minor loss derivation asked in KTU. It comes from a momentum balance (not energy balance), making it a classic board question.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Board Style Numerical

**Problem:** Water at 25 °C flows through a horizontal commercial steel pipe of diameter **D = 50 mm** and length **L = 100 m** at a volumetric flow rate of **V̇ = 0.005 m³/s**. The pipe has **two 90° standard elbows** and **one fully open gate valve**. Calculate the total pressure drop.

Given data:
- ρ = 998 kg/m³
- μ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s (water at 25 °C)
- ε (commercial steel) = 0.046 mm
- K_elbow = 0.9, K_gate = 0.17

**Step 1: Calculate Average Velocity**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^2}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{\dot{V}}{A} = \frac{0.005}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.547 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Reynolds Number**

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{998 \times 2.547 \times 0.05}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$Re = \frac{127.10}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.428 \times 10^5$$

> Flow is **turbulent** (Re > 4000).

**Step 3: Calculate Relative Roughness**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.05} = 9.2 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Determine Friction Factor (Haaland Equation)**

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{9.2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.428 \times 10^5}\right]$$

Computing the first term inside the log:

$$\left(\frac{9.2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} = (2.486 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} = 1.225 \times 10^{-4}$$

Computing the second term:

$$\frac{6.9}{1.428 \times 10^5} = 4.832 \times 10^{-5}$$

Sum inside log = 1.225 × 10⁻⁴ + 0.4832 × 10⁻⁴ = 1.708 × 10⁻⁴

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}(1.708 \times 10^{-4}) = -1.8 \times (-3.768) = 6.782$$

$$\sqrt{f} = \frac{1}{6.782} = 0.1474 \quad \Rightarrow \quad f = 0.02174$$

**Step 5: Calculate Major Loss Pressure Drop**

$$\Delta P_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 0.02174 \times \frac{100}{0.05} \times \frac{998 \times (2.547)^2}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.02174 \times 2000 \times \frac{998 \times 6.487}{2}$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.02174 \times 2000 \times 3237.1 = 43.48 \times 3237.1$$

$$\Delta P_f = 140{,}747 \;\; \text{Pa} \approx 140.75 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 6: Calculate Minor Loss Pressure Drop**

$$\sum K = 2 \times 0.9 + 1 \times 0.17 = 1.97$$

$$\Delta P_m = \sum K \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2} = 1.97 \times 3237.1 = 6377 \;\; \text{Pa} \approx 6.38 \;\; \text{kPa}$$

**Step 7: Total Pressure Drop**

$$\Delta P_{total} = \Delta P_f + \Delta P_m = 140{,}747 + 6377 = 147{,}124 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{total} \approx 147.12 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The minor loss contributes only about **4.3%** of the total pressure drop in this long-pipe system. However, in **short pipelines with many fittings**, minor losses can dominate (sometimes > 50%).

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Pressure Drop Calculation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    StartA([Start: Given Flow System]) --> B[Identify Pipe Geometry: L, D, epsilon]
    B --> C[Identify Fluid Properties: rho, mu]
    C --> D[Identify Flow Rate: Vdot or v]
    D --> E[Calculate Reynolds Number: Re = rho v D / mu]
    E --> F{Re less than 2100?}
    F -- Yes Laminar --> G["f = 64 / Re"]
    F -- No --> H{Re greater than 4000?}
    H -- No Transitional --> I[Use Moody Chart or Interpolate]
    H -- Yes Turbulent --> J["Use Haaland or Colebrook for f"]
    G --> K[Calculate Major Loss: DeltaPf = f L D rho v squared / 2]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Identify All Fittings: elbows, valves, etc.]
    L --> M[Look up K values or calculate L equivalent]
    M --> N[Calculate Minor Loss: DeltaPm = Sum K rho v squared / 2]
    N --> O[Add Elevation and Velocity Changes if any]
    O --> P["Total DeltaP = DeltaPf + DeltaPm + DeltaPz + DeltaPv"]
    P --> Q([Output: Total Pressure Drop in Pa])
    style StartA fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000

```

flowchart LR

### 4.3 Decision Logic — When to Use Which Friction Factor Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Compute Re and epsilon by D] --> Lam{Re less than 2100?}
    Lam -- Yes --> FLam["f = 64 / Re"]
    Lam -- No --> Turb{Re greater than 4000?}
    Turb -- No --> Tran[Transition: Use Moody Chart Interpolation]
    Turb -- Yes --> Choice{Need Analytical Value?}
    Choice -- High Accuracy --> Cole["Colebrook-White Equation: Implicit in f, requires iteration"]
    Choice -- Quick Estimate --> Haal["Haaland Equation: Explicit, no iteration"]
    Choice -- Fully Rough --> Rough["f approximately equals 1 divided by -2 log10 epsilon by D over 3.7 all squared"]
    Cole --> Out[Apply Darcy-Weisbach: DeltaP equals f L by D rho v squared over 2]
    Haal --> Out
    Rough --> Out
    FLam --> Out
    Tran --> Out
    style Start fill:#E6E6FA,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    style Out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```
