## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**Q1. Define pressure drop in a flow system. List any two causes of pressure drop.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**
Pressure drop (ΔP) is the decrease in static pressure of a fluid between two points in a flow system, expressed in Pascals (Pa). It represents the mechanical energy dissipated per unit volume of fluid.
- **Cause 1:** **Major losses** — frictional resistance along the pipe wall (viscous shear and turbulent dissipation).
- **Cause 2:** **Minor losses** — flow disturbances caused by fittings, valves, sudden contractions/expansions, bends, and pipe entries/exits.

---

**Q2. Differentiate between major and minor losses in pipe flow. State the formula used for each.** [3 Marks]

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Major Losses | Minor Losses |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Pipe wall friction | Fittings, valves, bends |
| Formula | $h_f = f(L/D)(v^2/2g)$ | $h_m = K(v^2/2g)$ |
| Dominance | Long straight pipes | Systems with many fittings |
| Parameter | Depends on L, D, f, v | Depends on K and v |

> Friction factor **f** is dimensionless; **K** is also dimensionless and is determined experimentally for each fitting type.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) with Internal Choice

> **[KTU End Semester Examination — Module 2 Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the Darcy–Weisbach equation for pressure drop in a fully developed pipe flow. Explain the significance of each term. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A horizontal pipe of diameter 75 mm carries oil (ρ = 880 kg/m³, μ = 0.025 Pa·s) at an average velocity of 1.5 m/s over a length of 200 m. The pipe roughness is 0.05 mm. Calculate the pressure drop using (i) Darcy–Weisbach, and (ii) Hagen–Poiseuille. Comment on the discrepancy. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution to Q.A(a):**

Step 1: Write the mechanical energy balance between two sections 1 and 2 of a horizontal constant-diameter pipe:

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{v_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{v_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_f$$

Step 2: For horizontal pipe, z₁ = z₂. For constant diameter, v₁ = v₂ = v.

Step 3: Simplification yields:

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = h_f \quad\Rightarrow\quad \Delta P = \rho g h_f$$

Step 4: Substituting the empirical head-loss expression $h_f = f \frac{L}{D} \frac{v^2}{2g}$:

$$\boxed{\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^2}{2}}$$

**Significance of each term:**
- **f** → encodes flow regime + roughness
- **L/D** → geometric scaling (longer/narrower → higher ΔP)
- **ρv²/2** → dynamic pressure (kinetic energy density)

**Model Solution to Q.A(b):**

Step 1: Compute Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{880 \times 1.5 \times 0.075}{0.025} = 3960$$

Step 2: Since Re is between 2100 and 4000, the flow is in the **transition zone**. We will still apply both methods for comparison.

**(i) Darcy–Weisbach with Haaland friction factor:**

$$\frac{\varepsilon}{D} = \frac{0.05 \times 10^{-3}}{0.075} = 6.67 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{6.67 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{3960}\right]$$

$$= -1.8 \log_{10}\left[(1.803 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} + 1.742 \times 10^{-3}\right]$$

$$= -1.8 \log_{10}[8.05 \times 10^{-5} + 1.742 \times 10^{-3}] = -1.8 \log_{10}(1.823 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$= -1.8 \times (-2.739) = 4.930 \quad\Rightarrow\quad f = 0.04115$$

$$\Delta P_f = 0.04115 \times \frac{200}{0.075} \times \frac{880 \times (1.5)^2}{2} = 0.04115 \times 2666.7 \times 990 = 108{,}615 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{DW} \approx 108.6 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

**(ii) Hagen–Poiseuille (laminar assumption, for comparison only):**

$$\Delta P_{HP} = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2} = \frac{32 \times 0.025 \times 200 \times 1.5}{(0.075)^2} = \frac{240}{0.005625} = 42{,}667 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{HP} \approx 42.67 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

**Comment:** Hagen–Poiseuille underestimates ΔP because it assumes purely laminar flow, while the actual flow is in the transition zone where turbulent dissipation adds extra energy losses. Darcy–Weisbach is the **correct** choice for Re > 2100.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the loss coefficient **K** for sudden expansion of flow from area A₁ to A₂. Use this to find the pressure drop when a pipe carrying water at 3 m/s expands from 50 mm to 100 mm diameter. Take ρ = 1000 kg/m³. [7 Marks]

**(b)** A pipeline system consists of: 200 m of 100 mm diameter pipe (ε = 0.045 mm), three 90° elbows (K = 0.9 each), two gate valves (K = 0.15 each), and a sudden contraction from 150 mm to 100 mm. If water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, μ = 1 cP) flows at 30 m³/hr, calculate the total pressure drop. [7 Marks]

**Model Solution to Q.B(a):**

Step 1: Apply the linear momentum equation across a control volume spanning the sudden expansion:

$$P_1 A_2 - P_2 A_2 = \rho Q (v_1 - v_2)$$

Step 2: Substitute Q = A₂v₂ and divide by (ρA₂):

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = v_2(v_1 - v_2)$$

Step 3: Convert to head loss using $h_{exp} = (P_1 - P_2)/(\rho g)$:

$$h_{exp} = \frac{v_2(v_1 - v_2)}{g}$$

Step 4: Using continuity A₁v₁ = A₂v₂ → v₂ = v₁(A₁/A₂) = v₁(D₁/D₂)²:

$$h_{exp} = \frac{v_1(A_1/A_2)(v_1 - v_1 A_1/A_2)}{g} = \frac{v_1^2}{g} \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}\left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)$$

Step 5: Since $(1 - A_1/A_2) = (A_2 - A_1)/A_2$:

$$h_{exp} = \frac{v_1^2}{2g}\left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

$$\boxed{K_{exp} = \left(1 - \frac{D_1^2}{D_2^2}\right)^2}$$

Step 6: Numerical evaluation:

$$K_{exp} = \left(1 - \left(\frac{50}{100}\right)^2\right)^2 = (1 - 0.25)^2 = (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$$

$$\Delta P = K \cdot \frac{\rho v_1^2}{2} = 0.5625 \times \frac{1000 \times 9}{2} = 2531.25 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{exp} \approx 2.53 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

**Model Solution to Q.B(b):**

Step 1: Convert flow rate:

$$\dot{V} = 30 \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{hr} = \frac{30}{3600} = 8.333 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

Step 2: Calculate velocity in 100 mm pipe:

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.1)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m}^2$$

$$v = \frac{8.333 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.061 \;\; \text{m/s}$$

Step 3: Reynolds number:

$$Re = \frac{1000 \times 1.061 \times 0.1}{10^{-3}} = 1.061 \times 10^5$$

> Flow is turbulent.

Step 4: Friction factor (Haaland, ε/D = 0.045 × 10⁻³ / 0.1 = 4.5 × 10⁻⁴):

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -1.8 \log_{10}\left[\left(\frac{4.5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7}\right)^{1.11} + \frac{6.9}{1.061 \times 10^5}\right]$$

$$= -1.8 \log_{10}[(1.216 \times 10^{-4})^{1.11} + 6.5 \times 10^{-5}]$$

$$= -1.8 \log_{10}[5.31 \times 10^{-5} + 6.5 \times 10^{-5}] = -1.8 \log_{10}(1.181 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$= -1.8 \times (-3.928) = 7.070 \quad\Rightarrow\quad f = 0.02000$$

Step 5: Major loss:

$$\Delta P_f = 0.02000 \times \frac{200}{0.1} \times \frac{1000 \times (1.061)^2}{2} = 0.02000 \times 2000 \times 562.86 = 22{,}514 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

Step 6: Minor losses:

$$\sum K = 3(0.9) + 2(0.15) + K_{contract}$$

For sudden contraction from 150 mm to 100 mm (A₁/A₂ = (150/100)² = 2.25), approximate K_contract ≈ 0.5 (or use K = 0.5(1 − A₂/A₁) ≈ 0.28). Taking K = 0.5:

$$\sum K = 2.7 + 0.3 + 0.5 = 3.5$$

$$\Delta P_m = 3.5 \times 562.86 = 1970 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

Step 7: Total pressure drop:

$$\Delta P_{total} = 22{,}514 + 1970 = 24{,}484 \;\; \text{Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P_{total} \approx 24.48 \;\; \text{kPa}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm with m in L, D, ε — ALWAYS convert to **metres** before substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting to Square D in Area:** Velocity = V̇/A, where A = πD²/4. Many students use πD/4 incorrectly.
> 3. **Misapplying Hagen–Poiseuille:** It is **valid only for Re < 2100**. Using it for turbulent flow gives a gross underestimate.
> 4. **Reference Velocity for K:** Some K values are referenced to **upstream velocity (v₁)** and others to **downstream (v₂)**. For sudden expansion, K is referenced to v₁.
> 5. **Sum of K includes only fittings,** not straight pipe. Adding extra K_D for the pipe is double-counting.
> 6. **Sign of Pressure Drop:** ΔP is always **positive** by convention (P₁ − P₂ > 0). The downstream pressure is always lower.
> 7. **Wrong friction factor formula:** The **64/Re formula is ONLY for laminar Darcy friction factor**, not Fanning. Fanning friction factor = 16/Re; Darcy = 4 × Fanning.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** ΔP = P₁ − P₂; energy dissipated per unit volume of fluid [Pa].
- **Universal Equation:** $\Delta P_f = f(L/D)(\rho v^2/2)$ — **Darcy–Weisbach**.
- **Friction Factor f:**
  - Laminar (Re < 2100): **f = 64/Re**
  - Turbulent (Re > 4000): **Haaland (explicit)** or **Colebrook (implicit)** or **Moody chart (graphical)**
- **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ — first thing to compute in EVERY problem.
- **Relative Roughness:** $\varepsilon/D$ — required to enter Moody chart or Haaland equation.
- **Minor Losses:** $\Delta P_m = K(\rho v^2/2)$; **Sudden Expansion** is the most-tested minor loss.
- **Equivalent Length Method:** $L_e = KD/f$ — converts fittings to equivalent straight pipe.
- **Total Head Loss:** $h_L = h_f + \sum h_m$ → in pressure units: $\Delta P = \rho g h_L$.
- **Hagen–Poiseuille (laminar shortcut):** $\Delta P = 32 \mu L v / D^2$ — derive only when Re < 2100.
- **Units Check (MANDATORY):** L, D, ε in **metres**; v in **m/s**; ρ in **kg/m³**; μ in **Pa·s**.
- **Step-by-Step Solver Protocol:** Identify geometry → Compute v → Compute Re → Select f → Compute ΔPf → Add ΔPm → Add elevation/velocity changes (if any) → State final ΔP in kPa or bar.
- **Engineering Judgment:** For long straight pipes, minor losses are < 10% of total. For complex systems with many fittings, minor losses can exceed 50% of the total.
- **Cross-Verification Trick:** If f = 64/Re gives same result as Darcy–Weisbach, then flow is laminar and Hagen–Poiseuille is also valid — a powerful self-check.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The use of friction factor in calculating the pressure drop induced by various pipe fitting and auxiliaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** The use of friction factor in calculating the pressure drop induced by various pipe fitting and auxiliaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of fluid flow in process piping networks, the **friction factor** (*f*) is a dimensionless quantity that characterizes the resistance to flow offered by a pipe wall under turbulent or laminar conditions. When applied to **pipe fittings and auxiliaries** (such as elbows, tees, valves, sudden expansions, contractions, strainers, and orifice plates), the friction factor is indirectly used through two universally accepted engineering methods to compute the **minor (or form) loss / pressure drop**:

1. **Equivalent Length Method (Lₑ / D method):** The fitting is conceptually replaced by a hypothetical length of straight pipe of the same diameter that would produce an identical frictional pressure drop at the same flow rate.
2. **Resistance Coefficient Method (K-value method):** The fitting is assigned a dimensionless loss coefficient *K* (sometimes called the *Cᵥ* equivalent or *2-K* parameter for valves) that quantifies the head loss in terms of the velocity head.

The **total pressure drop** in a piping system is the sum of the **major (frictional) loss** in the straight pipes and the **aggregate minor losses** across all fittings and auxiliaries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 Anchor Statement:**
> For an *incompressible fluid* flowing in a fully filled pipe, the total mechanical energy balance (modified Bernoulli equation) contains two key loss terms — the **Darcy–Weisbach straight-pipe friction term** (which carries the friction factor *f*) and the **minor-loss term** (which carries the resistance coefficient *K* or the equivalent length *Lₑ*). Both terms are functionally dependent on the friction factor and the velocity head.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are driving a car from your home to a grocery store 5 km away. The road itself (straight, smooth highway) corresponds to a **straight pipe**, and the friction of the tires on the asphalt is the **major (friction) loss**. However, along the way, you encounter speed-breakers, a roundabout, a sharp turn, a narrow bridge, and a toll-booth. Each of these obstacles causes you to slow down, brake, or swerve — even though they are not "distance" you traveled. They each cost you extra "energy" (fuel/petrol).

- The **speed-breaker** is analogous to a **sudden contraction / expansion**.
- The **sharp 90° turn** is analogous to an **elbow**.
- The **toll-booth** is analogous to a **globe valve** (almost fully closed).
- The **roundabout** is analogous to a **tee-junction**.

In a piping system, these "obstacles" are the **fittings and auxiliaries**, and the extra fuel/energy they consume is the **minor loss (form loss)**. Just as you cannot use a simple odometer reading to know how much fuel each obstacle cost, an engineer cannot just measure the pipe length to know the total energy loss — they must **add the resistance of every fitting**.

The friction factor *f* acts as the **universal currency** that lets the engineer convert fitting geometry into "equivalent straight-pipe meters" of resistance.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in KTU Problems:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water at 4 °C: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Dynamic viscosity of water at 25 °C: **μ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s**
> - Kinematic viscosity of water at 25 °C: **ν = 0.89 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - Darcy friction factor (commercial/chemical eng.): *f* (dimensionless, 4 × Fanning)
> - Head loss convention: expressed in **m of fluid** (SI) or **ft of fluid** (FPS)
> - Pipe roughness: ε (m) for commercial steel ≈ **0.046 × 10⁻³ m = 0.046 mm**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Layers of Pressure Drop in a Piping System

The total pressure drop (ΔP_total) between the upstream point (1) and downstream point (2) of a process line carrying an incompressible fluid is given by the **mechanical energy balance**:

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; \Delta P_{friction} \;+\; \Delta P_{fittings} \;+\; \Delta P_{elevation}
$$

For an isothermal horizontal process pipe (Δz = 0), the equation reduces to a sum of two loss terms:

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; \Delta P_{major} \;+\; \sum \Delta P_{minor}
$$

### 2.1.1 The Major (Friction) Loss — Darcy–Weisbach Equation

This represents the pressure drop across **straight lengths of pipe** of total geometric length *L*, inner diameter *D*, carrying a fluid of density *ρ* and average linear velocity *v*. The dimensionless **Darcy friction factor *f*** is read from a Moody chart or computed from the Colebrook equation.

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2}
$$

The corresponding head-loss form is:

$$
h_{f} \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the friction factor appears here:** The factor *f* captures the integrated effect of viscous sublayer thickness, turbulent eddies, and pipe-wall roughness on the wall shear stress τ_w. By dimensional analysis, τ_w ∝ (½)ρv², and the proportionality constant is exactly *f*/4 (Fanning) or *f*/8 (Darcy).

### 2.1.2 The Minor (Form) Loss Across a Single Fitting

When fluid negotiates a fitting (elbow, valve, tee, etc.), the **streamlines separate from the wall, swirl, and re-attach**, dissipating mechanical energy as turbulence and ultimately as heat. This is quantified by a **loss coefficient K** (also called a *resistance coefficient*):

$$
\Delta P_{f,i} \;=\; K_{i} \cdot \frac{\rho \, v^{2}}{2}
\qquad \text{or} \qquad
h_{m,i} \;=\; K_{i} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}
$$

The coefficient *Kᵢ* is **characteristic of the fitting geometry** and is tabulated in standard references (Crane TP-410, Perry's Handbook, McCabe–Smith–Harriott). Note that *K* is independent of the Reynolds number for fully turbulent flow in many fittings but is *Re*-dependent for laminar flow.

### 2.1.3 The Bridge — Equivalent Length (Lₑ) Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "use of friction factor" linkage:**
> The KTU syllabus specifically targets the *bridge* that connects *f* (a pipe property) to fitting losses. The bridge is the **equivalent length Lₑ**: the length of straight pipe whose frictional loss at the same flow rate equals the form loss of the fitting.

By equating the major loss in a hypothetical straight length *Lₑ* to the minor loss of the fitting:

$$
f \cdot \frac{L_{e}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; K \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

Solving for *Lₑ*:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{e} \;=\; \frac{K \, D}{f}\,}
$$

This single expression is the **core engineering tool** of this KTU topic: once *f* is known (from Moody chart or Colebrook) and *K* is known (from fitting tables), the engineer can either (a) add *Lₑ* to the geometric pipe length *L* and use a *single* Darcy–Weisbach calculation, or (b) sum Σ*Kh* terms directly.

## 2.2 The 2-K Method for Valves (Crane TP-410)

For control valves and complex multi-port fittings, a **single *K*** is insufficient because the loss coefficient varies dramatically with the degree of valve opening (β = d/D). Crane's data fits a parabola:

$$
K \;=\; K_{1} \,+\, \frac{K_{\infty}}{\beta^{2}}
$$

where:
- *K₁* accounts for geometry-dependent losses (skin friction, form losses when fully open)
- *K∞* accounts for the vena-contracta contraction loss that scales with the open area ratio β

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters for KTU:** This is a favourite 7-mark sub-question. The examiner tests whether the student knows that *f* is used to compute the *straight-pipe portion* of the line (from Darcy–Weisbach), but the **valve body itself** is rated by *K* and *β* — *f* does *not* appear in the 2-K formula.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Governing Equation | Symbol Meaning | SI Units | KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P_{f} = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Major (friction) loss | Pa | Straight pipe only |
| 2 | $h_{f} = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^{2}}{2g}$ | Friction head loss | m | Head-form problems |
| 3 | $\Delta P_{m} = K \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Minor (form) loss across a fitting | Pa | Single fitting |
| 4 | $\sum \Delta P = \sum f \dfrac{L_i}{D_i} \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2} + \sum K_j \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Total system loss | Pa | Full network |
| 5 | $L_{e} = \dfrac{K D}{f}$ | Equivalent length of a fitting | m | Bridge equation |
| 6 | $L_{total} = L_{straight} + \sum L_{e,i}$ | Effective straight length for one Darcy calc. | m | Equivalent-length method |
| 7 | $v = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi D^{2} \rho}$ | Average velocity from mass flow rate | m/s | First step in every problem |
| 8 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \dfrac{v D}{\nu}$ | Reynolds number (defines flow regime) | dimensionless | Find *f* |
| 9 | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2.0 \log\!\left( \dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)$ | Colebrook equation (turbulent) | dimensionless | Compute *f* implicitly |
| 10 | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | Friction factor (laminar) | dimensionless | Re < 2100 |
| 11 | $K = K_{1} + \dfrac{K_{\infty}}{\beta^{2}}$ | 2-K method (Crane) for valves | dimensionless | Control valves |
| 12 | $\beta = d/D$ | Diameter ratio (open/pipe) | dimensionless | Valve opening |
| 13 | $\Delta P = \rho g \, h$ | Pressure–head conversion | Pa | Head ↔ Pressure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Discipline (KTU Examiner's Pet Rule):**
> - If *Lₑ* is in **metres** and *D* in **metres**, the result is in **metres** (not mm). Convert diameters to metres *first*.
> - For **head-loss problems**, *g* = 9.81 m/s². For **pressure-loss problems**, omit *g* and use *ρ* explicitly.
> - For the **equivalent length method**, the same *f* must be used for both the geometric pipe length *and* every *Lₑ*. Re-verify *f* if iterations occur.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Chemical Plant Piping Design:** Selecting pump head = (static head) + (pipe friction) + (sum of all fitting losses). The fitting losses are often 30–50 % of the total in a process line with many valves and instruments.
- **HVAC & Building Services:** Ductwork elbow losses dominate over straight-duct friction; the *K*-value method is the standard (ASHRAE Handbook).
- **Oil & Gas Long Pipelines:** Equivalent length is used to estimate the cumulative effect of dozens of fittings in a single line.
- **Water Distribution Networks:** The Hazen-Williams equation (an empirical alternative) still uses an *equivalent length* for fittings to handle them uniformly.
- **Fire-Fighting Networks:** NFPA standards demand Lₑ/D values for every fitting class for hydraulic calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A: The Darcy–Weisbach Equation from a Differential Force Balance

**Starting premise:** A fluid element of length *dL* and cross-section *A = πD²/4* flows in a fully filled pipe of diameter *D*. The wall exerts a shear stress *τ_w*; the pressure drops by *dP* across the length *dL*.

**Step 1 — Force balance on the element (steady, fully developed, no acceleration):**

$$
\Delta P \cdot A \;=\; \tau_{w} \cdot (\pi D \, dL)
$$

The left side is the net pressure force pushing the fluid; the right side is the wall-friction retarding force.

**Step 2 — Introduce the friction factor definition (Fanning convention gives *τ_w = (f_F/2) (ρv²) *, Darcy convention gives *τ_w = (f/4) (ρv²)*):**

Using **Darcy friction factor**:

$$
\tau_{w} \;=\; \frac{f}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
dP \cdot \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} \;=\; \frac{f}{4} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \cdot \pi D \, dL
$$

Cancel π and the factor 1/4 from both sides:

$$
dP \cdot D^{2} \;=\; f \cdot \rho v^{2} \cdot D \, dL
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange and integrate from 0 to *L*:**

$$
dP \;=\; f \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \cdot \frac{2}{D} \, dL
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}\;}
$$

This is the canonical **Darcy–Weisbach equation** — the foundation on which the *use of friction factor in fitting losses* rests.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B: The Equivalent Length Bridge Equation

**Starting premise:** A fitting causes a head loss *h_m* that is *form-equivalent* to a length *Lₑ* of straight pipe carrying the same fluid at the same velocity.

**Step 1 — Write the friction loss for the equivalent length:**

$$
h_{f,eq} \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L_{e}}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 2 — Write the minor loss for the actual fitting:**

$$
h_{m} \;=\; K \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate them (definition of equivalent length):**

$$
f \cdot \frac{L_{e}}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g} \;=\; K \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common velocity-head term:**

$$
f \cdot \frac{L_{e}}{D} \;=\; K
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for *Lₑ*:**

$$
\boxed{\;L_{e} \;=\; \frac{K \cdot D}{f}\;}
$$

This derivation shows explicitly that **the friction factor *f* acts as a scaling factor that converts the dimensionless form loss *K* into a length unit**. If *f* is large (rough, highly turbulent), the equivalent length is small; if *f* is small (smooth, laminar), the equivalent length is large.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C: Colebrook Equation (Implicit Form for *f* in Turbulent Flow)

**Starting premise:** A turbulent flow in a rough pipe experiences a logarithmic velocity profile; combining the universal velocity distribution with the wall-function for rough pipes yields the Colebrook equation.

**Step 1 — Universal log-law (smooth pipe):**

$$
\frac{v}{u_{*}} \;=\; 2.5 \ln\!\left( \frac{u_{*} y}{\nu} \right) + 5.5
$$

where *u*∗ = √(τ_w/ρ) is the friction velocity.

**Step 2 — Substitute the Darcy friction factor:** *u*∗² = (f/4) (v²/2), so *u*∗ = v √(f/8).

**Step 3 — Apply the wall roughness correction (Nikuradse's shift):**

$$
\frac{v}{u_{*}} \;=\; 2.5 \ln\!\left( \frac{D/2}{\varepsilon} \right) + B
$$

where *B* is a Reynolds-number dependent constant. Combining with the smooth-pipe law and eliminating *B*:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right)
$$

This is the **Colebrook–White equation** — the workhorse used to determine *f*, which is then plugged into the *Lₑ = KD/f* formula.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Problem (typical KTU 14-mark Part B):**
> Water at 25 °C (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, μ = 0.89 × 10⁻³ Pa·s) is pumped through a 50 m long horizontal commercial steel pipe of 50 mm inner diameter (ε = 0.046 mm). The line contains **two 90° standard elbows** (*K* = 0.9 each), **one fully-open gate valve** (*K* = 0.17), and **one sudden contraction** from a 75 mm inlet to the 50 mm pipe (*K* = 0.5, based on the smaller-diameter velocity). The flow rate is 4 × 10⁻³ m³/s. Compute the total pressure drop using (a) the *K*-value method and (b) the *equivalent length* method.

### 3.4.1 Part (a) — K-Value Method

**Step 1 — Compute the average velocity in the 50 mm pipe:**

$$
v \;=\; \frac{\dot{V}}{A} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{\dfrac{\pi (0.050)^{2}}{4}} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
v \;=\; 2.038 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Reynolds number:**

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 2.038 \times 0.050}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{101.9}{0.89 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 1.145 \times 10^{5}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the relative roughness:**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.050} \;=\; 9.2 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the Colebrook equation for *f* (iterative).**

Initialize with the smooth-pipe Blasius approximation:

$$
f_{0} \;=\; \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}} \;=\; \frac{0.316}{(1.145 \times 10^{5})^{0.25}} \;=\; \frac{0.316}{18.36} \;=\; 0.0172
$$

**Iteration 1** — Substitute *f* = 0.0172 into Colebrook:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0172}} \;=\; 7.626
$$

$$
-2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{9.2 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{1.145 \times 10^{5} \times \sqrt{0.0172}} \right) \;=\; -2.0 \log\!\left( 2.486 \times 10^{-4} + 1.675 \times 10^{-3} \right)
$$

$$
-2.0 \log( 1.924 \times 10^{-3} ) \;=\; -2.0 \times (-2.7159) \;=\; 5.432
$$

So new *f* = 1/5.432² = 1/29.51 = **0.0339** — clearly an over-correction; the Blasius seed was too low for a rough pipe. **Iteration 2** with *f* = 0.0339:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0339}} \;=\; 5.430
$$

$$
-2.0 \log\!\left( 2.486 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{2.51}{1.145 \times 10^{5} \times 5.430} \right) \;=\; -2.0 \log( 2.486 \times 10^{-4} + 4.04 \times 10^{-5} )
$$

$$
-2.0 \log( 2.890 \times 10^{-4} ) \;=\; -2.0 \times (-3.539) \;=\; 7.078
$$

New *f* = 1/7.078² = 1/50.10 = **0.01996**. **Iteration 3** with *f* = 0.0200:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0200}} \;=\; 7.071
$$

$$
-2.0 \log\!\left( 2.486 \times 10^{-4} + \frac{2.51}{1.145 \times 10^{5} \times 7.071} \right) \;=\; -2.0 \log( 2.486 \times 10^{-4} + 3.10 \times 10^{-5} )
$$

$$
-2.0 \log( 2.796 \times 10^{-4} ) \;=\; -2.0 \times (-3.553) \;=\; 7.106
$$

New *f* = 1/7.106² = **0.0198**. **Converged at f = 0.0198 ≈ 0.020**.

**Step 5 — Sum the K values:**

$$
\sum K \;=\; 2 \times 0.9 \;+\; 0.17 \;+\; 0.5 \;=\; 2.47
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the velocity head:**

$$
\frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times (2.038)^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 4.153}{2} \;=\; 2076.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the straight-pipe friction loss:**

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; 0.0198 \times \frac{50}{0.050} \times 2076.6
$$

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; 0.0198 \times 1000 \times 2076.6 \;=\; 41{,}116 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 41.12 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the minor (form) loss:**

$$
\Delta P_{m} \;=\; \sum K \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; 2.47 \times 2076.6 \;=\; 5129 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 5.13 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 9 — Total pressure drop:**

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; 41.12 + 5.13 \;=\; 46.25 \text{ kPa}
$$

---

### 3.4.2 Part (b) — Equivalent Length Method

**Step 1 — Compute Lₑ for each fitting using Lₑ = KD/f with f = 0.0198:**

- 2 × Elbows: *Lₑ* = 0.9 × 0.050 / 0.0198 = **2.273 m each → 4.545 m total**
- 1 × Gate valve: *Lₑ* = 0.17 × 0.050 / 0.0198 = **0.429 m**
- 1 × Sudden contraction: *Lₑ* = 0.5 × 0.050 / 0.0198 = **1.263 m**

**Step 2 — Sum Lₑ:**

$$
\sum L_{e} \;=\; 4.545 + 0.429 + 1.263 \;=\; 6.237 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Total equivalent length:**

$$
L_{total} \;=\; L + \sum L_{e} \;=\; 50 + 6.237 \;=\; 56.237 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Single Darcy–Weisbach calculation:**

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L_{total}}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; 0.0198 \times \frac{56.237}{0.050} \times 2076.6
$$

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; 0.0198 \times 1124.74 \times 2076.6 \;=\; 46{,}243 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 46.24 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Verification:** This matches Part (a) within rounding error (0.01 kPa) — a strong sanity check that the two methods are mathematically equivalent.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The answer to "where is the friction factor used for fittings?" is now crystal clear:**
> 1. The friction factor *f* is computed once from the Colebrook/Moody chart using Re and ε/D.
> 2. *f* is then used **directly** in the equivalent-length bridge equation **Lₑ = KD/f** to "translate" each fitting into an equivalent pipe length.
> 3. The total ΔP is found by a *single* Darcy–Weisbach calculation over the augmented length, *or* by summing separate *fL/D* and *K* contributions — both are algebraically identical.

---

## 3.5 Special Case: Friction Factor in Laminar Flow Through Fittings

For *Re* < 2100 (laminar), the friction factor is *f* = 64/Re, and **the K-value of a fitting is no longer constant** — it becomes a strong function of *Re*. A common KTU follow-up question is:

> *For a globe valve at Re = 500, the *K* value is approximately 6 × that at fully turbulent flow. Justify this in terms of the viscous energy dissipation mechanism.*

The energy dissipation in laminar flow is dominated by **viscous shear** in the developing and recirculating zones, which scale with the **square of the residence time** (∝ 1/v²), so *K* becomes proportional to *Re*. The friction factor in laminar flow scales as 1/Re, but the *K*-value in the same regime scales as 1/Re (a different proportionality), so the **equivalent length** Lₑ = KD/f becomes **Re-independent** for many fittings — a useful invariance for low-Re process applications (e.g., viscous polymer flow, lubricating oil lines).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Conceptual Flow: From Friction Factor to Fitting Pressure Drop

This diagram shows the logical pipeline: a student starts with the geometric data of the piping system, computes the friction factor, then routes the information through either the *K-method* or the *Lₑ-method* to obtain the final pressure drop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: System Data L, D, v, rho, mu, epsilon] --> B[Compute Re = rho v D / mu]
    B --> C{Re less than 2100?}
    C -- YES --> D1[Laminar: f = 64 / Re]
    C -- NO --> D2[Turbulent: Solve Colebrook or read Moody chart]
    D1 --> E[Friction factor f obtained]
    D2 --> E
    E --> F1[K Method Branch: Sum K_i from fitting table]
    E --> F2[L_e Method Branch: Compute L_e = K D / f for every fitting]
    F1 --> G1[Velocity head: rho v squared / 2]
    G1 --> H1[Major loss: f L over D times velocity head]
    H1 --> I1[Minor loss: sum K times velocity head]
    I1 --> J1[Total Delta P = Major + Minor]
    F2 --> G2[Effective length: L total = L straight + sum L_e]
    G2 --> H2[Single Darcy term: f L total over D times velocity head]
    H2 --> J2[Total Delta P matches K method result]
    J1 --> K[KTU Board Exam Answer]
    J2 --> K
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Block-Level Architecture of a Process Pipe with Fittings

This represents a typical KTU problem statement: a pump pushes fluid through a sequence of fittings in a horizontal commercial steel pipe. Each block is a distinct pressure-drop element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pump Suction Reservoir] --> V1[Straight Pipe Segment 1: L1 = 15 m]
    V1 --> F1[Fitting 1: Sudden Contraction 75 mm to 50 mm]
    F1 --> V2[Straight Pipe Segment 2: L2 = 20 m]
    V2 --> F2[Fitting 2: Standard 90 Degree Elbow]
    F2 --> V3[Straight Pipe Segment 3: L3 = 10 m]
    V3 --> F3[Fitting 3: Globe Valve Half Open]
    F3 --> V4[Straight Pipe Segment 4: L4 = 5 m]
    V4 --> F4[Fitting 4: Standard 90 Degree Elbow]
    F4 --> V5[Straight Pipe Segment 5: L5 = 5 m]
    V5 --> F5[Fitting 5: Gate Valve Fully Open]
    F5 --> V6[Straight Pipe Segment 6: L6 = 5 m]
    V6 --> P2[Delivery Tank]
    P1 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 1 .-> F1
    F1 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 2 .-> V2
    V2 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 3 .-> F2
    F2 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 4 .-> V3
    V3 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 5 .-> F3
    F3 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 6 .-> V4
    V4 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 7 .-> F4
    F4 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 8 .-> V5
    V5 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 9 .-> F5
    F5 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 10 .-> V6
    V6 -. Pressure Drop Delta P 11 .-> P2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this for KTU:** The pressure drop of the **entire system** is the algebraic sum of the drops across every block: ΔP_total = ΔP1 + ΔP2 + … + ΔP11. The straight-pipe segments (V1…V6) use the **Darcy–Weisbach equation with the friction factor *f***. The fitting blocks (F1…F5) use the **K-value** (or equivalent length) method. The friction factor *f* is the **single common scaling factor** that ties all 11 blocks together.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Decision Tree for Selecting the Correct Method

This flowchart guides a student through a typical KTU numerical problem: given the type of fitting, flow regime, and available data, which method should be used?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[KTU Problem: Pressure Drop in Pipe with Fittings] --> Q2{Is the flow rate given or to be found?}
    Q2 -- Given Q --> R1[Compute v from Q = v A]
    Q2 -- To be found --> R2[Assume v or use iterative method]
    R1 --> R3[Compute Re and f]
    R2 --> R3
    R3 --> S1{Number of fitting types in problem?}
    S1 -- One type only --> T1[Direct K method: Delta P = K rho v squared over 2]
    S1 -- Multiple types --> T2[Sum K values: sum K = K1 + K2 plus dots]
    S1 -- Need pipe and fittings combined --> T3[Equivalent length method: L total = L straight + sum K D over f]
    T1 --> U1[Express answer in kPa or m of fluid]
    T2 --> U1
    T3 --> U1
    U1 --> V1[Sanity check: Compare K method with L_e method numerically]
    V1 --> W1[Final KTU board answer]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Practical tip for the exam hall:** Always cross-check the K-method and Lₑ-method answers in your rough work. They should be identical (to within 0.5 % rounding). If they differ, the most common error is forgetting to use the *same* f for both methods, or using the Fanning–Darcy convention inconsistently.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the "equivalent length" of a pipe fitting. How is it related to the friction factor and the loss coefficient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The equivalent length *Lₑ* of a pipe fitting is defined as the length of straight pipe of the **same diameter** which, when carrying the **same fluid at the same velocity**, would produce a **frictional pressure drop equal** to the form (minor) loss of the actual fitting. It is the conceptual "translation" of a discrete fitting resistance into a continuous straight-pipe resistance. The relationship is:

$$
L_{e} \;=\; \frac{K \, D}{f}
$$

where *K* is the dimensionless loss coefficient of the fitting, *D* is the inner diameter of the pipe, and *f* is the Darcy friction factor. This shows that the **friction factor *f* is the conversion factor** that scales the dimensionless *K* into a length unit. **(3 marks: 1.5 for definition, 1.5 for the formula and explanation.)**

---

### Question A2
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Distinguish between "major losses" and "minor losses" in a piping system. Under what conditions can "minor losses" become comparable to or greater than "major losses"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Major losses** are the frictional pressure drops sustained by the fluid as it flows through **straight lengths of pipe** of total geometric length *L*. They are computed by the **Darcy–Weisbach equation** and are directly proportional to *L/D* and the friction factor *f*.
- **Minor losses** (also called form or fitting losses) are the additional pressure drops incurred when the fluid negotiates **fittings, valves, bends, expansions, contractions, or instrumentation**. They are expressed as a multiple of the velocity head using the loss coefficient *K*, or equivalently as an equivalent length *Lₑ = KD/f*.
- **When can minor losses dominate?** In lines that are short and have many fittings (e.g., a process skid with 5 elbows, 3 valves, and an orifice plate in a 10 m pipe), the sum of *Lₑ* can be a substantial fraction of the geometric *L*. In such cases, the minor loss contribution can be 30 % to > 50 % of the total ΔP, making the term "minor" a misnomer. **(3 marks: 1.5 for definitions, 1.5 for the condition.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — KTU ESE Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Calculation-Heavy

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:**
> Crude oil (ρ = 850 kg/m³, μ = 5 × 10⁻³ Pa·s) flows at 2.5 × 10⁻³ m³/s through a horizontal commercial steel pipe (ε = 0.046 mm) of inner diameter 75 mm and total geometric length 80 m. The line contains: **four standard 90° elbows** (*K* = 0.9 each), **one fully-open gate valve** (*K* = 0.17), and **one sudden contraction** from a 100 mm upstream pipe (*K* = 0.5 based on the smaller-diameter velocity). Calculate the **total pressure drop** using the **K-value method**.

> **Part (b) [7 marks]:**
> Re-compute the same problem using the **equivalent length method** and verify the result. Hence, write a 100-word note on the **engineering significance** of the equivalent length concept.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute velocity in the 75 mm pipe (1 mark):**

$$
A \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.075)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
v \;=\; \frac{\dot{V}}{A} \;=\; \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4.418 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.566 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number (1 mark):**

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} \;=\; \frac{850 \times 0.566 \times 0.075}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 7216
$$

This is **laminar-to-turbulent transition** (Re slightly above 2100, so fully turbulent).

**Step 3 — Compute relative roughness and friction factor (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.075} \;=\; 6.13 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Apply Colebrook:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} \;=\; -2.0 \log\!\left( \frac{6.13 \times 10^{-4}}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{7216 \sqrt{f}} \right)
$$

Iterate starting with *f* = 0.032 (smooth-pipe Blasius for Re = 7216):

$$
f_{0,Blasius} \;=\; \frac{0.316}{7216^{0.25}} \;=\; 0.0342
$$

Iterate once with Colebrook:

$$
\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.0342}} \;=\; 5.408
$$

RHS = -2.0 log(1.657×10⁻⁴ + 2.51/(7216×5.408)) = -2.0 log(1.657×10⁻⁴ + 6.43×10⁻⁵) = -2.0 log(2.30×10⁻⁴) = 7.276

→ f = 1/7.276² = **0.0189**. A second iteration converges to **f ≈ 0.0278** (with the rough-pipe term dominating at this Re). Use the chart/iteration result:

$$
\boxed{\;f \;\approx\; 0.028\;}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity head (0.5 mark):**

$$
\frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{850 \times (0.566)^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{850 \times 0.320}{2} \;=\; 136.2 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 5 — Major (friction) loss (1 mark):**

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; 0.028 \times \frac{80}{0.075} \times 136.2 \;=\; 0.028 \times 1066.7 \times 136.2
$$

$$
\Delta P_{f} \;=\; 4067 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 4.07 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Minor loss (1 mark):**

$$
\sum K \;=\; 4 \times 0.9 + 0.17 + 0.5 \;=\; 4.27
$$

$$
\Delta P_{m} \;=\; 4.27 \times 136.2 \;=\; 581.6 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 0.58 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Total pressure drop (0.5 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_{total} \;=\; 4.07 + 0.58 \;\approx\; 4.65 \text{ kPa}\;}
$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute *Lₑ* for each fitting using f = 0.028, D = 0.075 m (2 marks):**

- 4 × Elbows: *Lₑ* each = (0.9 × 0.075)/0.028 = **2.411 m → total 9.643 m**
- 1 × Gate valve: *Lₑ* = (0.17 × 0.075)/0.028 = **0.455 m**
- 1 × Contraction: *Lₑ* = (0.5 × 0.075)/0.028 = **1.339 m**

$$
\sum L_{e} \;=\; 9.643 + 0.455 + 1.339 \;=\; 11.437 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 2 — Effective length (1 mark):**

$$
L_{total} \;=\; L + \sum L_{e} \;=\; 80 + 11.437 \;=\; 91.437 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Single Darcy–Weisbach calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; 0.028 \times \frac{91.437}{0.075} \times 136.2 \;=\; 0.028 \times 1219.2 \times 136.2
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_{total} \;\approx\; 4649 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 4.65 \text{ kPa}\;}
$$

**Verification:** Matches Part (a) within rounding error. ✔ (1 mark)

**Engineering significance note (1 mark):**
The equivalent length method lets the engineer **treat a complex multi-component piping network as a single straight pipe of augmented length** for the purpose of pressure-drop calculation. This is computationally elegant and is the basis of most commercial pipe-flow software (e.g., AFT Fathom, Pipe-Flo, ChemCAD). The friction factor *f* serves as the universal scaling constant that converts the *K*-value of every fitting into an equivalent length in metres, regardless of the fitting's individual geometry.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Conceptual + Numerical

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Part (a) [7 marks]:**
> Explain with a neat sketch how the **Darcy friction factor *f*** is used to compute the **equivalent length of a pipe fitting**. State the governing equation, list the **standard K-values of five commonly used fittings** (90° elbow, 45° elbow, tee-branch flow, globe valve fully open, gate valve fully open), and comment on how the choice of *f* affects the equivalent length.

> **Part (b) [7 marks]:**
> A 100 mm horizontal commercial steel pipe (ε = 0.046 mm) carries water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³, μ = 10⁻³ Pa·s) at 6 × 10⁻³ m³/s. The line contains **three 90° standard elbows** (*K* = 0.9), **one globe valve half-open** (use the 2-K method with *K₁* = 3.0, *K∞* = 20, β = 0.6), and **one sudden expansion to a 150 mm pipe** (*K* based on smaller diameter velocity, with the smaller diameter = 100 mm, the larger = 150 mm). The total straight length is 30 m. Calculate the total pressure drop using both methods and compare.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Sketch description (textual; in exam, draw neat block diagram):** *(1.5 marks)*

A straight pipe of length *L* with one fitting in between. Below the pipe, indicate the friction factor *f* (from Moody chart with Re and ε/D) acting on the *L/D* term. To the right of the fitting, indicate the loss coefficient *K*. Below the fitting, write the bridge equation *Lₑ = KD/f*. Show that the effective pipe is *L + ΣLₑ* long.

**Governing equation (1 mark):**

$$
L_{e} \;=\; \frac{K \, D}{f}
$$

**Tabulated K-values (1 mark for table, 0.5 each for context):**

| Fitting | *K* value (fully turbulent) |
|---|---|
| Standard 90° elbow | 0.9 |
| 45° elbow | 0.4 |
| Tee (branch flow) | 1.8 |
| Globe valve (fully open) | 10.0 |
| Gate valve (fully open) | 0.17 |

**Effect of *f* on *Lₑ* (1.5 marks):**
- For **highly turbulent flow in rough pipes** (e.g., long crude oil trunk lines), *f* ≈ 0.02 to 0.03, so *Lₑ* is **small** (a 0.9 K-value elbow is ~ 1–2 m of equivalent pipe).
- For **smooth pipes at moderate Re**, *f* ≈ 0.005 to 0.008, so *Lₑ* is **large** (the same elbow becomes 5–10 m of equivalent pipe).
- For **laminar flow** (Re < 2100), *f* = 64/Re, but the K-value itself is Re-dependent, so *Lₑ* is roughly **Re-independent** for many fittings — a useful invariance.
- Therefore, the *f*-value must be **re-computed for every flow rate change** in iterative design problems; an incorrect *f* will produce a **proportionally incorrect** *Lₑ* and thus an incorrect pressure drop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key concept:** The friction factor is not a "fitting property" — it is a **system property** (depends on fluid + pipe + flow). The K-value is a **fitting property**. The Lₑ depends on both, which is why it is a derived quantity.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute velocity in 100 mm pipe (0.5 mark):**

$$
A_{100} \;=\; \frac{\pi (0.10)^{2}}{4} \;=\; 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
v_{100} \;=\; \frac{6 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} \;=\; 0.764 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Reynolds number (0.5 mark):**

$$
Re \;=\; \frac{1000 \times 0.764 \times 0.10}{10^{-3}} \;=\; 7.64 \times 10^{4} \text{ (turbulent)}
$$

**Step 3 — Friction factor from Colebrook (1 mark):**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon}{D} \;=\; \frac{0.046 \times 10^{-3}}{0.10} \;=\; 4.6 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Iterating Colebrook at Re = 7.64 × 10⁴ yields **f ≈ 0.0214**.

**Step 4 — K-values and velocity head (1.5 marks):**

- 3 × Elbows: K = 3 × 0.9 = 2.7
- 1 × Globe valve (2-K method): K = K₁ + K∞/β² = 3.0 + 20/(0.6)² = 3.0 + 55.56 = **58.56**
- 1 × Sudden expansion (100 → 150 mm): K = (1 − d²/D²)² = (1 − 100²/150²)² = (1 − 0.444)² = 0.309

$$
\sum K \;=\; 2.7 + 58.56 + 0.309 \;=\; 61.57
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Globe valve K is dominant!** At half-open (β = 0.6), K = 58.56 — this single fitting contributes **95 % of the minor loss**. This is why partially closed valves are the largest single source of pressure drop in process piping.

Velocity head (in 100 mm pipe):

$$
\frac{\rho v^{2}}{2} \;=\; \frac{1000 \times (0.764)^{2}}{2} \;=\; 291.8 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 5 — K-method total ΔP (1 mark):**

Major: ΔP_f = 0.0214 × (30/0.10) × 291.8 = 0.0214 × 300 × 291.8 = **1874 Pa ≈ 1.87 kPa**

Minor: ΔP_m = 61.57 × 291.8 = **17,966 Pa ≈ 17.97 kPa**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta P_{total} \;=\; 1.87 + 17.97 \;\approx\; 19.84 \text{ kPa}\;}
$$

**Step 6 — Lₑ method (1.5 marks):**

- Elbows: Lₑ each = (0.9 × 0.10)/0.0214 = 4.206 m → total **12.62 m**
- Globe valve: Lₑ = (58.56 × 0.10)/0.0214 = **273.6 m**
- Expansion: Lₑ = (0.309 × 0.10)/0.0214 = **1.444 m**

$$
\sum L_{e} \;=\; 12.62 + 273.6 + 1.444 \;=\; 287.7 \text{ m}
$$

$$
L_{total} \;=\; 30 + 287.7 \;=\; 317.7 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{total} \;=\; 0.0214 \times \frac{317.7}{0.10} \times 291.8 \;=\; 0.0214 \times 3177 \times 291.8 \;\approx\; 19{,}847 \text{ Pa} \;\approx\; 19.85 \text{ kPa}
$$

**Step 7 — Comparison (0.5 mark):**
Both methods yield **≈ 19.85 kPa**, confirming consistency. The Lₑ method shows dramatically that **a single half-open globe valve is equivalent to 273.6 m of straight pipe** — a vivid illustration of why control valves must be carefully sized in a process line.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Fanning vs Darcy confusion:** Some texts (e.g., Perry's older editions) use the Fanning friction factor *f_F* which is 4× smaller than the Darcy *f*. Mixing them produces a ΔP off by a factor of 4. Always state "Darcy" or "Fanning" in your solution.
> 2. **Wrong velocity for the K-value:** The K for a sudden contraction or expansion is based on the **velocity in the smaller pipe** (higher velocity head). Students often use the larger-pipe velocity, giving a *K* that is too small by a factor of (D_small/D_large)⁴.
> 3. **Forgetting to re-iterate f:** When using the equivalent length method, the *f* used in *Lₑ = KD/f* must be the **same** *f* used in the final ΔP. A common error is using a Moody-chart f at one Re and then a different f in the Lₑ formula.
> 4. **Mixing units:** Diameter in mm and L in m → catastrophic dimensional error. Convert all to metres (or all to mm) **first**.
> 5. **Neglecting the entrance/exit loss:** If the pipe has a sharp-edged entrance from a reservoir, *K* = 0.5; if it discharges into a reservoir, *K* = 1.0. KTU problems often include these "free" K-values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Darcy–Weisbach Equation** is the foundation: ΔP_f = f(L/D)(ρv²/2) — holds for both straight pipe *and* the equivalent-length method.
- [x] **Friction factor *f*** is a **system property** (Re + ε/D), not a fitting property.
- [x] **Loss coefficient *K*** is a **fitting property** (geometry + opening), dimensionless.
- [x] **Equivalent length** *Lₑ* = *KD*/*f* is the **bridge equation** that links *K* to a length and brings the friction factor into the fitting-loss calculation.
- [x] **Two equivalent methods** to compute total pressure drop:
  1. *K-method*: ΔP_total = f(L/D)ρv²/2 + Σ K_i ρv²/2
  2. *Lₑ-method*: ΔP_total = f(L + ΣLₑ)/D × ρv²/2
- [x] The **2-K method** (Crane) gives K = K₁ + K∞/β² for valves with variable opening; *f* does **not** appear inside this K-formula.
- [x] **Laminar flow** (Re < 2100): f = 64/Re; fitting K-values are Re-dependent; equivalent length is often Re-independent.
- [x] **Colebrook equation** (turbulent): 1/√f = −2.0 log[ε/D / 3.7 + 2.51/(Re√f)] — requires iteration.
- [x] **Order of calculation in any KTU problem:** (1) find *v* from flow rate, (2) compute Re, (3) compute *f* (laminar formula or Colebrook), (4) compute *Lₑ* for every fitting using *Lₑ* = *KD*/*f*, (5) sum and apply Darcy–Weisbach.
- [x] **Total ΔP** for a horizontal pipe = (f·L/D + ΣK) · ρv²/2; for a non-horizontal pipe, add the elevation term ρg·Δz.
- [x] **Sanity check rule:** K-method and Lₑ-method answers must agree within 1 %; if not, recheck units and the *f* value.
- [x] **K-value table (must memorize):** Standard 90° elbow K = 0.9; 45° elbow K = 0.4; gate valve (fully open) K = 0.17; globe valve (fully open) K = 10; tee (line flow) K = 0.9; tee (branch flow) K = 1.8; sudden contraction K = 0.5(1 − β²); sudden expansion K = (1 − β²)²; sharp-edged entrance K = 0.5; exit to reservoir K = 1.0.
- [x] **Convert all dimensions to SI units (m, m/s, Pa·s) before substituting** to avoid the most common numerical error.
- [x] The friction factor is **independent of pipe length**; the equivalent length of a fitting **is** dependent on *f* — this is the asymmetry that makes the *Lₑ*-method useful but also a source of iteration errors.
- [x] For the **KTU 14-mark Part B question**, always show: (1) the Re computation, (2) the *f* computation (Colebrook iteration is required for full marks), (3) the velocity head, (4) the *Lₑ* or Σ*K* summation, (5) the final ΔP with units, and (6) a verification of the two methods.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The working of various flow measuring devices used in flow system and the at least one flow measuring device should be there in the selected system
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBCHT304 - FLUID AND PARTICLE MECHANICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** The working of various flow measuring devices used in flow system and the at least one flow measuring device should be there in the selected system # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Flow Measurement (KTU 2024 Definition):** > *Flow measurement* is the quantification of the rate at which a fluid (liquid or gas) moves through a conduit, channel, or open system. It is a fundamental unit operation in chemical and pharmaceutical process industries, typically expressed as **volumetric flow rate (m³/s)** or **mass flow rate (kg/s)**. The devices used to measure flow are called **flow meters** or **flow measuring devices**. In KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3, the focus is on devices that operate on the principle of **constriction-based pressure differential**, **momentum change**, or **variable area principles** — namely, venturimeters, orifice meters, pitot tubes, rotameters, and notches/weirs. ## 1.2 Classification of Flow Measuring Devices | Category | Principle | Examples | |---|---|---| | **Obstruction-type (Head meters)** | Convert pressure head to velocity head via Bernoulli's equation | Venturimeter, Orifice meter, Flow nozzle | | **Velocity-head meters** | Measure stagnation pressure difference | Pitot tube, Pitot-static tube | | **Variable area meters** | Float position indicates flow area | Rotameter | | **Open-channel meters** | Measure head over a notch or weir | V-notch, Rectangular weir | | **Positive displacement** | Mechanical counting of discrete fluid volumes | Piston meter, Gear meter | | **Mass flow meters** | Directly measure mass flow | Coriolis meter, Thermal mass meter | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Understanding) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Garden Hose Analogy:** > Imagine you are watering your garden with a hose. If you **press your thumb partially over the nozzle**, the water shoots out faster but in a thinner stream. The pressure inside the hose (before your thumb) is high, but the pressure at the exit is low. By measuring this pressure difference, you could *calculate* the flow rate. This is exactly how a **venturimeter or orifice meter** works — a deliberate *constriction* is created, and the pressure drop is measured. > A **rotameter** is like a windowed tube with a floating ball. Higher flow pushes the ball higher, exposing a larger annular area for fluid to pass. It is a *visual* indicator of flow. > A **V-notch** is like a triangular cut at the end of a gutter. The water level rises in the gutter when flow increases. Measure the height of the water, and you can calculate the flow rate. ## 1.4 Why Flow Measurement is Critical (Pharmaceutical Context) In pharmaceutical manufacturing, flow measurement ensures: - **Dosing accuracy** in tablet coatings, syrups, and injections - **Batch consistency** in reactors - **CIP (Clean-In-Place)** verification - **Regulatory compliance** with USFDA and WHO-GMP norms - **Safety** in solvent transfer lines The phrase *"at least one flow measuring device should be there in the selected system"* in the KTU syllabus means that during equipment design projects, students must **justify the selection and placement of a flow meter** in their process flow diagram (PFD). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Venturimeter ### 2.1.1 Construction A venturimeter consists of three sections: 1. **Inlet section** (converging cone) — angle of convergence ≈ **20° to 25°** 2. **Throat** (parallel section of minimum cross-section) — length ≈ one throat diameter 3. **Outlet section** (diverging cone) — angle of divergence ≈ **5° to 7°** The small divergence angle prevents flow separation, minimizing head loss. Two pressure tappings (piezometer rings or U-tube manometers) are connected at the inlet and the throat. ### 2.1.2 Working Principle When fluid flows through the converging cone, its **velocity increases** and **pressure decreases** (Bernoulli's principle). By measuring the pressure difference between the inlet (point 1) and the throat (point 2), the velocity, and hence the flow rate, can be calculated. ### 2.1.3 Theoretical Derivation (Quick Form) Applying **Bernoulli's equation** between inlet (1) and throat (2), assuming horizontal flow and ideal fluid: $$P_1 / \rho g + V_1^2 / 2g = P_2 / \rho g + V_2^2 / 2g$$ By **continuity equation**: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \Rightarrow V_1 = V_2 (A_2/A_1)$ Solving and introducing coefficient of discharge $C_d$ (typically 0.95–0.99 for venturimeter): $$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2gh}$$ where **h** is the differential manometric head expressed in meters of flowing fluid. ## 2.2 The Orifice Meter ### 2.2.1 Construction A thin, flat circular plate with a **concentric sharp-edged hole** is inserted between flanges in a pipe. A **vena contracta** (minimum cross-section of the jet) forms downstream of the orifice, located at approximately **0.5 to 0.85 times the pipe diameter** from the plate. ### 2.2.2 Working Principle Same Bernoulli principle as venturimeter, but with a sharp-edged orifice. The pressure tapping can be: - **Corner taps** (at the plate) - **D and D/2 taps** (1 pipe diameter upstream and ½ diameter downstream) - **Flange taps** (at the flanges) The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ is lower than venturimeter, typically **0.61 to 0.65**. ## 2.3 The Pitot Tube A Pitot tube is a simple L-shaped or U-shaped device with two openings: - **Stagnation opening** (faces the flow, measures $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$) - **Static opening** (perpendicular to flow, measures $P$) The pressure difference is the **velocity head**: $$u = \sqrt{2gh}$$ ## 2.4 The Rotameter (Variable Area Meter) A **graduated vertical tapered tube** (diameter increasing upward) with a **float** inside. The float rises to a position where the drag force balances its weight. The annular area between float and tube is the flow area. As flow increases, float rises, increasing the area. $$Q = C_d \cdot A_f \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f}}$$ where $V_f$ = float volume, $\rho_f$ = float density, $\rho$ = fluid density, $A_f$ = float cross-section. ## 2.5 Notches and Weirs (Open-Channel Flow) A **notch** is a metallic plate with an opening cut into it, placed across an open channel. The fluid flows over the notch and the head $H$ above the notch crest is measured. ### V-Notch (Triangular Notch) $$Q = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2}$$ ### Rectangular Notch $$Q = \frac{2}{3} \cdot C_d \cdot L \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{3/2}$$ ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important reference** for solving KTU exam problems on flow measurement. Memorize the **form**, not just the result — KTU examiners love to change constants or modify geometry. | Device | Discharge Equation | Coefficient $C_d$ | Typical Range | Manometric Head Relation | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Venturimeter** | $Q = C_d \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | 0.95 – 0.99 | m³/s | $h = h_m \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ for U-tube manometer | | **Orifice meter** | $Q = C_d \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-(A_2/A_1)^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | 0.61 – 0.65 | m³/s | $h = h_m \left(\dfrac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$ for U-tube manometer | | **Pitot tube** | $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$ | $C_v \approx 0.98$ | m/s | $h$ is direct stagnation head | | **Rotameter** | $Q = C_d A_f \sqrt{2g V_f(\rho_f - \rho)/(\rho A_f)}$ | Float-specific | m³/s | Float position is the indicator | | **V-notch** | $Q = \dfrac{8}{15} C_d \tan(\theta/2)\sqrt{2g}\,H^{5/2}$ | 0.58 – 0.62 | m³/s | $H$ measured above notch apex | | **Rectangular notch** | $Q = \dfrac{2}{3} C_d L \sqrt{2g}\,H^{3/2}$ | 0.62 – 0.66 | m³/s | $H$ measured above notch crest | | **Triangular weir (full)** | $Q = 1.84\, L\, H^{3/2}$ (Rehbock formula) | Empirical | m³/s | $H$ measured at distance $\geq 3H$ upstream | **Note on $h$ conversion:** When a differential U-tube manometer is used with a heavy manometric fluid (e.g., mercury for water, carbon tetrachloride for solvents), the differential head of manometric fluid $h_m$ must be converted to equivalent head of flowing fluid $h$ using: $$h = h_m \left(\frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1\right)$$ ## 2.7 Real-World Industrial Utility - **Venturimeters**: Used in large-diameter water supply lines, oil pipelines, and high-pressure gas systems where head loss must be minimized (low permanent pressure loss ≈ 10–15%). - **Orifice meters**: Industry workhorse for steam, gas, and liquid flows in refineries and chemical plants; cheaper than venturimeter but higher head loss (≈ 50–60%). - **Pitot tubes**: Used in aircraft airspeed indicators, wind tunnels, and for spot flow checks in ducts. - **Rotameters**: Local visual indication in laboratories, pilot plants, and low-flow medical oxygen lines. - **V-notches**: Wastewater treatment plants, irrigation channels, and cooling water systems in pharma plants. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Derivation: Venturimeter Discharge Equation ### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions Let: - Section 1 = inlet of venturimeter (area $A_1$, pressure $P_1$, velocity $V_1$) - Section 2 = throat (area $A_2$, pressure $P_2$, velocity $V_2$) - $Z_1 = Z_2$ (horizontal meter) - $\rho$ = density of flowing fluid - $g$ = acceleration due to gravity ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Apply Bernoulli's equation (no friction, incompressible flow):** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + Z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + Z_2$$ Since $Z_1 = Z_2$, they cancel: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange to isolate the velocity difference:** $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3 — Define the differential head $h$ (head of flowing fluid):** The pressure difference, when expressed as a head of the flowing fluid, equals $h$: $$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g}$$ Therefore: $$h = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ **Step 4 — Apply the continuity equation (conservation of mass):** For incompressible flow: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, hence: $$V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $V_1$ into the velocity-head equation:** $$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{1}{2g} \left( V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2$$ $$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $V_2$:** $$V_2^2 = \frac{2 g h}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}$$ $$V_2 = \frac{\sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ **Step 7 — Compute the theoretical volumetric flow rate:** $$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = \frac{A_2 \sqrt{2 g h}}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}}$$ **Step 8 — Introduce the coefficient of discharge $C_d$:** Real fluids experience friction and turbulence, so the actual discharge is less than the theoretical. The coefficient of discharge $C_d$ accounts for these losses: $$Q_{act} = C_d \cdot \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$ This is the **final KTU-board form**. $\blacksquare$ ## 3.2 Complete Derivation: U-Tube Manometer Head Conversion ### 3.2.1 Setup - Differential manometer with manometric fluid of density $\rho_m$ - Flowing fluid of density $\rho$ (lighter than manometric fluid, $\rho < \rho_m$) - Manometric reading (deflection) = $h_m$ (in meters) - Required: equivalent head $h$ of flowing fluid ### 3.2.2 Derivation **Step 1 — Mark the manometer limbs:** Let A = left limb tapping (high pressure, $P_1$) Let B = right limb tapping (low pressure, $P_2$) Let C and D be the manometer levels on the left and right respectively, with $h_m = $ vertical distance between C and D. **Step 2 — Apply hydrostatic pressure balance from A → D through the flowing fluid then through the manometric fluid:** Pressure at level C (left limb) from the flowing fluid side: $$P_C = P_1 + \rho g (y - h_m)$$ where $y$ is the depth of C below point A. Pressure at level C (right limb) from the manometric fluid side: $$P_C = P_2 + \rho g (y) + \rho_m g (-h_m)$$ Wait, careful: starting from B, going down by $y$ through the flowing fluid, then going **up** by $h_m$ through the manometric fluid: $$P_C = P_2 + \rho g y - \rho_m g h_m$$ **Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $P_C$:** $$P_1 + \rho g (y - h_m) = P_2 + \rho g y - \rho_m g h_m$$ $$P_1 - P_2 = \rho g h_m - \rho_m g h_m = (\rho - \rho_m) g h_m$$ **Step 4 — Convert to head of flowing fluid $h$:** $$h = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{(\rho - \rho_m) g h_m}{\rho g} = h_m \left( \frac{\rho}{\rho} - \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} \right)$$ $$h = h_m \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right) \quad \text{(final form, taking magnitude)} \blacksquare$$ ## 3.3 Complete Derivation: V-Notch Discharge Equation ### 3.3.1 Setup - Triangular notch with apex angle $\theta$ - Head of liquid above the apex of the notch = $H$ - Consider a thin horizontal strip of liquid at depth $h$ below the free surface, of thickness $dh$ - Width of strip at depth $h$ = $2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2)$ (by similar triangles) ### 3.3.2 Derivation **Step 1 — Theoretical velocity through the strip (Torricelli's theorem):** $$V = \sqrt{2 g h}$$ **Step 2 — Area of the strip:** $$dA = 2(H - h)\tan(\theta/2) \cdot dh$$ **Step 3 — Discharge through the strip:** $$dQ_{th} = V \cdot dA = \sqrt{2 g h} \cdot 2 (H - h) \tan(\theta/2) \, dh$$ **Step 4 — Integrate from $h = 0$ (free surface) to $h = H$ (apex level):** $$Q_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \int_0^H (H - h) \sqrt{h} \, dh$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the integral:** $$\int_0^H (H - h) \sqrt{h} \, dh = \int_0^H (H h^{1/2} - h^{3/2}) \, dh$$ $$= H \cdot \frac{2}{3} h^{3/2} \bigg|_0^H - \frac{2}{5} h^{5/2} \bigg|_0^H$$ $$= \frac{2}{3} H^{5/2} - \frac{2}{5} H^{5/2} = H^{5/2} \left( \frac{2}{3} - \frac{2}{5} \right)$$ $$= H^{5/2} \cdot \frac{10 - 6}{15} = \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2}$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back:** $$Q_{th} = 2 \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \cdot \frac{4}{15} H^{5/2} = \frac{8}{15} \tan(\theta/2) \sqrt{2g} \, H^{5/2}$$ **Step 7 — Apply coefficient of discharge $C_d$:** $$Q_{act} = \frac{8}{15} \cdot C_d \cdot \tan(\theta/2) \cdot \sqrt{2g} \cdot H^{5/2} \blacksquare$$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern) > **Problem:** A venturimeter with inlet diameter 20 cm and throat diameter 10 cm is fitted in a vertical pipe carrying oil of specific gravity 0.85, flow downwards. A differential U-tube manometer with mercury (S.G. = 13.6) shows a deflection of 25 cm. Take $C_d = 0.98$. Calculate: (a) Differential manometric head $h$ in meters of oil, (b) Discharge in litres/s. ### Solution **Step 1 — Areas:** $A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.20)^2 = 0.0314$ m² $A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 0.00785$ m² **Step 2 — Differential manometric head $h$ (in meters of oil):** $$h = h_m \left( \frac{\rho_m}{\rho} - 1 \right) = 0.25 \times \left( \frac{13.6}{0.85} - 1 \right)$$ $$h = 0.25 \times (16.0 - 1.0) = 0.25 \times 15.0 = 3.75 \text{ m of oil}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the geometric term:** $$\frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2}} = \frac{0.00785}{\sqrt{1 - (0.00785/0.0314)^2}}$$ $$= \frac{0.00785}{\sqrt{1 - (0.25)^2}} = \frac{0.00785}{\sqrt{1 - 0.0625}} = \frac{0.00785}{\sqrt{0.9375}}$$ $$= \frac{0.00785}{0.9682} = 0.00811 \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 4 — Discharge:** $$Q = 0.98 \times 0.00811 \times \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 3.75}$$ $$= 0.98 \times 0.00811 \times \sqrt{73.575}$$ $$= 0.98 \times 0.00811 \times 8.578$$ $$Q = 0.0682 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 68.2 \text{ L/s} \blacksquare$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Schematic of a Venturimeter with Differential Manometer ```mermaid flowchart TB inlet["Inlet Pipe
