# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Terminal Velocity for a Falling Sphere (Classical Model)

The terminal velocity is the steady-state velocity of a particle falling under gravity when drag balances the net weight. This is the **most common KTU exam problem** related to M&S in this module.

### Starting Point: Force Balance on a Sphere

The forces acting on a sphere settling in a fluid are:
- Gravitational force (downward): $F_g = m_p g = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_p g$
- Buoyant force (upward): $F_B = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} \rho_f g$
- Drag force (opposes motion): $F_D = \dfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho_f A_p v^{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho_f \dfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4} v^{2}$

### Step 1 — Net Driving Force (Weight minus Buoyancy)

At steady state, the net force in the direction of motion equals the drag:

$$F_{net} = F_g - F_B = \dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$

This must equal the drag force at terminal velocity $v_t$:

$$\dfrac{\pi}{6} d_p^{3} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \dfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho_f \dfrac{\pi d_p^{2}}{4} v_t^{2}$$

### Step 2 — Simplify by Cancelling Common Terms

Cancel $\pi$ and $d_p^{2}$ from both sides (assuming $d_p \neq 0$):

$$\dfrac{1}{6} d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = \dfrac{1}{8} C_D \rho_f v_t^{2}$$

Multiply both sides by 24 to clear denominators:

$$4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g = 3 C_D \rho_f v_t^{2}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Terminal Velocity

Rearranging for $v_t$:

$$v_t^{2} = \dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}$$

Taking the positive square root (since velocity is a magnitude):

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 C_D \rho_f}}$$

### Step 4 — Stokes Regime Special Case

In the creeping flow regime ($Re_p < 0.1$), the drag coefficient follows Stokes' law:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}$$

Substitute this into the general expression:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 \left( \dfrac{24}{Re_p} \right) \rho_f}}$$

But $Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f d_p v_t}{\mu_f}$ at terminal velocity. Substituting:

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g \, Re_p}{72 \rho_f}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p (\rho_p - \rho_f) g \, \rho_f d_p v_t}{72 \rho_f \mu_f}}$$

$$v_t = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g \, v_t}{72 \mu_f}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$v_t^{2} = \dfrac{4 d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g \, v_t}{72 \mu_f}$$

Divide both sides by $v_t$ (assuming $v_t \neq 0$):

$$v_t = \dfrac{4 d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{72 \mu_f} = \dfrac{d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{18 \mu_f}$$

This is the famous **Stokes' terminal velocity**:

$$v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f}$$

### Step 5 — Check Validity of Stokes' Law

Compute $Re_p$ at this terminal velocity:

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f d_p v_t}{\mu_f} = \dfrac{\rho_f d_p}{\mu_f} \cdot \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f} = \dfrac{\rho_f g d_p^{3} (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f^{2}}$$

If $Re_p < 0.1$, the Stokes assumption is valid; otherwise, use the **Schiller–Naumann** or **Clift–Gauvin** correlation.

## 3.2 Derivation of Pressure Drop in a Pipe (Modelling Bernoulli + Friction)

For a horizontal pipe of length $L$ and diameter $D$ carrying an incompressible fluid:

### Step 1 — Mechanical Energy Balance

$$-\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} = \dfrac{\Delta v^{2}}{2} + g \Delta z + f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^{2}}{2}$$

For a constant-area, horizontal pipe: $\Delta v = 0$ and $\Delta z = 0$. So:

$$-\dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho} = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^{2}}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for Pressure Drop

$$\Delta P = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$$

### Step 3 — Friction Factor for Laminar Flow

In the laminar regime ($Re < 2300$ for pipes):

$$f = \dfrac{64}{Re} = \dfrac{64 \mu}{\rho v D}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{64 \mu}{\rho v D} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot \dfrac{\rho v^{2}}{2} = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}$$

This is the **Hagen–Poiseuille equation**:

$$\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \mu L v}{D^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** The Hagen–Poiseuille equation is the simplest validation case for any CFD code simulating laminar pipe flow. Students can simulate flow in ANSYS Fluent or COMSOL and compare $\Delta P$ with this analytical result.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Simulation vs Validation

**Problem Statement:** A glass sphere of diameter $d_p = 2$ mm and density $\rho_p = 2500$ kg/m³ is dropped in water ($\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu_f = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) at $T = 20°C$. Use Stokes' law to predict the terminal velocity, then validate against a hypothetical experimental observation of $v_{t,exp} = 0.215$ m/s.

### Step 1 — Apply Stokes' Equation

$$v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f}$$

Substitute values ($g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} \times (2500 - 1000)}{18 \times 1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

Compute the numerator term by term:

$$(2 \times 10^{-3})^{2} = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{2}$$

$$(2500 - 1000) = 1500 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$$

$$\text{Numerator} = 9.81 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1500 = 0.05886 \text{ (Pa·m}^{2}\text{)}$$

The denominator:

$$18 \times 10^{-3} = 0.018 \text{ Pa·s}$$

Therefore:

$$v_t = \dfrac{0.05886}{0.018} = 3.27 \text{ m/s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **This value is far too high!** The issue is that $Re_p$ exceeds 0.1, so Stokes' law is invalid. This is a common KTU exam trap.

### Step 2 — Check Reynolds Number

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f d_p v_t}{\mu_f} = \dfrac{1000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 3.27}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = 6540$$

This is highly turbulent particle settling. Stokes' law is **not applicable**.

### Step 3 — Iterate Using Schiller–Naumann

Use $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}\left(1 + 0.15 Re_p^{0.687}\right)$ and iterate. Initial guess $Re_p = 100$:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{100}\left(1 + 0.15 \times 100^{0.687}\right) = 0.24 \times (1 + 0.15 \times 22.4) = 0.24 \times 4.36 = 1.046$$

Update $Re_p$ from:

$$Re_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d_p^{3} \rho_f (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 \mu_f^{2} C_D}}$$

$$Re_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times (2 \times 10^{-3})^{3} \times 1000 \times 1500 \times 9.81}{3 \times (10^{-3})^{2} \times 1.046}}$$

Numerator: $4 \times 8 \times 10^{-9} \times 1000 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 4.71 \times 10^{-1}$

Denominator: $3 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.046 = 3.138 \times 10^{-6}$

$$Re_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.471}{3.138 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{150063} \approx 387$$

Iterate again with $Re_p = 387$:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{387}\left(1 + 0.15 \times 387^{0.687}\right) = 0.062 \times (1 + 0.15 \times 49.0) = 0.062 \times 8.35 = 0.518$$

$$Re_p = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.471}{3 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.518}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.471}{1.554 \times 10^{-6}}} = \sqrt{303000} \approx 550$$

Converged $Re_p \approx 550$, $C_D \approx 0.5$. Final $v_t$:

$$v_t = \dfrac{Re_p \cdot \mu_f}{\rho_f d_p} = \dfrac{550 \times 10^{-3}}{1000 \times 2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.275 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 4 — Validate Against Experiment

$$\% \text{ Error} = \dfrac{\vert 0.275 - 0.215 \vert}{0.215} \times 100 = 27.9\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> The error is high, indicating that the experimental measurement may include wall effects, particle roughness, or non-sphericity. In a KTU project, you would refine the model (e.g., include wall correction factor, particle shape factor) and re-run the simulation.

### Step 5 — Apply Wall Correction Factor (Faxén correction)

For a sphere settling near a wall, multiply $v_t$ by $(1 - 2.104 \, d/D_w + 2.09 (d/D_w)^{3} - 0.95 (d/D_w)^{5})$ where $D_w$ is the wall distance. For a glass tube of $D_w = 50$ mm:

$$\text{Correction} = 1 - 2.104 \times \dfrac{2}{50} + ... \approx 0.916$$

$$v_{t,corrected} = 0.275 \times 0.916 = 0.252 \text{ m/s}$$

Error reduces to $\approx 17\%$. Iterating further with this corrected $v_t$ refines the prediction.

## 3.4 Particle Equation of Motion in CFD (Discrete Phase Model)

For Lagrangian particle tracking, the equation solved by ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, etc., is:

$$m_p \dfrac{d\vec{v}_p}{dt} = \underbrace{\dfrac{1}{2} C_D \rho_f A_p \vert \vec{u} - \vec{v}_p \vert (\vec{u} - \vec{v}_p)}_{\text{Drag}} + \underbrace{m_p \vec{g}}_{\text{Gravity}} - \underbrace{m_p \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_p} \vec{g}}_{\text{Buoyancy}} + \vec{F}_{other}$$

where $\vec{F}_{other}$ may include **Saffman lift**, **Magnus lift**, **virtual mass**, and **Basset history force**.

## 3.5 Grid Independence Study Procedure

A grid (mesh) independence study is essential for validation. The standard procedure:

1. Run simulation on Mesh 1 (coarse), Mesh 2 (medium), Mesh 3 (fine)
2. Extract a representative variable $\phi$ (e.g., $\Delta P$, $v_{max}$)
3. Compute refinement ratio $r$ (typically $r = 2$ for halving cell size)
4. Calculate observed order of accuracy $p$:

$$p = \dfrac{\ln \left( \dfrac{\phi_3 - \phi_2}{\phi_2 - \phi_1} \right)}{\ln(r)}$$

5. Compute Grid Convergence Index (GCI):

$$GCI = \dfrac{1.25 \vert \phi_3 - \phi_1 \vert}{\vert \phi_1 \vert (r^{p} - 1)} \times 100\%$$

6. Acceptable mesh when GCI $< 5\%$

## 4.1 Overall M&S Workflow Flowchart

The following Mermaid block renders the **end-to-end workflow** of modelling and simulating a simple fluid–particle system, including the validation loop.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Simple System e.g. settling sphere] --> B[Define Geometry and Domain]
    B --> C[List Fluid and Particle Properties]
    C --> D[Derive Governing Equations]

    F --> G[Set Boundary Conditions and Solver Settings]
    G --> H[Run Simulation]
    H --> I{Convergence Achieved?}
    I -- No --> G
    I -- Yes --> J[Post Process Results]
    J --> K[Conduct Grid Independence Study]
    K --> L[Perform Lab Experiment for Validation]
    L --> M[Compare Simulation vs Experiment]
    M --> N{Error within 5 to 15 percent?}
    N -- No --> O[Refine Model Adjust BC, Turbulence Model]
    O --> E
    N -- Yes --> P[Final Validated Model]
    P --> Q[Document and Report Findings]
```

## 4.2 Force Balance on a Particle in Fluid

This schematic shows the vector forces acting on a particle, which is the heart of particle-tracking simulation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> D[Buoyancy FB upward]
    B --> E[Drag FD opposite to relative motion]
    B --> F[Lift Forces Saffman, Magnus]
    B --> G[Virtual Mass Fvm in unsteady flow]

    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Integrate to get velocity and position]
    I --> J[Trajectory Output]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define mathematical modelling. List any two software tools used for the simulation of fluid and particle systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Mathematical modelling is the process of representing a physical system using mathematical equations derived from fundamental conservation laws (mass, momentum, energy).

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** A mathematical model is a set of governing equations — typically partial differential equations (PDEs) such as the Navier–Stokes and continuity equations — that describe the physical behaviour of a fluid or particle system within defined boundary conditions.

**Two software tools (1 mark):**
1. **ANSYS Fluent** — commercial CFD software based on the Finite Volume Method, widely used for multiphase flow and particle tracking.
2. **OpenFOAM** — open-source CFD toolbox that allows full customization of solvers for fluid and particle systems.

*(Acceptable alternatives: COMSOL Multiphysics, MATLAB, STAR-CCM+, ANSYS Rocky DEM, LIGGGHTS.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "validation" in the context of simulation? How is it different from "verification"?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Validation (1.5 marks):** Validation is the process of comparing simulation results with **experimental measurements** to ensure that the model accurately represents the real physical system. It answers the question: *"Are we solving the right equations?"*

**Verification (1.5 marks):** Verification is the process of confirming that the **numerical solution** of the equations is correct, e.g., through grid independence studies, error analysis, and code benchmarking. It answers: *"Are we solving the equations right?"*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** "V**a**lidation = re**A**l world" (compare with experiment); "Ver**i**fication = **i**nternal" (code/math check).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU ESE pattern requires students to answer **one full question** from a choice of two. Both questions below are designed as per the **2024 Scheme 14-mark structure** with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** A spherical glass particle of diameter $d_p = 1$ mm and density $\rho_p = 2400$ kg/m³ settles in a column of water ($\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu_f = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s) under gravity. **(7 marks)**

- **(i)** Check if Stokes' law is applicable. **(3 marks)**
- **(ii)** If not applicable, calculate the terminal velocity using the Schiller–Naumann correlation. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** Briefly describe the steps involved in modelling and simulating a "simple system" using ANSYS Fluent. Discuss the importance of grid independence study. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a)(i) — Validity of Stokes' law (3 marks):**

First assume Stokes' law holds and compute $v_t$:

$$v_t = \dfrac{g d_p^{2} (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu_f} = \dfrac{9.81 \times (10^{-3})^{2} \times (2400 - 1000)}{18 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$v_t = \dfrac{9.81 \times 10^{-6} \times 1400}{0.018} = \dfrac{0.01373}{0.018} = 0.763 \text{ m/s}$$

Check Reynolds number:

$$Re_p = \dfrac{\rho_f d_p v_t}{\mu_f} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.763}{10^{-3}} = 763$$

Since $Re_p = 763 \gg 0.1$, **Stokes' law is NOT applicable**. We must use the Schiller–Naumann correlation. **(3 marks)**

**Part (a)(ii) — Schiller–Naumann calculation (4 marks):**

Iterate using $C_D = \dfrac{24}{Re_p}\left(1 + 0.15 Re_p^{0.687}\right)$.

**Iteration 1:** Assume $Re_p = 100$. Then:

$$C_D = \dfrac{24}{100}\left(1 + 0.15 \times 100^{0.687}\right) = 0.24 \times (1 + 0.15 \times 22.4) = 0.24 \times 4.36 = 1.046$$
